name: Monitoring and redosing
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial gentamicin and vancomycin help gentamicin in paediatrics gentamicin in neonates and children monitoring and redosing monitoring and redosing 

    Monitoring and redosing 
  


    How to decide if monitoring needed 
  

Toxicity is uncommon in courses less than 72 hours, and levels are not required for short courses up to 5 days in duration if the following criteria also apply:

In children or neonates who are making a good clinical recovery,
In children or neonates who have good and predictable renal function, and no significant change in renal function tests, or urine output
In children and neonates who are not on other agents associated with nephrotoxicity or ototoxicity (especially cisplatin, NSAIDs, ciclosporin, glycopeptide antibiotics)

U+Es should be monitored regularly (at least twice a week) in order to determine whether gentamicin levels are necessary.
In all other patients monitor levels as in How to take levels for gentamicin, below. (Step 6.6 in full guideline).
 


    How to take levels for gentamicin 
  


Take a level with routine bloods, including CRP, 24 hours after the initial dose
For longer courses, levels should be checked before every 3rd dose, or earlier if there is a significant change in renal function.
Place sample in a yellow topped (adult sized) tube or brown topped (paediatric sized) tube (clotted blood). 　
Document on the biochemistry request form the: 
    
EXACT time and date the last dose was given (see prescription chart), and the;
EXACT time and date the sample was taken. 
 
The specimen bottle and form must be labelled in accordance with the Trust Specimen Acceptance Policy.

Do not take the blood sample from the IV line used for Gentamicin administration.
Assays will usually be processed between 10am and 10pm every day. At weekends, and if specimens are taken after 10pm (and result needed before 10am) then ensure the laboratory is aware the specimen is to be expected, and arrange for transport. Assay results will generally be available within one hour of processing.


    Redosing Neonates 0-7 days 
  

Neonates should receive gentamicin 36 hourly, subject to therapeutic drug monitoring (age range 0-7 days).
Take clotted blood sample 24 hours after the initial dose, then review whether gentamicin is to continue at 36 hours post-dose

If level is over 2mg/L at 24 hours, repeat level at 34 hours after the initial dose
If level is 2mg/L or under at 24 hours after the initial dose, write up subsequent doses in antimicrobial section of drug chart at 5mg/kg every 36 hours. Cross out unused time slots to prevent administration errors.

At 34 hours after the initial dose: If out of routine laboratory hours inform the biochemist on call that an urgent level is required

If level is still over 2mg/L at 34 hours 

Consider audiology follow up
Contact Consultant Microbiologist for further advice if gentamicin prescription is to continue after 36 hours
 

Review the need to continue gentamicin prescription every 36 hours prior to administering each dose.
Check trough level every third dose
NB. A Consultant Paediatrician or Consultant Microbiologist can make a decision to reduce dose intervals in specific clinical situations (e.g. severe sepsis and gram negative bacteraemia). The reason for reducing the dose interval must be stated in the notes, and on the drug chart. Seek advice on when to take a gentamicin level in this situation, although in general follow the principles of dosing and monitoring in older children.


    Redosing Neonates 8-28 days 
  

Neonates 8-28 days of age should receive gentamicin 24 hourly, subject to therapeutic drug monitoring.
Take clotted blood sample 22 hours after the initial dose, then review whether gentamicin is to continue at 24 hours post-dose

If level is 2mg/L or under at 22 hours after the initial dose, write up subsequent doses in antimicrobial section of drug chart at 5mg/kg every 24 hours. Cross out unused time slots to prevent administration errors.
If level is over 2mg/L at 22 hours, repeat level at 34 hours after the initial dose and if it is 2mg/L or under, write up subsequent doses in antimicrobial section of drug chart at 5mg/kg every 36 hours. Cross out unused time slots to prevent administration errors.

When sending initial gentamicin levels: If out of routine laboratory hours inform the biochemist on call that an urgent level is required. 

If level is still over 2mg/L at 34 hours 

Consider audiology follow up
Contact Consultant Microbiologist for further advice if gentamicin prescription is to continue after 2 days
 

Review the need to continue gentamicin prescription every 24 hours prior to administering each dose.
Check trough level every third dose
NB. A Consultant Paediatrician or Consultant Microbiologist can make a decision to reduce dose intervals in specific clinical situations (e.g. severe sepsis and gram negative bacteraemia). The reason for reducing the dose interval must be stated in the notes, and on the drug chart. Seek advice on when to take a gentamicin level in this situation, although in general follow the principles of dosing and monitoring in older children.


    Redosing Children 
  

Children should receive gentamicin 24 hourly, subject to therapeutic drug monitoring (age range 4 weeks - 16 years).
Take clotted blood sample 24 hours after the initial dose, then review whether gentamicin is to continue

If gentamicin is to continue, give 2nd dose without assay result. 

Review whether gentamicin is to continue prior to third dose

If gentamicin is to continue, check pre-2nd dose levels 
    
If level is 1mg/L or under at 24 hours after the initial dose, write up subsequent doses in antimicrobial section of drug chart at 5mg/kg every 24 hours. Cross out unused time slots to prevent administration errors.
If level is over 1mg/L at 24 hours, hold 3rd dose and repeat level. Contact Consultant Microbiologist for advice.
 

If urgent repeat gentamicin levels are being requested out of routine laboratory hours, inform the biochemist on call that an urgent level is required.
Review the need to continue gentamicin prescription every 24 hours prior to administering each dose.
Check trough level twice weekly, unless renal function deteriorates.
NB. If child is well after 24 hours and there is no clear indication to carry on antibiotics (eg. positive culture results, rising inflammatory markers) then the risk of treatment may outweigh potential benefits and  it may be appropriate to withhold further doses.

start antibiotic treatment as soon as possible after establishing a diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia, and certainly within 4 hours (within 1 hour if the person has suspected sepsis). doses are for guidance only and apply to adults of average size with normal renal and hepatic function. co-trimoxazole: if crcl ≤30mls/min reduce dose by half. discuss with microbiology if pregnant or breastfeeding. levofloxacin (and other quinolones): levofloxacin is an alternative for all indications only in patients under 65, if there is renal impairment or non-resolution, and requires approval from micro as is associated with an increased risk of clostridium difficile diarrhoea, tendonitis, muscle pain, peripheral neuropathy, aortic aneurysm rupture / dissection, and prolonged qt interval. tendonitis risk increases with age and steroid use (may occur within 48 hours of starting). stop treatment at the first signs of serious adverse effects and discuss alternative treatment with microbiology. see mhra alert for further information. the absorption of doxycycline may be impaired by concurrent administration of antacids containing aluminium, calcium, magnesium (or other drugs containing these cations), oral zinc, iron salts, or bismuth preparations – dosages should be maximally separated (ideally withhold oral iron supplements while on doxycycline). doxycycline should be taken whilst upright with ≥100mls water & well before bedtime. counsel patients on risks of photosensitivity. doxycycline is contra indicated in pregnancy & breastfeeding – contact microbiology for name: General Interest and Other Points
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology dacrocystitis general interest and other points general interest and other points 
In cases where infection has been ruled out, or adequately treated and symptoms persist, a hot compress and massage over the tear sac may help to relieve the patient’s symptoms.

For adults with lacrimal sac swelling and suspicion of obstruction of the lacrimal drainage system, conservative treatment is preferred. Surgery should be reserved for cases refractory to these techniques

searchField:

'acute kidney injury on adults' guidance (review april 2024) is available on hub this guidance is now out of date please check any prescribing with the bnf identifying & flagging aki identifying aki identifying aki requires monitoring of serum creatinine and urine output.
serum creatinine the computer system in the biochemistry lab will generate an alert for the changes in serum creatinine.
the lab report will include the message 'aki nil' or 'aki 1, 2 or 3'.
if the patient is new or does not have a creatinine level from the past year the computer will not generate an aki stage and the following message will appear -
'unable to calculate aki - no creatinine baseline'.
aki stage 2 and 3 will also be phoned through to the wards by the biochemistry team.
urine output you need to check your patient’s urine output. this information can be obtained from the patient themselves and/or nursing staff and should be recorded on their observation charts.
input/output charts or fluid balance charts need to be started on epic for patients at risk of aki. flagging aki images for epic flag awaited document review date april 2024 committee approving cec microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked

name: Dry, granulating wounds
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care choosing a wound dressing dry, granulating wounds dry, granulating wounds dry, granulating wounds 
Granulating wounds with low volumes of exudate need dressings that provide moisture, rehydrating the wound bed and promoting autolysis and healing.
ActivHeal Gel
ActivHeal Gel: for necrotic, sloughy or granulating wounds with nil/low exudate. 
Suitable for pressure ulcers, cavity wounds, leg ulcers, skin donor site, diabetic foot ulcers. 
This is not adhesive but water-based and provides moisture to a dry wound bed. As it is not adhesive, a secondary dressing is required.  
Hydrogel dressings need to be changed daily 

DuoDerm Extra Thin
DuoDerm Extra Thin: sterile, thin, hydrocolloid dressing which is able to contain any exudate by forming a cohesive gel. 
Suitable for chronic wounds, dry to light exuding wounds, leg ulcers, superficial pressure ulcers, surgical wounds, minor burns, abrasions/lacerations.
These dressings are adhesive and mouldable to the contour of the body. Hydrocolloid dressings need to be changed every 3-5 days.

name: Chaplaincy Team
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my health and wellbeing spiritual support chaplaincy team chaplaincy team spiritual support this section is reproduced in my hospital life chaplaincy team the chaplaincy team are based on level 1. the team have their own page on the hub - search chaplaincy and click on the northern services tab.
please be aware that all members of the team are part time so may not always be available.
contact the team on 01271 322362 (extension 2362 internally) or via pager 103. please leave a message.
the out of hours service can be reached via switchboard. as this is a voluntary service there may not always be someone available. introducing the team the chaplaincy team offers an invaluable resource providing pastoral care, as well as spiritual and religious support when needed to patients, their families, their carers and to hospital staff.
the team includes chaplains from different denominations who are trained and experienced in healthcare chaplaincy.
they are supported by a team of volunteers, who visit each ward weekly, along with a wider network of honorary chaplains and community support for all local faith groups.
all members of the chaplaincy team, including the volunteers, wear the nhs official badge and, like all hospital staff, are bound by strict rules of confidentiality.
they seek to engage with people of all faiths or none, respecting each person’s individuality.
you (or your patient) do not have to belong to a major faith or the same faith as the chaplaincy team to request their support.
in north devon it can be challenging to find a leader from non-christian religions because of the demographics of where we live and work.
the chaplaincy team can offer advice to all needing spiritual guidance. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 chaplaincy team spiritual support

name: O & G Department
searchField:
hospital guidelines obstetrics & gynaecology induction o & g department o & g department o & g department o & g department - nddh location, location, location! wards we are fairly spread out around the hospital. the ladywell unit is our base and contains delivery suite, bassett ward and petter ward, as well as both antenatal and gynaecology clinics. all offices are within the ladywell unit. gynaecology inpatients are usually on king george v (kgv) and lundy ward. bassett ward level 1, ladywell unit - ante/postnatal ward and dau (day assessment unit).
petter ward level 0, ladywell unit - day case elective gynaecology, early pregnancy assessment, hysteroscopy, and colposcopy. labour ward/delivery suite level 0, ladywell unit.
king george v (kgv) level 4, main hospital - emergency gynaecology admissions via aaa (ambulatory assessment area), and gynaecology inpatients.
lundy ward level 3, main hospital - elective gynaecology post-operative patients requiring an overnight stay.
surgical examination clinic (sec) room - next to the ward clerk’s desk in kgv, suitable for speculum and ultrasound examinations. outpatients antenatal and gynaecology clinics, level 0, ladywell unit
early pregnancy clinics (epac), hysteroscopy, and colposcopy clinics take place on petter ward, level 0, ladywell unit.
theatres
elective gynae lists take place in either day surgery (level 2) or main theatres (level 3). occasionally, emergencies (e.g. removal of ectopic pregnancy) are carried out on the cepod list (emergency theatre, also in main theatres).
theatre 8 is our main obstetric theatre, located on labour ward. obstetric operations including elective caesarians are performed here.
theatre 7 was our gynaecology theatre until changes brought about by covid-19.
day assessment unit (dau)
antenatal patients from 18 weeks up to term and postnatal patients up to three weeks presenting with any obstetric or medical issue are seen in the day assessment unit (dau).
inductions of labour (iol) also begin on dau. you will normally be asked to prescribe propess (prostaglandin) for induction and terbutaline 250 mcg sc in cases of uterine hyperstimulation. more information is available here.
ambulatory assessment area (aaa)
most gynaecology referrals from the emergency department (ed) or via the gp will come to aaa, located on kgv ward (level 4). when you accept the referral, ask if the patient can come to aaa. occasionally patients are too unstable to be transferred from ed, so you will need to review them there (level 2). the nurses on aaa are very helpful and will do any bloods/cannulas if you ask. don't forget, almost every patient you see will need a pregnancy test! other useful locations
handovers take place at 9.00am and 9.00pm in the labour ward office. changing rooms on labour ward and lockers available. you will need to wear theatre scrubs whilst on-call/in theatre, not your own clothes. there is a small sho office located just outside bassett ward. there is a code. the restaurant and doctors' mess are in the main hospital, level o.
the library and the mec (medical education centre) are on level 1. document review date na committee approving na microguide page created 2023 microguide page last checked 22/8/24 o & g department o & g department - nddh location, location, location!

name: Mx after inadvertant administration
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial approach to reported penicillin allergy mx after inadvertant administration mx after inadvertant administration mx after inadvertant administration mx after inadvertant administration management of a patient where there is a history of inadvertent administration of a penicillin since the application of the allergy label. history of reaction after administration of a specific beta-lactam antibiotic, with subsequent administration of the same antibiotic. the label of penicillin allergy should be removed. ensure adequate communication to other healthcare providers and the patient regarding the removal of the allergy label to avoid confusion in the future history of reaction after administration of a specific beta-lactam antibiotic, with subsequent administration of a different beta-lactam antibiotic; or unable to identify beta-lactam antibiotic implicated in initial reaction. (ie. possible reaction to moiety other than beta-lactam ring) if there is a history of serious / life-threatening reaction then : avoid all beta-lactams and discuss further management with consultant immunologist or microbiologist. if there is a history of accelerated type 1 reaction with the initial antibiotic, and no reaction to the subsequent antibiotic: it is safe to use the antibiotic that was inadvertently administered in all settings.
if other penicillins are intended : in the community, avoid all other penicillins and caution with cephalosporins.
in hospital, use provocation test for adult patients (children require referral to rd&e allergy clinic), and if tolerated, remove penicillin allergy label. ensure communication to other healthcare providers and the patient regarding the removal of the allergy label, to avoid confusion in the future. see appendix in bob document policy for cross-reactivity diagram to aid choice of alternative agents. if there is a history of non-urticarial rash with no blistering or mucous membrane involvement or vague history or unsubstantiated history of allergy from family remove label of penicillin allergy. ensure adequate communication to other healthcare providers and the patient regarding the removal of the allergy label to avoid confusion in the future mx after inadvertant administration mx after inadvertant administration management of a patient where there is a history of inadvertent administration of a penicillin since the application of the allergy label. history of reaction after administration of a specific beta-lactam antibiotic, with subsequent administration of the same antibiotic. the label of penicillin allergy should be removed. ensure adequate communication to other healthcare providers and the patient regarding the removal of the allergy label to avoid confusion in the future history of reaction after administration of a specific beta-lactam antibiotic, with subsequent administration of a different beta-lactam antibiotic; or unable to identify beta-lactam antibiotic implicated in initial reaction. if uncertain, normal ecg but strong cardiac history, give test dose 0.5 mg iv proton pump inhibitor only use following consultant advice. nice advice not to give prior to endoscopy. tranexamic acid
summary from the halt-it trial “we found that txa did not reduce death from gi bleeding and that, in some cases, txa increased the risk of thromboembolic events (clots in the veins of the legs that can move to the lungs). there were also more seizures with txa. these results are very important because they show that txa does not have the same effect in all types of bleeding, and that patients with stomach bleeding may be more prone to side effects from unwanted clotting. the results of halt-it mean that txa should not be used for the routine treatment of gi bleeding, and have the potential to change medical practice” antibiotics antimicrobial advice found on microguide antimicrobial guide, spontaneous bacterial peritonitis in gastrointestinal infections. here last checked 23/2/24 review date feb 2025 medications medications northern service guidance on upper gastrointestinal bleed (over age 16years) guideline (review date feb 2025) search hub check all doses and contraindications in bnf

'acute kidney injury on adults' guidance (review april 2024) is available on hub this guidance is now out of date please check any prescribing with the bnf which patients to refer all stage 3 aki,
any patient with an acute kidney injury who is not improving despite following care pathway,
renal transplant patients,
chronic kidney disease stages 4 or 5,
complications associated with acute kidney injury,
acute kidney injury with no clear cause, possible diagnoses that require specialist treatment (for example, vasculitis, glomerulonephritis, tubulointerstitial nephritis or myeloma). patients need a urine dipstick test. when and how to refer to nephrology discuss cases as early as possible but always within 24 hours of detection.

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment gastrointestinal spontaneous bacterial peritonitis diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
SBP should always be suspected in patients with possible infection and ascites
The following symptoms and signs may be present: 
  
Fever and / or rigors in most patients
Abdominal pain and discomfort in most patients
Worsening encephalopathy, diarrhoea, diuretic resistant ascites, worsening renal function, ileus occurring without any precipitating factor
Many infections may be poorly localized.

 
Differentials
 
If abdominal pain, raised white cells in blood and severe diarrhoea on a background of broad-spectrum antibiotic use, consider C.difficile gut infection
 
Always rule out abdominal compartment syndrome in patients with tense ascites who present with acute kidney injury and severe pain

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment obstetric asymptomatic bacteriuria (asb) diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Dipsticks have no role in the diagnosis of asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnant women.
All pregnant women should have an MSU sent at 1st antenatal booking for culture and sensitivity


Asymptomatic bacteriuria is routinely screened for and treated with antibiotics in pregnancy because it is a risk factor for pyelonephritis and premature delivery. 
    
Meta-analyses of studies evaluating asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy conclude that there are true associations with preterm delivery and low birthweight.
In addition, there are increased risks of preeclampsia, anaemia, chorioamnionitis and postpartum endometritis. Fetal risks include fetal growth restriction, stillbirth, perinatal mortality, mental retardation and developmental delay. It is postulated that direct bacterial endotoxin damage, in combination with cerebral hypoperfusion is responsible.
 
All women should be screened for asymptomatic bacteriuria at the 1st antenatal (booking) appointment. Urine sample (MSU) for culture and sensitivity. If initial sample grows mixed bacteria, the woman should be invited back to give a specimen using the Peezy™ MSU collection device
If Group B Streptococcus (GBS) is isolated, this information must be recorded in the woman’s handheld notes. GBS can persist after antibiotic treatment, and is associated with increased risk of neonatal GBS disease, which must be considered as part of the birth plan. See guidelines on management of GBS for further advice on risk assessments.
Thirty percent of women with asymptomatic bacteriuria will develop acute cystitis during their pregnancy.
DO NOT use dipstick testing to screen for asymptomatic bacteriuria at first or subsequent visits as it lacks the sensitivity.
A Cochrane review established that treatment reduces the risk of pyelonephritis in pregnancy and, consequently, the risks of preterm delivery and low birthweight. This review recommends that treatment schedules are directed by urine culture and sensitivity testing and that appropriate antibiotics are continued for at least 7 days.

name: Wildlife Parks and Zoos
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook settling in to exeter life attractions & places to visit wildlife parks and zoos wildlife parks and zoos 

   Wildlife Parks, Aquariums and Zoos
  
Shaldon Wildlife Trust, click HERE.
A small zoo set into the hillside of Shaldon. If you visit, other attractions nearby include the beautiful village of Shaldon, Ness Cove Beach, see HERE, which is accessed via the original smuggler's tunnel. You can also take a passenger ferry across to Teignmouth and visit the Victorian pier.The Donkey Sanctuary, click HERE.
For over 50 years, has worked to transform the lives of donkeys across the world. Discover more about who they are and how they make the world a better place for donkeys by visiting. The Donkey Sanctuary is a working farm, please dress appropriately and bring suitable footwear for the weather.Dartmoor Zoo, click HERE. If you’ve seen the film ‘We Bought a Zoo’ it was based on the story of Dartmoor Zoo.Paignton Zoo, click HERE.
With over 2,000 animals and countless amazing plants across 80 acres of beautiful, spacious grounds, you’re in for a fantastic day out at Paignton Zoo! It’s a place to roam, discover, explore and get in touch with nature. We’re a registered charity and support a number of projects in the UK and overseas that aim to help halt species decline.Dartmoor Otter Sanctuary & Buckfast Butterfly Farm, click HERE.
If you like wildlife this is a good option for close encounters with butterflies and the chance to spot playful otters. There are regular talks and the opportunity to pre-book a personal Otter Experience. The Sanctuary is at the same location as South Devon Railway and there is an option to buy a ticket for both attractions.
National Marine Aquarium (NMA), Plymouth, click HERE.
The NMA is the UK’s largest aquarium with lots of different tanks and exhibits to explore marine life and conservation. An entry ticket can become a year pass to return throughout the year for free.

name: General Interest and Other Points

hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology endophthalmitis (exogenous) general interest and other points general interest and other points 
Samples from vitrectomy have the highest yield for positive cultures. Up to 31% patients have negative or equivocal cultures in post-cataract endophthalmitis.

Follow-up appointments with Consultant Ophthalmologist will be needed for inpatients with endophthalmitis. Liaise with the Consultant or their secretary directly to arrange ward visits if it is not possible to transport the patient to eye clinic for examination.

name: Medications
searchField: medications investigations, medications and procedures medication,meds,medication administration,fluids,iv,ivs,eye drops,medication cupoboards,meds cupboards,tta,ttas,eye room,mar,medication administration record,prescribing,prescriptions,prescription,drug,drugs,controlled drugs, medications be proactive about administering medications yourself, especially for patients in the waiting room where there is a very limited nurse to patient ratio. there are medication cupboards in green majors, red majors, resus, minors and the eye room. the keys are with the nurses in each respective area, out-of-hours the minors keys are with the nurse in charge. single-user medications (inhalers, throat spray) are with tta (to take away) medications in the fluid cupboard (keys held by nurse in charge). eye medications are in locked cupboard in eye room (key with minors keys). pr meds are also in fluid cupboard (in drawers on the right). make sure you sign on epic mar (medication administration record) for any meds administered. nb when administering controlled drugs these will require signatures from two separate members of staff to ‘sign the medication out’. these are recorded in the controlled medication books found in green majors, resus and minors. this also includes documenting the supply left in the cupboard after you have signed out your dose. see "discharging patients" section for details for tta medications. guidance review committee/group guidance review date eolas page date created & editor number 25/7/25 editor number 009k eolas page date last checked & editor number 27/07/25 008

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology acanthamoeba diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials  Predisposing factors:
  
  
Contact lens wear is associated with >90% cases of Acanthamoeba keratitis -majority are soft lenses (particularly reusable or extended wear) 
    
Inadequate contact lens disinfection
Use of non-sterile solutions to clean lenses
Tap water rinse of contact lens storage case
Contamination of contact lens storage case with bacteria and fungi (± biofilm) which provide substrate for Acanthamoebae
 
Exposure to shower, pool or spa water whilst wearing contact lenses
Agricultural injuries
   
  Diagnosis is difficult. Signs may masquerade as Herpes simplex with temporary improvement on anti-herpetic treatment, further delaying diagnosis 
    
  Suspect any painful epitheliopathy that: 
    
  
Does not respond to normal treatment
Has known risk factor (e.g. contact lens wear or corneal trauma associated with soil or contaminated water)
   
   Symptoms
  
  
Pain (may be severe and out of proportion to degree of ocular inflammation)
Visual loss
Redness
Epiphora
Photophobia
Can be bilateral
May be a long history and condition may be misdiagnosed as herpetic, bacterial or fungal keratitis
 NB in earliest stages, pain may be minimal 
    
   Early signs:
  
  
Epithelial or subepithelial infiltrates
Pseudodendrites
radial keratoneuritis (infiltrates along corneal nerves)
Recurrent breakdown of the corneal epithelium
   
   Later signs:
  
  
Deep inflammation of the cornea consisting of a central or >Stromal thinning
Extension of inflammation into sclera
Anterior chamber cells and flare
Hypopyon
   
  Examination by confocal microscopy is the gold standard for clinical diagnosis. 
    
   Differential diagnosis:

Bacterial or fungal keratitis
   
  NB. Dendritic keratitis in a contact lens wearer should be assumed to be caused by Acanthamoeba until proved otherwise. Concurrent bacterial or fungal infection can occur.

name: How to refer a Patient to the SP & EoL Care Team
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life departments - clinical specialist palliative and end of life care (sp & eolc) team how to refer a patient to the sp & eol care team how to refer a patient to the sp & eol care team how to refer a patient to the specialist palliative and end of life care team
referrals to the specialist palliative and end of life care (sp & eolc) team require the consent of the patient. however, if a patient lacks capacity, then referral can be made in their best interests by the ward team in consultation with those close to patient.
however, when the clinical team has recognised that a patient is in the last hours or days of life, the 'last hours days of life' tab should be completed. this document is found under the ward navigator tab on the patient's record and captures the five 'priorities of care of the dying person'. once completed, the patient automatically appears on the list of the hospital specialist palliative & end of life care team. the team will aim to review them (or their records) to ensure they are receiving optimal end of life care, and will advise accordingly. inpatient referral epic tips 1. to refer to the sp & eol care team via epic, in 'order', type 'inpatient palliative' then complete the order form.
2. to prescribe a subcutaneous syringe pump infusion, in 'order', type 'syringe driver' and you will see the option for an order panel called 'medications for control of symptoms and end of life care'.
3. to create a new treatment escalation plan (tep) form, please complete a tep order first and then write a tep note (see tip sheet in epic learning dashboard). referral to the hospice community team patient consent will need to be obtained for referral to north devon hospice services. however, if a patient lacks capacity, then referral can be made in their best interests by the ward team in consultation with those close to patient.
a referral requesting that a patient is seen at home by a hospice clinical nurse specialist can be made via the north devon hospice referral form, accessed from the hospice website here. once completed, send it to the email address at the bottom of the form and import it to epic media so it is saved in the patient’s record. for more information on the north devon hospice click here. spc & eol specialist palliative & end of life tep treatment escalation plan microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 how to refer a patient to the sp & eol care team how to refer a patient to the specialist palliative and end of life care team

inpatient referral epic tips 1. to refer to the sp & eol care team via epic, in 'order', type 'inpatient palliative' then complete the order form.
2. to prescribe a subcutaneous syringe pump infusion, in 'order', type 'syringe driver' and you will see the option for an order panel called 'medications for control of symptoms and end of life care'.
3. to create a new treatment escalation plan (tep) form, please complete a tep order first and then write a tep note (see tip sheet in epic learning dashboard).

patient consent will need to be obtained for referral to north devon hospice services. however, if a patient lacks capacity, then referral can be made in their best interests by the ward team in consultation with those close to patient.

name: Introduction and Guide to Specimen Collection
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life departments - clinical pathology microbiology microbiology tests urinalysis introduction and guide to specimen collection introduction and guide to specimen collection microbiology - urinalysis sample collection
introduction contaminating bacteria from the external genitalia may give rise to misleading results.
therefore we can only accept the following specimens for routine culture: catheter or cystoscopy specimens
mid-stream urine specimens
supra-pubic aspirates please note that urinary catheter tips will not be processed as they do not provide helpful microbiological information.
for more information on the microbiology department and the tests they provide, see their page on the pathology laboratory website here.
the majority of urine results will be available the following day.
sample container
for routine culture and sensitivity testing, use the red top (boric acid) container.
the white top sterile universal bottle should only be used if this can be sent to the laboratory and processed immediately in order to avoid growth of any contaminating organism can take hold.
the minimum volume required is 1 ml.
urine collection for cytomegalovirus (cmv), mycobacterium tuberculosis (tb), schistosomiasis, and legionella and pneumococcal antigens is here.
mid-stream urine (msu) collection - female
if the patient is able to collect urine without assistance from the nursing staff they should be instructed as follows: separate the labia with cotton wool or a sponge moistened with water.
wipe the vuvla from the front to the back.
do not use disinfectant.
with the labia still separated, allow some urine to pass into the toilet.
then, without stopping, allow urine to pass into a sterile red top (boric acid) container and fill to the line.
pass the remaining urine into the toilet. mid-stream urine collection (msu) - male clean the glans penis with soap and water.
commence micturition, and when a few millilitres of urine have been passed, introduce a sterile red top (boric acid) container into the stream and fill the container to the line.
in elderly or very ill patients nursing assistance may be required.
send to the laboratory.
the specimen can be preserved in a refrigerator at 4°c prior to transport. specimens showing signs of contamination, e.g. with faecal material, are of no value and will not be cultured.
for information on chlamydia testing from male urine specimens click here.
urine collection - catheter specimen (csus) the specimen should not be collected from the drainage bag, only from the sampling port.
clean the sampling port with an alcoholic 2% chlorhexidine swab.
insert the syringe into the sampling port and aspirate urine. transfer 15 ml urine to a red topped sterile boric acid bottle. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 28/08/2024 introduction and guide to specimen collection microbiology - urinalysis sample collection

searchField:

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment genito-urinary urinary tract infection lower uti, no sepsis, no catheter diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Patients who show 3 or more classic signs and symptoms (including at least 1 of the highlighted symptoms) are likely to have a lower UTI.
Patients who show 2 or less classic signs and symptoms may have a UTI, but more attention should be paid to possible differentials and in particular avoiding unnecessary antibiotic use
Differentials include: 
  
Delirium: drug-induced, dehydration, disturbed sleep
Lower abdominal pathology: constipation, diverticular disease, appendicitis
Cystitis (inflammatory, non-infectious), caused by: 
    
Trauma due to genitourinary procedures incl. traumatic catheter insertion/removal, sexual intercourse, sexual abuse or physical activity (such as cycling).
Adverse drug effects — some drugs such as cyclophosphamide, opioids, and nifedipine can cause urinary tract symptoms.
Irritant contact dermatitis
 
Thrush
Gender-specific: 
    
Women: Fibroids, atrophic vaginitis, lichen sclerosis, lichen planus, PID, cervicitis, gynaecological malignancy (for example ovarian cancer) may present with persistent or frequent increased urinary urgency and/or frequency.
Men: benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), acute prostatitis, epididymitis, testicular torsion
 
Urolithiasis
Urological malignancy
Other infections such as sexually transmitted infections (for example Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea, genital herpes simplex), intestinal worms

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology penetrating globe injury diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Globe laceration – injury by sharp object or projectile, prefix “penetrating” to denote entry wound but no exit wound or “perforating” to denote both entry and exit wounds.

Laceration with intra-ocular foreign body (IOFB) carries additional risk factors.

Presence of IOFB should always be specified in a referral to Ophthalmology.

Site of laceration and zone of injury are also pieces of information which help prognostication, if able to be ascertained initially.

Leave examination to the Ophthalmologist if the structures of the eye appear unstable or are extruding through the injury.

Globe rupture is usually the result of blunt trauma. Risk factors for developing a rupture after trauma include:

Underlying infection (such as endophthalmitis)
Previous surgery causing weakness in the globe structures.

Rupture injuries are more common in elderly patients who have fallen over. Be aware of potential for abuse, where history is vague or the patient refuses to answer questions on clerking about how the injury was sustained.

Differentials include chemical eye injury, sub-conjunctival haemorrhage

please note - it is now out of date use with caution and check with the bnf why is this important? insulin is a high risk medication, with multiple possibilities to cause error.
between 2003 and 2009 the national reporting and learning system received 16600 patient safety incidents involving insulin, including 6 deaths.
the national diabetes inpatient audit in 2017 showed 40% of type 1 diabetes patients suffered some sort of insulin error as an inpatient.
insulin errors carry high risks for the patient as well as organisational risks in terms of bed days and delayed discharges. because of this, a great deal of work has been put into minimising risks of errors with insulin as a result. national patient safety alerts there have been several patient safety alerts relating to insulin and associated never events.

searchField:

   Occupational Health & Wellbeing
  
At the Royal Devon, staff health and wellbeing is hugely important. 
Remember - in order to be able to care for others, it is important that you are taking the time to care for yourself!
The Occupational Health Department can support you to improve your physical and emotional wellbeing and help you to lead a healthy lifestyle, and services include:

Treatment of Contamination/inoculation injuries 
Advice line 
Fitness for work advice/management referrals 
Vaccinations
Physiotherapy 
Pre-employment
Health surveillance
Vivup Employee Assistance Programme

To find out more about all of the services available search 'Occupational Health' on the HUB.

name: Food and Excipient Allergies
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services sick patients & resuscitation anaphylaxis management allergens and prescribing food and excipient allergies food and excipient allergies allergens and prescribing in addition to drug allergies, it is important to consider the possibility that patients may be allergic to constituent parts (excipients) of medications. medications may contain any of the 14 food allergens as described by the food standards agency here. food allergens there are 14 food allergens celery
cereals containing gluten (such as barley and oats), crustaceans (such as prawns, crabs and lobsters),
eggs, fish, lupin, milk, molluscs (such as mussels and oysters), mustard, peanuts (include arachis oil)
sesame, soybeans, sulphur dioxide and sulphites (at a concentration of more than ten parts per million), and
tree nuts (such as almonds, hazelnuts, walnuts, brazil nuts, cashews, pecans, pistachios and macadamia nuts). other excipients which may cause an allergic reaction e numbers including colourings, preservatives, flavourings. etc. drug examples haloperidol decanoate solution for injection may contain sesame oil,
naseptin nasal cream may contain arachis (peanut) oil,
colpermin may contain peanut (arachis) oil, baclofen may contain gluten. allergens in medicines resources 1. the british national formulary (bnf) - available as an app on your device, via the bnf icon in your zenworks window, or on hub search "bnf"
to find drugs that contain a particular allergen, choose 'drugs' from the tabs at the bottom of the page, then put the allergen into the search bar.
for more information on excipients, choose 'more' from the tabs at the bottom of the page, then 'guidance', then 'guidance on prescribing', and finally 'excipients'.
2. medicines and healthcare products regulatory agency (mhra) to find product information about medicines from the mhra on the gov.uk website click here.
3. the electronic medicines compendium (emc)
the emc contains up to date, easily accessible information about medicines licensed for use in the uk and can be accessed here. food and excipient allergies allergens and prescribing

eggs, fish, lupin, milk, molluscs (such as mussels and oysters), mustard, peanuts (include arachis oil)
sesame, soybeans, sulphur dioxide and sulphites (at a concentration of more than ten parts per million), and
tree nuts (such as almonds, hazelnuts, walnuts, brazil nuts, cashews, pecans, pistachios and macadamia nuts). other excipients which may cause an allergic reaction e numbers including colourings, preservatives, flavourings. etc.
drug examples haloperidol decanoate solution for injection may contain sesame oil,
naseptin nasal cream may contain arachis (peanut) oil,
colpermin may contain peanut (arachis) oil, baclofen may contain gluten. allergens in medicines resources
1. the british national formulary (bnf) - available as an app on your device, via the bnf icon in your zenworks window, or on hub search "bnf"
to find drugs that contain a particular allergen, choose 'drugs' from the tabs at the bottom of the page, then put the allergen into the search bar.
for more information on excipients, choose 'more' from the tabs at the bottom of the page, then 'guidance', then 'guidance on prescribing', and finally 'excipients'.
2. medicines and healthcare products regulatory agency (mhra) to find product information about medicines from the mhra on the gov.uk website click here.
3. the electronic medicines compendium (emc)
the emc contains up to date, easily accessible information about medicines licensed for use in the uk and can be accessed here. eolas page checked

name: Clostridium difficile
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life departments - clinical pathology microbiology microbiology tests faeces collection & testing clostridium difficile clostridium difficile microbiology - faeces testing - clostridium difficile
introduction
click here for information on faeces sample collection in this app.
the range of tests performed will be determined by the clinical history provided.
for more information on the microbiology department and the tests they provide, see their page on the pathology laboratory website here.
clostridium difficile (c. difficile)
c. difficile testing: samples should be refrigerated unless they are tested within 2 hours of collection.
c. difficile will be tested on fluid/mucoid/ bloodstained stools from inpatients and community patients > 2 years.
c. difficile will not be tested: on non- fluid stools
on patients < 2 years
if two samples have been sent in the previous 10 days
if positive within the last 28 days if the patient is still symptomatic, discuss with the consultant medical microbiologist.
samples tested early in a c. difficile infection may test as toxin negative. if symptoms continue, and c. difficile remains a clinical possibility, please repeat after 48 hours.
results timescale
testing for c. difficile toxin is performed daily on weekdays on specimens received before 1500,
on weekends and bank holidays – on specimens received before 1000. specimens received after these times will be tested the following day. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 28/08/2024 clostridium difficile microbiology - faeces testing - clostridium difficile

name: Cost of Living
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook settling in to exeter life cost of living cost of living cost of living 

   Cost of Living
  
Introduction
The cost of living in Exeter is relatively affordable compared to other cities in the United Kingdom, for example, London, Bath and Liverpool.
In the city, you may need an average of £1,700-£2,000 to cover your basic living expenses, including rent, transportation, food, and even leisure and entertainment.
Other expenses like child care, mortgage however, are not calculated among the estimated monthly budget.
For more information on Cost of Living Support, please visit the government website HERE and the Devon County Council website HERE.
Rent
Finding a home through a letting agency is the most common way to find a house, flat or room to rent. However, landlords also advertise property on websites, in local newspapers and on notice boards. Average rent per month could range from £600 (house-share) to £1,300 (two-bedroomed apartment). For more information on Housing, please visit the link HERE. 
See Accommodation for more information.
Shopping
Exeter has a great variety of shopping destinations, including higher-end outlets and vintage stores, as well as the usual high street shops. Primark is the most popular shopping outlet.
At the heart of the city is the award-winning Princesshay Centre with a great choice of shops, cafes and restaurants.
Saving Tip: Search for charity and second-hand shops. There are plenty of charity shops in Exeter where you can pick up some bargains for a few pounds and get yourself great items at even greater prices!
Food, Shopping and Groceries
There are several grocery stores and supermarkets that would fit into your budget in Exeter. You might need to keep your cupboards and fridge stocked with the essentials you’ll need after a long day at work. Weekly trips to the grocery stores to buy basic necessities like milk, eggs, bread, cheese, and coffee cost around £30 in Exeter. 
See the Food and Eating Out section of this guide HERE for information on speciality supermarkets.
Saving Tip: Shop in bulk, scout for deals and discounts, choose grocery stores that sell products at reasonable prices like Lidl, Aldi, Asda, Tesco & Sainsbury’s. To purchase gourmet products (much higher prices) visit shops like Marks & Spencer, Waitrose.
Eating Out
The experience of eating out in Exeter varies from one place to another. Prices depend on the place and type of food you order, with options ranging from fancy gourmet restaurants to cafes that serve extremely affordable meals. You’ll need an average of £15 - £20 if you’re planning to eat a decent meal outside for a minimum of three days a week.
See the Food and Eating Out section of this guide HERE for information on local eateries.
Saving Tip: Cooking and meal planning are the way to go! To save both time and money, you should definitely start cooking your meals at home, even better if you meal prep and cook for the entire week in advance. This saves the money you usually spend on eating out and the time you spend cooking every day. Try it!
Travel
The main mode of transportation around the city of Exeter is the bus. You can buy a single ticket (one way) for any bus journey. Prices vary depending on how far you travel, with fares starting from around £2.00 for short journeys up to £8 or £9 for longer journeys. Single tickets can be bought from the bus driver.
If you are going to return on the same day, it is cheaper to buy a day return ticket. However, please note that due to the current £2 single fare cap scheme, it may be cheaper to buy two single tickets. Find out more HERE.  
There is a whole section on travel in this app HERE.
Saving Tip: For travel to work download the Stagecoach app to receive 40% discount on your bus fare, use the park and ride service or car share. Find out more by searching 'Travel HUB' on the HUB.
Utilities
Utilities are the basic services a home, apartment, or business needs to keep it comfortable and functioning properly. Common utilities include water, sewer, electric, gas, trash, and recycling. Technology subscriptions like cable TV, internet, security, and phone service can also be considered utilities.
Water and sewage 
When you buy/rent a house, you are responsible for setting up water and sewage services with your city municipality. You can either pay their water bill based on a fixed rate or on the basis of the amount of water they actually use, measured with a water meter. To find the water supplier for your area please click HERE. 
Electric and gas
You can choose your energy supplier - this is the company that supplies your gas and electricity and charges you for the energy you use. The actual amount you pay will depend on how much energy your household uses, where you live and the type of meter you have. The name of your energy supplier will be on your bill. However, if you are not sure who your energy supplier is, your network operator can help you find out.
Your network operator can help with things like connecting your home to power, moving your meter, registering you for the Priority Services Register or assisting you if there's a power cut. You can find out who your network operator is from the Energy Networks Association website HERE.
Every three months, the Office of Gas and Electricity Markets (OfGEM) review and set a level on how much an energy supplier can charge for each unit of energy. Click HERE to find out more.
Rubbish and recycling
Your local district, city or borough council is responsible for collecting household rubbish and recycling. Click HERE to access the relevant website for more information. The website will also have information on the safe disposal of other items, including bulky items and garden waste, and the location of your local recycling centre.
TV, internet and phone services
For comforts like TV, internet, and phone service, contact your preferred service providers to connect your home.
These are not essential services so you can decide which provider and service level you prefer.
Purchasing a modem and router instead of renting, and choosing streaming services instead of cable helps homeowners save on technology utilities.
You will need a TV Licence to watch or record programmes on a TV, computer or other device live or on catch-up, and on BBC iPlayer. To purchase a TV Licence, click HERE.
Seasonal Energy and Utilities Savings Tips
Because the cost of several types of utilities is based on energy use, you have the power to lower your utility costs by making a few adjustments! Here’s how you can save on utilities every season:
Autumn

Open curtains to let the sun warm your home
Make use of good weather and dry your washing outside

Winter

Keep your thermostat at a low (but comfortable) temperature
Install insulating shades on drafty windows
Use LED lights for holiday decorations

Spring and Summer

Use fans instead of air conditioning when possible
Lower the temperature of your water heater
Air dry your clothing and hair instead of using electric appliances

Year-Round

Turn off lights when you leave a room
Power down electronics
Invest in new, energy-efficient appliances

Discounts
There are more than 15,000 blue light card discounts for members of the Blue Light community for a small membership fee. For more information click HERE. 
For more offers and discounts visit:

NHS Discounts, click HERE.
Health Service Discounts, click HERE.

For more information/advice on Money saving click HERE.
Find information, guidance and services in Devon, click HERE.

name: Treatment
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment respiratory community acquired pneumonia curb 0-1 treatment treatment nice guidance suggests that some patients at the end of their natural lifespan may be managed without the use of antimicrobials if they go on to develop a pneumonia.
use clinical judgement in conjunction with curb-65 score to decide on the best place of care: 0-1: consider home-based care
2 or more: hospital admission probably warranted initially
3 or more: request senior medic review, urgently if patient unwell. explain to patients with community-acquired pneumonia that after starting treatment their symptoms should steadily improve, although the rate of improvement will vary with the severity of the pneumonia, and most people can expect that by (nice cg191): 1 week: fever should have resolved
4 weeks: chest pain and sputum production should have substantially reduced
6 weeks: cough and breathlessness should have substantially reduced
3 months: most symptoms should have resolved but fatigue may still be present
6 months: most people will feel back to normal. mild pneumonia (curb = 0-1) first line amoxicillin 500mg tds po usually for 5 days second line / penicillin allergy clarithromycin* 500mg bd po usually for 5 days *interactions with many medicines - caution consider extending the course of antibiotics to 7 days as a possible management strategy for patients with low-severity cap whose symptoms do not improve as expected after 3 days. treatment nice guidance suggests that some patients at the end of their natural lifespan may be managed without the use of antimicrobials if they go on to develop a pneumonia

name: Blood Collection for Culture
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services pathology microbiology microbiology tests blood collection for culture blood collection for culture blood collection for culture microbiology - blood cultures
introduction
for more information on the microbiology department and the tests they provide, see their page in the pathology laboratory section on the hub
the poster at the bottom of this tab is a quick reference guide to the collection of blood for culture. results timescale samples are processed 24/7 on receipt in the laboratory.
most positive blood culture results occur within 24 hours. negative samples will be on labcentre within 48 hours. if blood cultures are positive - the consultant microbiologist will contact a member of the team looking after the patient. this is to let you know about the result and its implications.
they will want to know some information from you such as why the patient has come into hospital and any foreign objects, e.g. pic lines or joint implants.
they will advise you of the likely source and any further investigations or samples needed.
if out of hours, the consultant will contact one of the on-call doctors to discuss the next steps.

name: Initial Investigations for all patients
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment sepsis and septic shock neutropenic sepsis initial investigations for all patients initial investigations for all patients before starting antibiotics: take cultures (blood and urine). if central and peripheral lines in situ, take simultaneous blood cultures from both (10ml per bottle optimises yield). as soon as possible: mrsa screen – nose, throat and wound swabs (if previous mrsa positive add perineal swab, line swab and urinary catheter sample)
chest x-ray (within 4 hours if chest focus)
additional microbiology samples, depending on suspected focus chest: sputum for mc&s, viral throat swab for respiratory virus pcr, atypical pneumonia serology (clotted blood tube – include illness onset details). if curb≥3, send urine for pneumococcal and legionella antigens
gi: stool sample for mc&s, c.difficile
oropharyngeal: viral swab for perioral herpes, swab for fungal culture if previous treatment failure with antifungal for oropharyngeal thrush
line infection: exit site swab for mc&s initial investigations for all patients use the neutropenic sepsis inpatient order panel “rde haem onc suspected/confirmed febrile neutropenia (adult orderset)” contains standard blood tests, imaging and microbiology samples required for investigation of febrile, neutropenic patients commence monitoring at least hourly: full set of observations against news2 criteria
skin, and soft tissues at extremities (inspect for developing rashes, capillary refill time)
vascular device inspection (if present) before starting antibiotics: blood cultures - paired peripheral and central (picc/hickmann) lines at the same time
10ml per bottle optimises yield urine culture in red boric acid tube as soon as possible: chest x-ray (within 4 hours) should normally be taken, but may be omitted if no clinical suggestion of pneumonia or lung abscess. mrsa screen – nose, throat and wound swabs if previous mrsa positive add perineal swab, line swab and urinary catheter sample additional microbiology samples, depending on suspected focus chest: sputum for mc&s, extended panel viral throat swab for respiratory pathogens pcr and label that it is from a haem/onc patient to get appropriate tests run in lab, if curb≥3, send urine for pneumococcal and legionella antigens
gi: stool sample for mc&s, c.difficile
oropharyngeal: viral swab for perioral herpes, swab soft and hard palate for fungal culture and ask for speciation, particularly if previous treatment failure with antifungal for oropharyngeal thrush
line infection: exit site swab for mc&s
skin: swab vesicular rashes for varicella/herpes simplex/other viral skin infections, scrape suspected fungal infections and send for speciation, open or discharging wounds send swabs or pus for mc&s

name: General Information on Vascular Access at NDDH
searchField:
hospital guidelines anaesthetics and intensive care a to z vascular access referrals general information on vascular access at nddh general information on vascular access at nddh vascular access general information introduction
for advice on central line access in children click here.
there is information below on accessing the following types of vascular access in adults difficult peripheral cannulae
midlines
picc lines (peripherally inserted central catheter)
central / cvp lines
hickman lines / portacaths the vascular access device decision aid and nddh referral guide is here. a draft version of the venous access device insertion record & safety checklist is here.
please note referrals for picc and central lines must be discussed with the patient’s consultant. these invasive procedures are not without risks and may not be in the patient’s best interests.
referrals should always be made by a doctor who is familiar with the patient and the proposed treatment, including duration and indication.
it is unacceptable to ask nurses to refer a patient – this is not their responsibility.
referrals for non-urgent cases (e.g. tpn (total parenteral nutrition), long term antibiotics) should be made using the vascular access consult on epic. please be aware that there is not a 24/7 vascular access service so please follow the decision aid and referral guidance above. picc lines are generally inserted on tuesday mornings on seamoor unit and at various times on the wards by the gastro specialist nurse. some of the anaesthetic trainees and sas doctors and consultants might be available to help out but this is not guaranteed. difficult peripheral cannulae
patients with difficult peripheral venous access can be referred to the icu sho via bleep 504. we ask that a senior doctor has attempted cannulation prior to calling. if the anaesthetic team are busy we may have to delay helping unless the situation is clinically urgent. please understand that ultimate responsibility for cannulation is not with the anaesthetic team.
midlines
a midline is a long cannula inserted in the ante-cubital fossa that can be left in longterm. they are suitable for patients needing prolonged iv treatment (e.g. antibiotics). they are not suitable for tpn or chemotherapy.
a number of ward nurses have been trained to insert midlines so please ask around before referral. failing this some nursing staff on mau can insert these lines – call the ward and see if someone is free. if you cannot find a member of nursing staff or a doctor who is competent at insertion then referral can be made to the icu sho via bleep 504.
picc lines (peripherally inserted central catheter)
picc lines are a longer midline with the tip sitting in the superior vena cava. they are suitable for tpn and chemotherapy as peripheral injection of these treatments can cause problems.picc lines are inserted electively for chemotherapy on a tuesday morning in the treatment room on seamoor unit, please bleep 199 and make a vascular access consult on epic if you require a picc line for an inpatient.
central / cvp lines
central lines are usually inserted in the internal jugular vein in the neck or the subclavian vein. the tip of the line sits in the superior vena cava and thus can be used to transduce central venous pressure. referrals may be for patients with difficult fluid balance, for certain iv medications or in failed venous access by other routes. central line placement carries a number of risks and is unpleasant for the patient – referrals must be discussed with the patient’s consultant.
there are specific and detailed national standards for central line insertion which must be followed for all insertions (use of ultrasound, full aseptic technique etc.).
referrals should be made to the icu sho via bleep 504. the patient will need to be booked onto the emergency theatre list. a chest x-ray will be required to confirm tip placement prior to use.
hickman lines / portacaths
there is currently no provision for these lines at nddh referral should be via radiology in exeter. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 28/08/2024 general information on vascular access at nddh vascular access general information

name: Pension
searchField:
pension all about my pay pension nhs pension scheme
the nhs pension scheme is fully guaranteed by the government, with the nhs putting in 20.6% of a member’s salary on top of their own contributions.
new starters to the nhs are automatically enrolled in the nhs pension scheme. contributions are deducted from your pay and the trust also contributes to the scheme on your behalf. however, it is voluntary, and employees may choose to opt-out. the scheme plays a valuable role in the recruitment and retention of nhs staff as it is a key benefit. our payroll team assist any colleague that is considering drawing pension benefits and will liaise with the pensions agency in order to seek pension forecast information for you. we also support colleagues by providing information about pensions to help you consider and choose the best pension offering to suit you. to know more about pensions please contact the eastern services pension team by phone on 01392 403643 or email: rde-tr.pensionsdepartment@nhs.net. scheme guides are issued with offer of appointment letters and more detailed guide booklets are available from the nhs business services authority website, see here.
opting-out of the pension scheme
there are a number of ways you can leave or take a break from the scheme.
please visit the nhs pensions members hub here for help in understanding what you would be giving up if you decide to opt-out.
if you do decide to opt out, you will need to complete and sign the sd502 form available here and send it to the payroll department. microguide page created 10/07/2024 added to handbook north 23/02/2025

only treat if symptomatic. notes on uti uncomplicated utis in men do not require investigation in young men with symptoms of cystitis without predisposing conditions for uti, consider chlamydia – send urine for chlamydia testing catheterised patients with asymptomatic uti do not require antibiotics. this includes those with cloudy urine, debris in urine, encrusted catheters pseudomonas rarely requires treatment. if treatment is considered, discuss with microbiology or urology before proceeding for postmenopausal women, there is very limited evidence to support the use of vaginal oestrogens to reduce recurrent utis. where vaginal atrophy is present the use of these products in accordance with licensed dose instructions may bring about a reduction in uti as the atrophy improves urgent referral is indicated for patients with symptoms of possible urological cancer: patients of any age with painless macroscopic haematuria: if haematuria is associated with symptoms of uti, culture the urine before referring. if uti is not confirmed by urine culture, or if haematuria does not resolve with treatment of the uti, refer urgently. women aged 40 years or older who present with recurrent or persistent uti associated with haematuria. patients aged 50 years or older who are found to have unexplained microscopic haematuria. persistent frequency and dysuria either not associated with a proven urinary tract infection or symptoms that fail to resolve following treatment of a urinary tract infection. uncomplicated uti uncomplicated uti

name: HAP Prescribing Guidance
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines respiratory hospital acquired pneumonia (hap) hap prescribing guidance hap prescribing guidance hospital aquired pneumonia (hap) prescribing guidance hospital acquired pneumonia is associated with a significant mortality, although mortality may be also a function of a patients co-morbidities. never the less evidence suggests that prompt treatment with appropriate antibiotics leads to the best outcomes. however it is important that hap is carefully diagnosed to avoid inappropriate over use of antibiotics. early review by senior staff and modifying treatment according to microbiological results leads to better management. definition of hospital aquired pneumonia (hap)
hospital acquired pneumonia (hap) is defined as pneumonia that occurs 48 hours or more after admission to hospital, including transfers, which was not incubating at the time of admission but also see under mrsa below. do not confuse hap with hospital acquired lower respiratory tract infection, which is non-pneumonic (no infiltrates on chest x-ray), which is not included in this guidance. this guidance does not include ventilator associated pneumonia (vap). clinical features of hospital aquired pneumonia (hap) chest x-ray shows new or progressive infiltration plus at least two of the following: fever > 38o c leukocytosis or leukopenia (> 11 x109 or <4x109 wbc/ml respectively)
purulent secretions decline in oxygenation clinical assessment of severity for hospital aquired pneumonia (hap)
there is no validated assessment tool available to assess, however the presence of any of the following features may indicate severity. early review by senior medical staff is required to achieve accurate assessment. new onset of confusion x-ray showing shadowing bilateral or multilobular (preferably taken posteroanterior and lateral if not intubated) respiratory rate > 30/min low blood pressure (systolic < 90mmhg and or diastolic < 60 mmhg) pa o2 <8kpa (normal 10.7-13.3kpa) and or sats o2 < 93% on air these and laboratory studies (fbc, serum electrolytes, renal and liver function) can point to the presence of multiple organ dysfunction and thus help define the severity of illness. likely pathogens causing hospital aquired pneumonia (hap) streptococcus pneumoniae haemophilus influenzae endogenous flora, should always be considered especially after short hospital stays without additional risk factors. pseudomonas aeruginosa* multi-resistant gram negative organisms in immunocompromised patients; patients who have recently been on itu/hdu; patients with chronic pulmonary conditions (e.g. bronchiectasis, cf patients). mrsa* if suspected see antibiotic choice under mrsa patients known to be colonised with mrsa (e.g. pas alert); previously in any hospital in last 3 months, from nursing/residential home, chronic conditions e.g. haemodialysis, chronic wound care e.g. ulcers. fungi and yeasts* these should be considered in high-risk patients, e.g. malignancies esp. haematological, renal and transplant patients. discuss with microbiologist. * note sputum culture results may show evidence of colonisation with these organisms. do not treat unless there is good evidence for pneumonia. avoid inappropriate prescribing which may lead to acquisition of resistant organisms and c. difficile. microbiology investigation of hospital aquired pneumonia (hap) blood cultures, sputum or tracheal aspirate/broncho-alveolar lavage. note: a sterile culture of respiratory secretions in the absence of a new antibiotic in the past 72 hours virtually rules out the presence of bacterial pneumonia, but viral, legionella and mycobacterial infection is still possible. if these patients have on-going clinical signs of infection, another site of infection should also be considered. special considerations in hospital aquired pneumonia (hap)
if previous multidrug resistant pathogen isolated note susceptibility and treat as appropriate. antimicrobial considerations in hospital aquired pneumonia (hap) start antibiotic treatment as soon as possible after establishing a diagnosis of hospital-acquired pneumonia, and certainly within 4 hours (within 1 hour if the person has suspected sepsis ) doses are for guidance only and apply to adults of average size with normal renal and hepatic function. chloramphenicol : ensure appropriate monitoring including baseline lfts, u&es, cr, & fbc. during therapy monitor fbc a minimum of twice weekly. reduce dose in hepatic impairment (avoid if severe). any patient on interacting drugs or with liver impairment will need levels. stop treatment if lfts become deranged or if wbcs fall to <2.5x109/l – contact microbiology for advice. do not prescribe oral chloramphenicol unless specifically recommended by microbiology. co-trimoxazole : if crcl ≤30mls/min reduce dose by half. discuss with microbiology if pregnant or breastfeeding. levofloxacin : levofloxacin is an alternative for all indications only in patients under 65, if there is renal impairment or non-resolution, and requires approval from micro as is associated with an increased risk of clostridium difficile diarrhoea, tendonitis, muscle pain, peripheral neuropathy, aortic aneurysm rupture / dissection, and prolonged qt interval. tendonitis risk increases with age and steroid use (may occur within 48 hours of starting). stop treatment at the first signs of serious adverse effects and discuss alternative treatment with microbiology. see mhra alert for further information. the absorption of doxycycline may be impaired by concurrent administration of antacids containing aluminium, calcium, magnesium (or other drugs containing these cations), oral zinc, iron salts, or bismuth preparations – dosages should be maximally separated (ideally withhold oral iron supplements while on doxycycline). doxycycline should be taken whilst upright with ≥100mls water & well before bedtime. counsel patients on risks of photosensitivity. doxycycline is contraindicated in pregnancy & breastfeeding – contact microbiology for alternative options. dose vancomycin and gentamicin in accordance with the trust guidelines on the antimicrobial website. hap prescribing guidance hospital aquired pneumonia (hap) name: Diagnosis and Things to Watch Out For
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment bone and joint septic arthritis (hot joint) native joint diagnosis and things to watch out for diagnosis and things to watch out for 
Patients with a short history of a hot, red, tender, swollen joint with a restricted range of movement should be regarded as having septic arthritis till proven otherwise. If clinical suspicion is high it is important to treat as septic arthritis even in the absence of a fever or a raised white cell count or CRP.
  Presentation
 
A wide range of presentations may be seen, including rationalisation of joint pains as secondary to an injury.
 

More common in children and elderly
Often joint disease/problem or soft tissue injury prior – cut/graze/bite, arthritis (esp. rheumatoid), procedure on joint (injection, surgical manipulation)
Painful, markedly swollen joint – either very acute onset or 1-2 weeks of gradually worsening symptoms
General malaise, ~ 50% report fevers and rigors
Marked decrease in range of movement in affected joint(s),
More common in large joints, 20% cases may present with polyarticular involvement.

 
Things to watch out for:
 

Recent acute history or recurrent episodes of other “deep” infections e.g. endocarditis, endophthalmitis, liver abscess 
    
Contact microbiology to discuss treatment and further specialist investigations in these cases, after sending the usual samples
 
Note any recent bone / joint procedures
Recent skin-breaking injury prior to joint problem occurring – particularly note: 
    
acquired in marine/soil environment
human/animal bite
area/country where acquired, if travel history
 
Immunosuppression 
    
Patients’ immunosuppressing drugs should be managed in conjunction with their parent specialty – especially if for solid organ transplant
 
Previous or recent tick bite (Lyme)
No obvious factors and divulge history of risky sexual behaviour – consider Gonococcal arthritis, esp. if skin lesions and presenting with tenosynovitis

 
Samples

Blood cultures prior to first dose of antibiotics
Superficial wound swabs are not predictive of infecting organisms 
    
but if not possible to aspirate joint prior to first dose of antibiotics (e.g. due to haemodynamically unstable patient) and deep wound discharging pus, then a sample of this could be sent for MC&S
 
Deep samples (joint aspirate, tissue from surgical debridement) are the only way to diagnose less common infecting organisms, but must be collected before the first dose of antibiotics is given

Imaging

Plain X-ray should be performed to exclude other pathology, sometimes shows chondrocalcinosis suggestive of calcium pyrophosphate (pseudogout), but not helpful if looking solely for septic arthritis
MRI with contrast may identify developing osteomyelitis, likely to require surgical management

  Differentials

Gout / pseudogout
Non-infective arthritis – rheumatoid, reactive
Traumatic injury / intra-articular haemorrhage (suspect if bloody aspirate)
Viral or Lyme arthritis
