name: Training & Staff Support
searchField:
hospital guidelines adult clinical guidelines general guidance restraint & restrictive practice training & staff support training & staff support training & staff support restraint and restrictive practice/ use of force policy (review date march 2027) training staff education and training this policy promotes on-going education, training and awareness of the issues surrounding management of agitation and the use of restraint intervention. the emphasis of training and education should be on dealing effectively with situations in order to minimise the need for use of force interventions. additional training needs
there may be requirements in clinical settings to provide bespoke training based on levels of risks, patient's cohort and incident trends. managers are to contact workforce to highlight this need. nb – all techniques in the above should be approved via accredited organisations in line with the restraint reduction standards as applicable to acute nhs organisations. there is a professional responsibility to ensure training is up to date as well as an organisational responsibility. all staff should receive a minimum of level 1 conflict de-escalation/resolution, at least every 3 years. all frontline staff working in high risk areas where they may be required to apply the use of force to maintain safety should attend a combined breakaway and safe handling training session annually. please note that a health declaration is required to be completed by staff before breakaway and safe handling training, which may lead to a requirement for a risk assessment to be undertaken by the staff member and their line manager or staff with health restrictions. any such risk assessment must be presented to the trainer at the start of the session, prior to the commencement of training. conflict resolution
a mandated subject for all staff (ukcsf subject 4). this training must be undertaken once every three years and is accessed through the national elearning package. the learning aims and outcomes of this training address the way one communicates, patterns of behaviour, recognition of escalation warning signs, impact factors and preventative strategies. this training should be undertaken alongside other relevant training which supplements staff understanding and skills in managing complex patient groups for example, dementia and delirium awareness, learning disabilities and autism, mental health first aid, managing difficult conversations, courageous conversation, and safeguarding. de-escalation and breakaway
half day practical session for staff who have face to face patient contact (not only clinical) and feel that they would benefit by accessing this training. to ensure competency and confidence are kept up to date staff should attend this annually. to be signed off as competent, staff will be assessed on their breakaway techniques during the practical session. de-escalation, breakaway and safe handling
a full days training. this should be undertaken on an annual basis. this training includes the teaching of restraint and breakaway techniques. to be signed off as competent, staff will be assessed on their physical techniques during the practical session. de-escalation and breakaway – advanced physical intervention
a full day course for site teams and security only. to ensure competency and confidence are kept up to date staff should attend this annually. rapid tranquilisation
this training is currently provided for nursing staff by devon partnership trust (dpt). training for medical staff at induction twice a year (f1’s, f2’s and speciality doctors) is provided by the medical education department. an e-learning package is in development. nasogastric tube (ngt) feeding restraint training and use of safety pod
this training relates to safe holding techniques for patients under going ngt feeding in specific areas such as paediatrics, lowman ward (eastern) and capener (northern) and staples ward (northern). safe holding systems
this training relates to the safe application and management of the safe holding systems such as soft restraints and is only for staff working in specific areas such as itu. posey mitts
to be delivered via e-learning package from plymouth university hospitals nhs trust (as soon as available). staff support/debrief the trust will prioritise creating safe spaces where teams are able to reflect on their own emotional responses to the work and process the experiences that can leave people feeling overwhelmed, stressed or distressed.
creating consistent psychological safety requires leaders who are themselves fully supported. leaders have the biggest influence in creating and maintaining a psychologically safe culture within a staff group. support and debrief information can be found on hub under ‘health and wellbeing’ and includes access to trim (trauma risk management) and staff counselling.
violence and aggression pages on the hub contain useful information. occupational health should a member of staff be unable to physically deliver breakaway techniques this should be risk assessed by occupational health and amendments made to an individual's working environment. document review date march 2027 committee approving mental health steering group (mhsg), cec microguide page created 10/5/24 microguide page last checked

name: Atypical Pneumonia
searchField:
atypical pneumonia atypical pneumonia atypical pneumonia,community acquired pneumonia,cap atypical pneumonia risk factors & investigations atypical bacterial pathogens include legionella species, mycoplasma pneumoniae, chlamydophila species and coxiella burnetii. tb should also be considered in populations with risk factors. legionella pneumonia notifiable disease
legionella species are environmental organisms which cause human disease when inhaled from water or soil sources. outbreaks are associated with contaminated water systems, e.g. air conditioning units or rarely used showers. legionella pneumonia can be life threatening but is relatively rare with case rate of 9.2/million/year in europe, with most individuals being male and over 50 [9]. urinary antigen testing can detect legionella pneumophila serogroup 1. all requests are reviewed by microbiology and rejected when the pre-test probability is low. published data suggests that the following are independently associated with a higher background risk: foreign travel
high temperature (>39.4oc)
absence of sputum production
hyponatremia <133
high crp > 187
high ldh >225
thrombocytopenia (<171)
immunocompromise send urine for legionella antigen testing where > 2 of the above criteria are met
sputum cultures for legionella species should always be attempted for patients who are legionella urine antigen positive to provide isolates for epidemiological typing and comparison with isolates from putative environmental sources.
if you suspect legionella from the clinical history and the patient is unwell, please discuss antimicrobial choice with microbiology. mycoplasma pneumoniae mycoplasma pneumoniae is more common in children than adults.
epidemics tend to occur every few years and frequently arise among persons living in close quarters e.g. households, schools, universities, healthcare facilities and the military.
extra pulmonary manifestations include mucocutaneous manifestations e.g. erythematous maculopapular rash, vesicular rash through to steven johnson syndrome and cns involvement e.g. encephalitis and gullian-barre syndrome. inpatient testing is by pcr on viral throat swab. all requests will be reviewed by a consultant microbiologist unless previously advised. link: https://www.exeterlaboratory.com/microbiology/viral-respiratory-pcr/ non-beta lactam antibiotics are the treatment of choice: po azithromycin 500 mg od or doxycycline 100 mg bd for 5 days
if immunocompromised, multiply underlying co-morbidities or extrapulmonary manifestations of mycoplasma, treatment courses may need to be longer and discussion with microbiology is advised. chlamydophila species chlamydophila pneumoniae is a human infection typically presenting with mild pneumonia. it is spread in respiratory droplets and thus epidemics can occur in close living quarters. chlamydophila psittaci is a zoonotic infection associated with contact with birds and farm contact e.g. during lambing season (contact with infected lambs or products of conception). it usually presents with a systemic illness plus respiratory symptoms. typically, onset is abrupt with headache and dry cough.
chlamydophila pneumoniae can be tested for via upper respiratory tract viral pcr (see comment above regarding mycoplasma testing)
non-beta lactam antibiotics are the treatment of choice: po azithromycin 500 mg od or doxycycline 100 mg bd for 5 days
microbiology will have typically documented a plan for patients with positive results
however, please discuss with microbiology if there are concerns for slow clinical response coxiella burnettii (acute q fever) q fever is a zoonosis associated with exposure to livestock such as cattle, sheep and goats. exposure to recently born animals (including domestic animals) or their products of conception are risk factors.
although considered uncommon in the uk, the south west does have risk factors given extensive agricultural practices within the county, e.g. direct animal contact or living in the vicinity of livestock farming.
other risk factors such as travel to the middle east and certain european countries plus relevant exposure history (e.g. farm work) should raise suspicions. typically presents with an influenza-like illness (pyrexia, rigors, headache & arthralgia), non-productive cough. transaminitis often present. in patients with fever of unknown origin persisting for three weeks or more, consider q fever within the differential diagnosis
please contact microbiology if you wish to discuss suspected cases. tuberculosis (tb) suspect in patients with systemic symptoms of weight loss, fevers and night sweats with a relevant exposure history (country of birth or resided in a high incidence country, contact of someone with pulmonary tb) or history of previously treated or partially treated tb or where there is high clinical suspicion due to other aspects of history or presentation
radiological changes (x-ray or ct) which raise suspicion of pulmonary tb.
suspected cases must follow strict infection control precautions (link to policy) and be discussed with microbiology, respiratory & infection prevention teams
diagnosis relies on culture or pcr of early morning sputum or bal samples
empiric treatment is almost never given, unless directed by a respiratory or infection consultant

name: Diabetic foot infections - general comments
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines skin and soft tissue diabetic foot infections diabetic foot infections - general comments diabetic foot infections - general comments general prescribing information for diabetic foot antibiotic therapy is indicated when there is clinical evidence or suspicion of infection antibiotics are not required simply because of the presence of an ulcer if there are no signs of infection.
antibiotics are not required for a positive wound culture if there are no clinical signs of infection unless group a streptococcus (gas) or clostridium species are isolated. many wounds, especially chronic wounds, are colonised with bacteria.
simple slough on a superficial wound is not evidence of infection.
a thin rim of erythema around an ulcer is often simply a reaction to the wound and not evidence of cellulitis. erythema extending 1 cm beyond ulcer margins is more convincing of infection. infection severity mild diabetic foot infection, usually treated with oral antibiotics: localised infection with absence of systemic illness.
moderate diabetic foot infection, requiring iv antibiotics: absence of systemic illness but more extensive infection, e.g. extensive or spreading cellulitis, lymphangitic streaking.
severe diabetic foot infection, requiring iv antibiotics: systemic illness (e.g. fever, rigors, tachycardia, hypotension, confusion, vomiting, or metabolic instability such as severe hyperglycaemia). microbiology specimens should be taken only if there is clinical evidence of infection do not routinely swab an ulcer simply because it is there.
do take specimens if there is clinical evidence of infection – see above.
debride a wound before swabbing the base to improve yield of relevant pathogens.
loose bone fragments should not be sent for culture.
clearly label any samples as being from a ‘post debridement of diabetic foot’ – select this option from epic orders list. this affects the type of culture and antibiotic sensitivity testing that will be performed by the laboratory. when prescribing antibiotics this guidance is for adults of average size with normal renal and hepatic function.
review recent microbiology results, especially for mrsa, before prescribing antibiotics.
antibiotic prescriptions should be reviewed in the light of subsequent microbiology results (usually available within 72 hours). duration of oral antibiotic therapy this is primarily guided by clinical response.
simple infection without evidence of osteomyelitis – guide duration 2 weeks.
suspected or confirmed osteomyelitis – guide duration 6 weeks. consider osteomyelitis osteomyelitis is more likely if bone is exposed in the wound (visible or positive “probe to bone” test), or if a toe ulcer is associated with a typical “sausage-shaped” toe.
all diabetic foot infections with suspected osteomyelitis should be discussed with, or at least referred to, the diabetic foot clinic.
plain x-rays can diagnose osteomyelitis, but a normal early x-ray does not exclude osteomyelitis. consider other diagnoses a cool red foot may be due to severe arterial disease.
an acute charcot foot may present as red, hot or swollen and should be considered in the differential, particularly if there is neuropathy and no significant ulceration. ensure other measures to promote wound healing enacted antibiotics treat infection but do not heal ulcers.
general wound care, including dressings, offloading, and review of footwear remain essential. diabetic foot infections - general comments general prescribing information for diabetic foot antibiotic therapy is indicated when there is clinical evidence or suspicion of infection antibiotics are not required simply because of the presence of an ulcer if there are no signs of infection.

name: Help Notes
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment cns meningitis other non-viral aseptic help notes help notes    Causes of non-viral aseptic meningitis include: 
  
Tuberculosis, Lyme disease, Syphilis, Leptospira spp., Rickettsial spp., Cryptococcus, Coccidioides, Candida, Histoplasma, Blastomycosis, Toxoplasma gondii, Amoeba spp.


Drugs: NSAIDs, antimicrobials, azathioprine, IV immunoglobulin, intrathecal methotrexate, intrathecal cystine arabinoside, vaccines, allopurinol.


Systemic diseases: Sarcoidosis, leptomeningeal cancer, post-transplantation lymphoproliferative disorder, SLE, granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener’s Granulomatosis), CNS vasculitis, Behçet disease, Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome, Sjögren syndrome, Kikuchi-Fujimoto disease.

name: Tissue Samples
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services pathology microbiology microbiology tests tissue samples tissue samples tissue samples microbiology - tissue samples
introduction
for more information on the microbiology department and the tests they provide, see their page in the pathology laboratory section on the hub
turnaround times
turnaround time is between 48–72 hours. an interim microscopy report will be issued as soon as it is available.
tissue sample collection
such specimens tend to be non-repeatable and from normally sterile sites, hence results of culture or microscopy are of special importance.
please ensure all specimens are labelled appropriately and accompanied with an appropriately completed request form with full clinical details. individually wrapped sterile 30 or 60 ml containers are available on request from the pathology stores.
alternatively, tissue samples taken in theatre can be directly inoculated into cooked meat broths available on request from the microbiology laboratory.
click here for a pictorial guide to pathology swabs and containers for specimen collection. tissue samples microbiology - tissue samples

name: Necrotising pneumonia
searchField:
necrotising pneumonia necrotising pneumonia necrotising pneumonia,pneumonia,community acquired pneumonia,cap necrotising pneumonia
urgent discussion with a microbiologist is required where this presentation is suspected. it presents with a rapid onset pneumonia often with features of sepsis and possible haemoptysis (in contrast to lung abscess which may have a more indolent course over several weeks). it is observed post influenza and in immunocompromised patients. lung parenchymal necrosis is present on imaging.
distinguishing feature of necrotising pneumonia is the role of exotoxins e.g. panton valentine leucocidin (pvl) in its pathogenesis, although rare this requires urgent discussion with an infection specialist.
please review previous microbiology results to ensure the empirical therapy below has reported susceptibility results to the agents listed.
if deteriorating, features of severe disease including multiorgan involvement ensure rapid referral to icu and contact microbiology." 1st line penicillin allergy-no history of anaphylaxis severe penicillin allergy-history of anaphylaxis to penicillin or carbapenem necrotising cap iv piperacillin/tazobactam (tazocin®) 4.5g every six hours plus iv linezolid 600 mg every 12 hours iv meropenem 1g every 8 hours plus iv linezolid 600 mg every 12 hours iv levofloxacin 500mg every 12 hours plus iv linezolid 600 mg every 12 hours

name: Caesarean section
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial prophylaxis (surgical and other) surgery obstetrics caesarean section caesarean section surgery antibiotic guidelines for surgical prophylaxis v1.4 290620
caesarean section
1st line
cefuroxime 1.5g iv stat
or
severe penicillin allergy or mrsa teicoplanin 800mg iv stat (if maternal weight more than or equal to 60kg) or teicoplanin 400mg iv stat (if maternal weight less than 60kg) help notes
antibiotics given before c-section are mainly intended to reduce the incidence of surgical site infection
this means that the antibiotics must be administered before knife to skin, and generally 15 minutes before surgery (but within 1 hour of surgery)
exposure of the foetus to antibiotics has not been linked to adverse outcome
if there is co-incident chorioamnionitis or evidence of maternal sepsis, then these should be treated as per protocol. additional prophylactic antibiotics should usually be given (as in this appendix). phone microbiology for advice if necessary. caesarean section surgery- caesarean section

name: Non-operative Treatment of Appendicitis (NOTA)
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment gastrointestinal appendicitis & other intra-abdominal infection, incl. perforation treatment non-operative treatment of appendicitis (nota) non-operative treatment of appendicitis (nota) 

For full discussion of evidence and recommendations, see guideline  document on Trust Intranet “associated documentation” section.
Appendicectomy remains the gold standard treatment for appendicitis
Unless an appendicolith can be excluded on imaging, surgery should be preferred
If an appendicolith has been definitively excluded on imaging, NOTA can be discussed with patients/parents as an alternative to surgery, with the below information and caveats: 
    
The evidence for NOTA in children is considerably less than adults, with just one RCT published to date, and largely consists of non-randomized and retrospective comparisons
Overall, meta-analyses of non-randomized studies suggest NOTA has reduced efficacy, an increased readmission rate with comparable rates of complications.
However, patients with appendicoliths have considerably higher rates of failure and complications
In addition, the long term failure rates and implications are unknown
The WSES recommend ‘discussing [NOTA] with antibiotics as an alternative to surgery in children with uncomplicated acute appendicitis in the absence of an appendicolith, advising of the possibility of failure and misdiagnosing complicated appendicitis'

name: SBP treatment guidelines
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines gastrointestinal (gi) and liver spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (sbp) sbp treatment guidelines sbp treatment guidelines spontaneous bacterial peritionitis (sbp) treatment guidelines perform ascitic tap on admission for all patients admitted acutely with cirrhosis and ascites irrespective of platelet count or clotting function.
an ascitic tap with >250 pmns confirms a diagnosis of sbp
patients with bacteriascites (pmn <250, but with a positively identified organism from the ascitic fluid) should be managed in the same way as sbp
if sbp diagnosed then start adjunctive treatment with 20% human albumin solution (has): 1.5 g/kg on day 1 and 1 g/kg on day 3.
consider repeat ascitic tap on day 3 of treatment to monitor response. if there is no response (i.e. no reduction in pmn count) after 72 hours of iv antibiotic treatment, discuss with microbiology and consider resistant organisms.
patients with sbp or bacteriascites should be managed on okement ward only under the hepatology team - inform hepatology team of patient admission
a full septic screen, including ascitic tap, must be conducted prior to the initiation of antimicrobial therapy and irrespective of platelet count or clotting function 1st line piperacillin-tazobactam (tazocin®) 4.5g tds iv
duration: 5 days total mild penicillin allergy ceftriaxone 2g od iv duration: 5 days total
discuss with microbiology if previous enterococcal infection severe penicillin allergy ciprofloxacin 400mg bd iv (if patient taking quinolone prophylaxis, please discuss antimicrobial choice with microbiology)
duration: 5 days total
discuss with microbiology if previous enterococcal infection further information from full guidelines for sbp in adults cirrhosis and infection:
cirrhotic patients are at an increased risk of infections, and bacterial infection may be the cause of death in up to 50% of cirrhotic patients. sbp and utis are the commonest source of infection, followed by pneumonia, skin and soft tissue infections. incidence of infection with multidrug resistant organisms is common in cirrhosis, and this is associated with increased mortality rates, when compared with patients without cirrhosis. delays starting treatment for infection have been shown to be associated with poorer outcomes in cirrhotic patients, particularly in those with hypotension. rationale for antibiotic choice in sbp:
antibiotic choice for sbp takes into account the likely causative organisms, potential for nephrotoxicity, and the adverse outcomes of healthcare associated infections such as c. difficile. in the uk, the causative agents of sbp in order of prevalence are coliforms such as e. coli and klebsiella species, followed by gram positives such as enterococcus faecalis and streptococci. a cochrane database systematic review from 2009 included thirteen randomised control trials to assess the use of the following antibiotics for sbp: iv/po ciprofloxacin, iv ceftazidime, iv cefotaxime, iv amikacin, iv ampicillin-tobramycin, iv/po moxifloxacin, iv/po co-amoxiclav, po cefixime and po ofloxacin. the most commonly used antibiotics were 3rd generation cephalosporins although these did not demonstrate superior efficacy over other antibiotics, and ultimately this cochrane review made no firm conclusions from the randomised controlled trials. up to 10% of sbp infections are caused by gram-positive cocci (especially enterococcus species), and hence the previous guideline recommended co-amoxiclav as first line cover, with the assumption that this would provide adequate cover of coliforms and gram-positive cocci. third generation cephalosporins have also been established as the standard treatment of sbp in some british centres, but there is a move away from these agents, directed by concerns over selection for c. difficile and esbl producing coliforms. tazocin is now proposed for first line use against sbp, in order to provide appropriate antimicrobial cover (including enterococci, streptococci, and resistant gram-negative including pseudomonas species) and to avoid the use of cephalosporins, quinolones (whose activity can no longer be relied upon for empirical treatment) and gentamicin (to reduce the risk of toxicity). after 3 days of antibiotic treatment for sbp, repeat ascitic tap is recommended in order to assess response to treatment. if the pmn cell count remains high, discussion with microbiology regarding the possibility of antibiotic resistance should be sought. sbp treatment guidelines spontaneous bacterial peritionitis (sbp) treatment name: Nasal Fracture
searchField: hospital guidelines emergency medicine surgery ent nasal fracture nasal fracture nasal fracture there is no need for x-ray in a patient with a simple, isolated nasal fracture. ed management all patients must have a septal haematoma excluded. if a septal haematoma is present the patient should be referred to ent for drainage. untreated septal haematomas can cause septal perforation. follow-up/referral patients who have obvious deformity of their nose, severe swelling or difficulty in breathing should be referred to ent as an outpatient for an mua. this should be performed 7-14 days post injury. refer the patient on epic. on the 'dispo' tab click on 'common op clinic referrals from ed' and then 'referral to ent'. mark the referral as 'urgent' and write in the comments box that the patient has a nasal fracture. governance author of clinical guideline division/ department responsible for clinical guideline guideline reviewed by approving body and date approved expiry date nasal fracture nasal fracture there is no need for x-ray in a patient with a simple, isolated nasal fracture. ed management all patients must have a septal haematoma excluded. if a septal haematoma is present the patient should be referred to ent for drainage. untreated septal haematomas can cause septal perforation. follow-up/referral patients who have obvious deformity of their nose, severe swelling or difficulty in breathing should be referred to ent as an outpatient for an mua. this should be performed 7-14 days post injury. refer the patient on epic. on the 'dispo' tab click on 'common op clinic referrals from ed' and then 'referral to ent'. mark the referral as 'urgent' and write in the comments box that the patient has a nasal fracture. governance guidance review committee/group emergency medicine governance group guidance review date june 2028 eolas page date created & editor number june 2025; name: Procedures
searchField:
procedures investigations, medications and procedures procedures,sedation,suturing,mua,plastering,fractures,nerve block fascia-iliaca block,ring block,blocks,dislocation,manipulation,epistaxis,catheter,catheterisation,twoc procedures these are all procedures which you should expect to be performing by the end of your ed rotation. if in doubt, ask to be taught / supervised. record procedures in “procedure note” on epic. any medication used e.g. penthrox / local anaesthetic should also be prescribed and signed on mar. if sedation is required, use the “sedation” tab on epic to record pre-assessment and sedation details. types of procedures manipulating fractures / dislocations - speak to nurse in charge to arrange moving patient to resus to administer sedation. arrange a check x-ray to confirm satisfactory reduction.
plastering – if patient has had manipulation, arrange for plastering to be done in resus, otherwise speak to minors nurses. please speak to lead minors nurse re attending plastering training. suturing and ring blocks - local anaesthetic stored in fluid cupboard or in minors medication cupboard, suturing kits etc in minors corridor. cssd (central sterile services department) return bins in minor corridor and resus sluice. include on ed dispo when to see practice nurse for suture removal. if you staple a wound, issue patient with staple remover to take to their gp surgery appointment for removal.
fascia-illiaca block (fib) - for nof# (neck of femur fracture). ensure that patient has monitoring attached, ultrasound machine is kept in the room opposite hub, nerve block trolley in store-room outside resus. nb fib is done by ed not t&o (trauma & orthopaedics), and blocks require their own syringes and needles which are kept in the trolley.
catheter insertion - catheter trolley in store-room outside resus, further supplies in fluid cupboard including “take home” supply sets for patients who are being discharged with a new catheter. complex catheters e.g. wire guided insertions will need urology involvement. if discharging a patient with a catheter in situ who did not previously have one, remember to refer patient for appropriate follow-up e.g. trial without catheter (twoc) clinic.
epistaxis management – ent (ear, nose & throat) trolley in store-room outside resus with rapid-rhinos, silver nitrate cautery sticks etc. if patient has a rapid-rhino inserted, they will need to be referred and transferred to ent exeter prior to removal. for further information on follow up options please see the 'discharging patients' section guidance review committee/group guidance review date eolas page date created & editor number 25/7/25 editor number 009k eolas page date last checked & editor number 27/07/25 008

name: Total Reward Statements (TRS)
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my royal devon life (eastern services) all about my pay total reward statements (trs) total reward statements (trs) 
Total Reward Statements
Total Reward Statements (TRS) are an exciting new development for NHS employees. The TRS includes details of your NHS employment and the benefits available to you through work.
If you are a member of the NHS pension scheme it will also include an annual benefit statement, and will be updated and issued once a year. The statement will be based on data held for a staff on March 31 of that particular year.
Your statement will be available online via the government gateway click HERE. 
An interactive guide on how to access your statement is available click HERE. 
To find out more please visit the employee section of the TRS information website click HERE.

name: Clerking Patients (Obstetrics)
searchField:
hospital guidelines obstetrics & gynaecology induction clerking patients (obstetrics) clerking patients (obstetrics) clerking patients (obstetrics) clerking patients (obstetrics) you will see obstetric patients on both labour ward and dau.
the midwives do an initial review, find out the presenting complaint, relevant obstetric history, perform a set of observations, urine dipstick, and a fetal doppler or ctg (cardiotocography). they will then handover this information to you if they need a junior doctor review. midwives will never ask you to review a patient that is not appropriate to your level of training. for example, you should never be asked to review a ctg. certain patients will be referred directly to the middle grade.
taking an obstetric history gestation and parity
presenting complaint
associated symptoms e.g. bleeding, discharge, pain, headaches, etc.
foetal movements
problems/concerns in current pregnancy
previous obstetric history gravidity (how many pregnancies)
parity (how many pregnancies carried to term)
mode of previous deliveries
complications antenatally/intrapartum/postnatally past medical/surgical history
medications and allergies
social history examination
examination varies depending on the symptoms and history. abdominal examination is often appropriate. you don’t need to be able to assess the position/lie of the baby but you can do if you like.
if pre-eclampsia is suspected, check for reflexes, clonus and papilloedema.
neurological and cardiovascular examination may be appropriate depending on the presenting complaint. obstetrics patients can still have medical problems. speculum examination may be appropriate in cases of: pv bleeding
abnormal discharge / ?srom (spontaneous rupture of membranes)
tightenings / ?preterm labour there are specific swabs that can be taken for possible srom (actin prom)
suspected preterm labour (fetal fibronectin). the dau midwives are very experienced and will happily give you advice if you’re unsure what to do. ask the midwives or the middle grade if you’ve never taken these swabs before, or if you’re unsure about whether the patient needs one.
safest practice is to discuss every obstetric patient you see with a senior before discharging them, especially in the early days. as you gain more confidence and experience, you won’t have to discuss every single patient with a senior. if you have any doubts, no one will shout at you for checking with a senior. ctg cardiotocography dau day assessment unit srom spontaneous rupture of membranes document review date committee approving microguide page created 2023 microguide page last checked 22/8/24 clerking patients (obstetrics) clerking patients (obstetrics)

name: Maternal Sepsis
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult maternal sepsis obstetric maternal sepsis treatment maternal sepsis where the source of infection is unknown but the woman triggers: red flag x1, or amber flag x2, or
amber flag with abnormal blood results on the trust maternity sepsis screening tool treatment with antibiotics must commence within one hour of the screening tool assessment. antibiotics can be altered later if necessary to respond to the specific source of infection. where the source of infection is known, the appropriate treatment should be issued according to infectious agent; bacterial, viral, fungal or other agents. viral fungal or other treatment regimen should follow trust guidance and, as appropriate, the advice of the on-call consultant microbiologist. surgical intervention consideration should be given to eliminating the source of infection in cases where there is inadequate response to treatment or there is imminent threat to life: dilatation and curettage (post-partum)
emergency caesarean section
hysterectomy ± salpingo-oophorectomy (post-partum) [last resort] empirical antibiotic treatment first line
antepartum and intrapartum cefuroxime 1.5g tds iv and metronidazole 500mg tds iv consider adding gentamicin iv as per trust policy, if hypotension or evidence of significant end-organ dysfunction (aki or lactate above 4 mmol / l) postpartum co-amoxiclav 1.2g tds iv consider adding gentamicin iv as per trust policy, if hypotension or evidence of significant end-organ dysfunction (aki or lactate above 4 mmol / l) second line (cephalosporin or penicillin allergy) - all stages clindamycin 450mg qds iv and gentamicin iv as per trust policy duration discontinue gentamicin once signs of severe sepsis have resolved. antepartum / intrapartum: stop antibiotics either after delivery of infected tissue if afebrile, or continue for a further 24 hours if febrile at time of delivery postpartum: only continue treatment where there is a clear indication for antibiotic therapy. where organisms have been identified, liaise with microbiology to choose appropriate antibiotics and course length. treatment full clinical guideline available here (trust computers only) maternal sepsis first line piperacillin-tazobactam (tazocin®) 4.5g tds iv and clindamycin 900mg qds iv second line (mild/moderate/severe penicillin allergy) meropenem 500mg qds iv and clindamycin 900mg qds iv maternal sepsis oral switch stepdown first line cefalexin 500mg tds po and metronidazole 400mg tds po severe penicillin allergy please discuss with microbiology duration up to 7 days (dependent on clinical picture) for detailed information regarding prescribing in penicillin allergy (please follow link)

name: Red Flags
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment sepsis and septic shock neutropenic sepsis red flags red flags 
The progression of infection in neutropenic patients can be rapid, and neutropenic patients with early bacterial infections cannot be reliably distinguished from non-infected patients at presentation.  Empirical antibiotic therapy should be administered promptly to all neutropenic patients at the onset of fever or evidence of possible sepsis.

name: Always Remember To
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment bone and joint open fractures pre-operative management (start in ed) always remember to always remember to 
Give the first dose of antibiotic in the Emergency department, and within an hour of arrival.
Check the Tetanus status and treat according to Trust protocol

name: Antifungal treatment of Confirmed aspergillosis
searchField: hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines antifungal guidelines antifungal treatment of confirmed aspergillosis antifungal treatment of confirmed aspergillosis indication first line therapy 2nd line therapy comments empiric regimes confirmed aspergillosis (non-neutropenic patients) chronic cavitary pulmonary aspergillosis / allergic broncho-pulmonary aspergillosis itraconazole 200mg bd po or voriconazole po less than 40kg: 200mg bd for two doses then 100mg every 12 hours 40kg or greater: 400mg bd for two doses then 200mg bd note need testing for fungal infection – see below (notes under table 4) duration: d/w micro but typically 16 weeks (allergic broncho-pulmonary aspergillosis abpa) to lifelong (chronic cavitary aspergillus if no surgery) antifungal treatment of confirmed aspergillosis indication 1st line therapy 2nd line therapy comments empiric regimes confirmed aspergillosis (non-neutropenic patients) chronic cavitary pulmonary aspergillosis / allergic broncho-pulmonary aspergillosis itraconazole 200mg bd po or voriconazole po less than 40kg: 200mg bd for two doses then 100mg every 12 hours 40kg or greater: 400mg bd for two doses then 200mg bd note need testing for fungal infection – see testing for fungal infections duration: d/w micro but typically 16 weeks (allergic broncho-pulmonary aspergillosis abpa) to lifelong (chronic cavitary aspergillus if no surgery)

name: If No Better...
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment cns meningitis viral if no better... if no better... 
Progressive downhill course – if able, repeat LP, request CT contrast / MRI imaging (if signs on scan – rules out viral meningitis)
Partially treated bacterial meningitis may present initially with viral-type symptoms. Repeat LP.

name: Personal Support & Wellbeing at Work
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life personal support & wellbeing at work personal support & wellbeing at work personal support & wellbeing at work personal support and wellbeing at work introduction this section includes information on the occupational health department and how to access their counselling service.
the human resources department including the trust's bullying and harassment policy, and
the freedom to speak up guardians. physiotherapy for staff.
the doctor's mess. other useful sections in this guide include mental health here, night shift working advice here, and
the main 'my health and wellbeing' section. occupational health
barnstaple occupational health service, occupational health department, level 3, barnstaple health centre, vicarage street, barnstaple, ex32 7bh.
email ndht.occupationalhealth@nhs.net
phone 01271 341520
exeter occupational health service, heavitree hospital, gladstone road. exeter. ex1 2ed.
phone 01392 405800
staff can access confidential counselling through occupational health by calling 01271 341520.
their leaflet has further information and can be accessed here.
the department can also offer general health and wellbeing advice, including support with return to work programmes and sickness absence as well as tailored support to teams following a traumatic event.
human resources
the human resources advice line (01271 311533, ext.3533) is open monday to friday, 0900-1200 and is open to all staff. the hr team can answer queries and direct staff to the right support.
staff can raise concerns by talking to their line manager, with the hr team
talking to their trade union representative
making a self-referral to occupational health. the freedom to speak up guardians (ftsug) also provide confidential support and advice. they support staff who feel their concerns have not been effectively dealt with by the trust. by phone on 01271 311534 or extension 3534 internally,
by email on rduh.freedomtospeakupguardians-northern@nhs.net
by writing to ftsug at the suite 8, munro house, nddh, raleigh road, barnstaple, ex31 4jb, for more information, access the ftsug page on the hub - search 'freedom'. physiotherapy for staff more information of physiotherapy for staff is available on the hub - search 'physiotherapy service'. staff can self-refer for physiotherapy using the referral form on the hub - search for 'forms' and scroll down to forms & templates. devon wellbeing hub the devon wellbeing hub is designed to be a single place where someone concerned about their wellbeing can come, unpick their needs and be supported to get to the right services quickly.
colleagues working in healthcare, social care and the police can contact the hub free by emailing dpt.devonwellbeing@nhs.net, calling 0300 303 5455 or filling in our simple online referral form here.
to find out more about what the referral involves, click here.
doctor's mess
location the mess is inside the restaurant on level 0 and has the following facilities - sofas, a tv and computer,
games, and
snacks & beverages. the entrance code can be obtained during induction.
there is also a common room, also known as the 'old mess' located in grenville house, across the car park from the main entrance. this houses the pool table, sofas, a kitchen, surf boards and paddle boards.
fees £10 per month and payment comes directly from your wages. contact hr to apply for membership. the old mess is free to use for residents of the accomodation.
benefits include access to payday party tab,
reduced ball tickets,
use of the new mess, and
free entry to many social events (e.g movie screenings, pizza nights). social events most events will be announced via email then any updates will be posted on the mess whatsapp group. to join the mess whatsapp group – obtain details during induction. during the coronovirus pandemic, many events will not go ahead but here is a typical selection of mess events - payday party held on the last friday of the month, tab for mess members, £10 for non-members.
balls we host two balls per year. christmas ball is usually a formal black tie ocasion, the summer ball is more relaxed.
cake and catch up this is our peer support group that meets to discuss work related problems and welfare. the sessions are held on the last thursday of the month.
sports our current sports groups include football, badminton surfing & rugby. other events junior doctor forum this is a regular meeting where juniors can meet with the guardian of safe working and the director of medical education. any doctor can attend this or submit topics for discussion.
bma the bma reps are part of the mess committee. they are your point of contact for work related concerns or rota issues. get involved there are plenty of opportunities to get involved with the running of the mess. if you are interested then email the mess president - details available at induction. microguide page created 2021 last updated 03/05/2023 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 personal support & wellbeing at work personal support and wellbeing at work

bma the bma reps are part of the mess committee. they are your point of contact for work related concerns or rota issues. get involved there are plenty of opportunities to get involved with the running of the mess. if you are interested then email the mess president - details available at induction. microguide page created 2021 last updated 03/05/2023 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 eolas medical first publish layout/links checked 27/09/2024

name: Adult Guidance
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services peri-operative guidance pre-operative fasting guidelines adult guidance adult guidance pre-operative fasting adults the purpose of this document is to detail the fasting process required in preparing paediatric and adult patients for elective surgery under general or regional anaesthesia.
search 'fasting' on hub (review date feb 2025).
the anaesthetist is responsible for providing anaesthetic advice on the fasting of patients prior to surgery as requested.
staff are responsible for ensuring that all patients are given fasting instructions as per this guideline. adults solid food 6 hours
clear fluids 2 hours still water until sent for surgery clear fluid a clear non-particulate fluid is any drink through which newsprint can be read, for example water, dilute squash, fruit shoot® or clear apple juice, black tea/coffee, tea/coffee with milk (where the milk component is no more than a fifth of the total volume) thickened water is treated as a solid for the purposes of fasting times. other notes adult elective patients should be encouraged to drink clear fluids until 2 hours prior to anaesthesia and still water until sent for surgery
disease may alter the physiology of gastric emptying significantly. therefore, patients who are sick or require emergency or trauma surgery may require intravenous fluids prior to surgery and pre-operative fasting should be discussed with the anaesthetist
for all non-elective surgery refer to emergency guidance and discuss with the responsible surgeon or anaesthetist.
chewing gum or boiled sweets during the fasting period is not recommended but should not result in delayed or cancelled operation. medications necessary oral medications (premedication or a patient’s normal medication) must be given unless agreed with the anaesthetist. these can be taken with water. disease may alter the physiology of gastric emptying significantly. therefore, patients who are sick or require emergency or trauma surgery may require intravenous fluids prior to surgery and pre-operative fasting should be discussed with the anaesthetist adult guidance pre-operative fasting adults

name: Access to Inpatient Imaging
searchField:

introduction
all imaging requests (except on meadow view) should be made via epic. click here for more information. out of normal working hours there is an on-call system in place for urgent or essential imaging.
the following chart is a guide to inpatient imaging - how to access imaging and who has reporting responsibilities.
to speak to the on-call radiographers please bleep 042. please do not ask the radiographers to perform routine or non-urgent x-rays between midnight and 0700 unless the results will alter the immediate management of the patient. images required for the ward round the next day can be arranged between 0830 and 0900.
out of hours, a request on epic will not be looked at by a radiologist until the next working day unless phoned through. if you are on a device, you may need to 'pinch' the following chart so that it can be seen optimally on your screen. time xr ct mri u/s mon-fri opening times 0800–1700 0800–1800 0800–1700 0830–1700 mon-fri contact details to speak to a radiographer or chase inpatient requests phone ext 3678. between 0800 & 0900 there is reduced cover so contact the radiographer on bleep 042. the radiology secretaries can be contacted between 0900–1700 on ext 2684. to speak to a radiographer or chase inpatient requests phone ext 4130. for appointments phone ext 2741. to discuss a case, contact the liaison consultant radiologist via switchboard between 0900–1700. to speak to a radiographer or chase inpatient requests phone ext 3276. for appointments phone ext 3183. to arrange or chase inpatient appointments, or to discuss outpatients with the liaison sonographer phone ext 3518. there is a dvt service - contact the mau dvt clinic on ext 3103 for one-stop clinic access. the liaison sonographer is available to discuss scans between 0900–1600 on ext 3218. mon-fri out of hours contact details contact the on-call radiographer on bleep 042 from midnight to 0700, please do not request non-urgent x-rays. imaging required for the ward round the next day can be arranged between 0830–0900. consultant only referral except ed middle grades for protocolled scans. to request a scan between 1700–2100 contact medica via switchboard. after 2100 until 0900 contact proc. there is emergency backup from the nddh on-call radiologist via switchboard. mri requests are consultant to consultant and the scanner is unlikely to be available out of hours. contact the on-call consultant radiologist between 1600–2100 and the on-call radiographer on bleep 042 between 2100–0800. consultant only referrals to the on-call consultant radiologist via switchboard between 1600–2100. this service is not available between 2100–0800. saturday, sunday & bh contact details contact the on-call radiographer on bleep 042 from midnight to 0700, please do not request non-urgent x-rays. imaging required for the ward round the next day can be arranged between 0830–0900. consultant to consultant only referrals except for ed protocolled scans. contact the on-call consultant radiologist via switchboard. between 0900–1700 contact the on-call consultant radiologist to request a scan. between 1700–2100 contact medica via switchboard. between 2100–0900 contact proc. there is emergency backup from the nddh on-call radiologist via switchboard *there is currently no out-of-hours mri. consultant only referral for urgent referrals only for cauda equina + spinal cord compression. contact the on-call consultant radiologist via switchboard. consultant only referral. this service is not available after 2100. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 access to inpatient imaging inpatient imaging at nddh

name: FAQs
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care faqs faqs faqs 
Clexane and the Oozy Wound
On surgical wards it is common to be asked to review an oozy wound and decide if it is suitable to give a dose of clexane that evening. 
In these situations it would be appropriate to assess the wound using the steps mentioned above. Assess the amount and type of exudate.
Consider advising to do more frequent dressing changes or change the type of dressing being applied to the wound, according to the amount of exudate. 
If the exudate is bloody then consider holding clexane for a day, but the risk of VTE should be weighed against the risk of bleeding from the wound. If you are concerned, seek senior advice.

name: Gentamicin IV 5mg/kg TREATMENT: adults
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial dosing and monitoring of antimicrobials gentamicin gentamicin iv 5mg/kg treatment: adults gentamicin iv 5mg/kg treatment: adults gentamicin iv 5mg/kg treatment: adults this guidance is on the suitability, dosing and monitoring of extended interval gentamicin, in order to optimise treatment efficacy and minimize the risk of side-effects including nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity and neuromuscular blockade. extended interval dosing (previously referred to as once daily dosing) maximises the concentrationdependant killing and post antibiotic effect of aminoglycosides. extended interval gentamicin is as effective, and no more toxic, than ‘traditional’ multiple daily dose regimes but has the advantages of simple monitoring, reduced cost and administration time. incidence of gentamicin associated toxicity ranges widely according to population studied and definitions used. risk of nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity is usually associated with prolonged use and high levels, but may be idiosyncratic. toxicity can be minimised by appropriate selection of patients and appropriate dosing, therapeutic drug monitoring, duration of therapy and awareness of early signs of toxicity. indication
suspected or proven gram negative infection in accordance with trust guidelines and/or microbiology advice. determine patient suitability for extended interval gentamicin do not give gentamicin to patients with ascites, major burns (>20%) or myasthenia gravis. do not give gentamicin to patients with acute kidney injury (aki) stage 3 on admission as defined by creatinine rise ≥ 3x baseline creatinine or creatinine increase to ≥354 µmol/l or preexisting chronic kidney disease with creatinine clearance (crcl) ≤20ml/min. note that a single dose of gentamicin can be given to patients with crcl ≤20ml/min if they have severe sepsis but give as 2mg/kg, and if further doses are required, do not give any further doses until gentamicin level <1mg/l. extended interval gentamicin may not be suitable for the following patients: infective endocarditis: follow gentamicin for infective endocarditis
renal replacement (haemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, cvvh/cvvhdf): see creedy pages cystic fibrosis: discuss with respiratory clinician pregnancy: discuss with pharmacy or microbiology patients with risk factors for developing aki: if the patient has ≥3 risk factors for developing aki e.g. unresponsive hypovolaemia, hypotension, oliguria or anuria; age >65; ckd; nephrotoxic drugs; iv contrast, the first dose of extended interval 5mg/kg gentamicin can be given but a specialist registrar or consultant must be consulted before the second dose of gentamicin is given administering first dose gentamicin before the current creatinine is available in emergency admissions the first dose of extended interval 5mg/kg gentamicin can be given before the current cr is back as long as none of the exclusion criteria above are known to be present. caution in blind patients. calculate the patient’s creatinine clearance (crcl) see calculating renal function
cockcroft-gault crcl should be used for gentamicin dosing rather than the egfr produced by clinical chemistry. many elderly patients have a crcl below 50ml/min, but because of reduced muscle mass the serum creatinine may be within the normal range. it is important to calculate crcl for this group.
this is now calculated automatically on mycare if patient's height, weight and creatinine is known. calculate the gentamicin dose please prescribe via the "gentamicin in adults" order set on mycare - dose will automatically be calculated if patient's height and weight is recorded.
background to mycare calculation:
non-obese (actual body weight <120% lean body weight (lbw)) use actual body weight (abw) to calculate dose. obese (actual body weight is >120% lbw) use dose determining weight (ddw) to calculate dose (see two step calculation in box below) calculate gentamicin 5mg/kg dose (non-obese use abw; obese use ddw). then round dose to the nearest 40mg. maximum dose recommended 560mg – if the calculation suggests a greater dose please discuss with a pharmacist. mycare checks and endorsing check the patient has not had iv gentamicin in the last 24 hours (check mar for previous prescriptions). prescribe dose, intended duration and indication on mar and record in the medical notes. complete gentamicin level request on order set - this is pre-set at 10 hours to meet 6 to 14 hour monitoring window
do not prescribe beyond 5 days unless discussed with microbiology or pharmacy.
administer dose diluted in 100ml 0.9% saline or dextrose 5% over 60 minutes. if gentamicin is not administered at the same time as prescribed on mar, future administraiton times will need altering by the prescriber to allow accurate 24/36/48 hour dosing. therapeutic drug monitoring for extended interval gentamicin please order initial levels via the "gentamicin in adults" order set on mycare - this will prompt for the correct timing of therapeutic monitoring and record administration time to allow accurate interpretation of results. monitor drug levels 6–14 hours after the start of the first infusion. mycare will prompt for a level at 10 hours post initial dose. levels are processed 24 hours/day by biochemistry and will be available within 2–3 hours of receipt. level results must be checked, by a doctor or pharmacist, and acted on within 24 hours of the last dose being given: compare the gentamicin level against the urban & craig nomogram. the urban and craig nomogram can only be used to determine dosing intervals when 5mg/kg has been used. adjust the dosing interval in accordance with the nomogram do not adjust the size of the dose. maximum frequency is once daily. nurses have to acknowledge that levels have been checked, by the prescriber, before a second dose can be given, to reduce the risk of over dose in patients.
small timing adjustments (one or two hours) may be appropriate for patients that are haemodynamically stable with normal & stable renal function to bring administration times in line with routine nursing drug rounds. for example, if the second dose is due 24 hours after the first dose, which was given at 4am, the next dose can be prescribed for 6am the following day. if unsure discuss with a pharmacist. levels taken later than 14 hours cannot be interpreted using the nomogram. if the 6–14 hour window is missed instead take a trough level 18–24 hours after the last gentamicin dose was administered. if the trough level is <1mg/l the next dose can be given 24 hours after the last dose. if trough level is ≥1mg/l recheck the level 12 hours later. discuss with pharmacy if unsure. urban and craig nomogram rechecking levels for haemodynamically stable patients with stable renal function, levels should be monitored at least twice a week. most patients will receive less than 5 days of gentamicin so for stable patients two therapeutic drug levels per course should be sufficient. for patients that are haemodynamically unstable, and/or have deteriorating renal function, levels (and cr) should be taken after every dose of gentamicin. suitability criteria to receive extended interval gentamicin should also be reviewed before each gentamicin dose is given. monitor for symptoms and signs of toxicity all patients prescribed more than one dose of gentamicin should have a fluid balance chart completed and urine output monitored. renal toxicity: monitor serum creatinine 18–24 hours after the first dose. if the patient is haemodynamically stable with stable renal function measure creatinine a minimum of three times a week. deteriorating renal function whilst on gentamicin therapy may indicate gentamicin induced nephrotoxicity or nephrotoxicity from other causes such as hypovolaemia or sepsis. it may not be appropriate to continue with gentamicin therapy: refer back to the suitability criteria at the beginning of this document. cochlear toxicity symptoms and signs: fullness in ears, tinnitus or new hearing loss. consider audiometric testing. vestibular toxicity symptoms and signs: dizziness, nausea and vomiting, oscillpsia (visual blurring with head movement), true vertigo and/or nystagmus. if gentamicin toxicity is suspected discuss with senior clinician on team in the first instance. gentamicin iv 5mg/kg treatment: adults gentamicin iv 5mg/kg treatment: adults

administer dose diluted in 100ml 0.9% saline or dextrose 5% over 60 minutes. if gentamicin is not administered at the same time as prescribed on mar, future administraiton times will need altering by the prescriber to allow accurate 24/36/48 hour dosing. therapeutic drug monitoring for extended interval gentamicin please order initial levels via the "gentamicin in adults" order set on mycare - this will prompt for the correct timing of therapeutic monitoring and record administration time to allow accurate interpretation of results. monitor drug levels 6–14 hours after the start of the first infusion. mycare will prompt for a level at 10 hours post initial dose. levels are processed 24 hours/day by biochemistry and will be available within 2–3 hours of receipt. level results must be checked, by a doctor or pharmacist, and acted on within 24 hours of the last dose being given: compare the gentamicin level against the urban & craig nomogram. the urban and craig nomogram can only be used to determine dosing intervals when 5mg/kg has been used. adjust the dosing interval in accordance with the nomogram do not adjust the size of the dose. maximum frequency is once daily. nurses have to acknowledge that levels have been checked, by the prescriber, before a second dose can be given, to reduce the risk of over dose in patients.
small timing adjustments (one or two hours) may be appropriate for patients that are haemodynamically stable with normal & stable renal function to bring administration times in line with routine nursing drug rounds. for example, if the second dose is due 24 hours after the first dose, which was given at 4am, the next dose can be prescribed for 6am the following day. if unsure discuss with a pharmacist. levels taken later than 14 hours cannot be interpreted using the nomogram. if the 6–14 hour window is missed instead take a trough level 18–24 hours after the last gentamicin dose was administered. if the trough level is <1mg/l the next dose can be given 24 hours after the last dose. if trough level is ≥1mg/l recheck the level 12 hours later. discuss with pharmacy if unsure. urban and craig nomogram rechecking levels for haemodynamically stable patients with stable renal function, levels should be monitored at least twice a week. most patients will receive less than 5 days of gentamicin so for stable patients two therapeutic drug levels per course should be sufficient. for patients that are haemodynamically unstable, and/or have deteriorating renal function, levels (and cr) should be taken after every dose of gentamicin. suitability criteria to receive extended interval gentamicin should also be reviewed before each gentamicin dose is given. monitor for symptoms and signs of toxicity all patients prescribed more than one dose of gentamicin should have a fluid balance chart completed and urine output monitored. renal toxicity: monitor serum creatinine 18–24 hours after the first dose. if the patient is haemodynamically stable with stable renal function measure creatinine a minimum of three times a week. deteriorating renal function whilst on gentamicin therapy may indicate gentamicin induced nephrotoxicity or nephrotoxicity from other causes such as hypovolaemia or sepsis. it may not be appropriate to continue with gentamicin therapy: refer back to the suitability criteria at the beginning of this document. cochlear toxicity symptoms and signs: fullness in ears, tinnitus or new hearing loss. consider audiometric testing. vestibular toxicity symptoms and signs: dizziness, nausea and vomiting, oscillpsia (visual blurring with head movement), true vertigo and/or nystagmus. if gentamicin toxicity is suspected discuss with senior clinician on team in the first instance.

name: WATCh - The Wales & West Acute Transport for Children Service
searchField:
hospital guidelines anaesthetics and intensive care a to z paediatric anaesthesia watch - the wales & west acute transport for children service watch - the wales & west acute transport for children service watch - the wales & west acute transport for children service introduction
the wales & west acute transport for children service (watch) is the team that provides acute management advice and inter-hospital transport for children in south wales and south west england. watch is a joint partnership between between the two paediatric intensive care units in cardiff and bristol. the team is funded on a 24-hour basis to provide immediate management advice and transport of children requiring intensive care support. their website is here.
referring a child
for information on how to refer a child to watch, see their website here.
watch drug sheet
the watch drug sheet has been developed over many years to provide a rapid means for drawing up common drugs and infusions used in emergency paediatric situations. it reflects current practice within the lead picus served by watch in bristol and cardiff and is regularly updated in response to best evidence, changes in practice and user demand. .

name: Diagnostic Classification and Things to Watch Out For
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment bone and joint open fractures pre-operative management (start in ed) diagnostic classification and things to watch out for diagnostic classification and things to watch out for 
An open fracture is defined as a broken bone that is in communication with the environment through broken skin. Requires urgent senior orthopaedic review.
Open fracture classifications [Gustilo]: 
  
Type I: Open fracture with a skin wound less than 1cm long and clean
Type II: Open fracture with a laceration more than 1cm long without extensive soft tissue damage, flaps or avulsions
Type III: Either an open segmental fracture, an open fracture with extensive soft tissue damage, or a traumatic amputation.

Monitor the patient for complications such as compartment syndrome.
Do not take wound swabs unless there is clearly infection and frank pus present as culture results are rarely helpful from a fresh, contaminated wound.

name: Necrotizing Otitis Externa (NOE)
searchField:
necrotizing otitis externa (noe) ear, nose and throat (ent) otitis externa
necrotising
necrotizing
noe necrotizing otitis externa necrotizing (malignant) otitis externa (noe) is caused by pseudomonas aeruginosa in more than 95% of cases. other significant organisms include staphylococcus aureus, as well as aspergillus species. deep samples are particularly important in noe. this is because of the possibility of serious complications, use of broad-spectrum antibiotics with long course lengths, and the limited options for treating pseudomonas. imaging is indicated to look at extent of infection and bone erosion. topical antibiotics have no place for the treatment for noe. please refer to recent susceptibilities for pseudomonas species when isolated and ensure the empirical regimen below is labelled as susceptible. please contact microbiology if there are concerns regarding resistance. treatment is at least 28 days with close review. in the case of treatment failures please discuss with microbiology. if a prolonged course of iv antibiotics is required please refer to the opat team to aid discharge planning. no previous pseudomonas species isolated:
1st line: piperacillin/tazobactam tazocin© 4.5g qds iv penicillin allergy: vancomycin iv (as per trust guidelines) + ciprofloxacin 750mg bd po
note: ciprofloxacin is associated with an increased risk of clostridium difficile diarrhoea, tendonitis, muscle pain, peripheral neuropathy, aortic aneurysm rupture / dissection, suicidal ideation and prolonged qt interval. tendonitis risk increases with age and steroid use (may occur within 48 hours of starting). pseudomonas species isolated and listed as susceptible:
1st line: ceftazidime 2g tds iv (reduce dose if over 80 years, or renal impairment) penicillin allergy: ciprofloxacin 750mg bd po note: ciprofloxacin is associated with an increased risk of clostridium difficile diarrhoea, tendonitis, muscle pain, peripheral neuropathy, aortic aneurysm rupture / dissection, suicidal ideation and prolonged qt interval. tendonitis risk increases with age and steroid use (may occur within 48 hours of starting).

name: Mild Acute Infection Treatment
searchField: hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment gastrointestinal oropharyngeal candidiasis mild acute infection treatment mild acute infection treatment acute infections should normally resolve with good oral hygiene and topical treatment. pay attention to risk factors (see non-pharmacological measures) first-line nystatin oral suspension 100,000 units/ml 1ml qds po nb: not absorbed systemically, can be used alongside systemic treatment for fungal infection if required. avoid contact with eyes and other mucous membranes. treatment usually for 7 days, and continued for 48 hours after lesions have resolved. divide administration of the dose between both sides of the mouth. administer after feeds/meals/drinks, advise the patient to retain the suspension in their mouth near to lesions for as long as possible before swallowing (ideally 10 minutes or more). remove any dental devices at night and brush and rinse thoroughly using appropriate cleaning chemicals for the material, to remove contamination and avoid reinfection. second-line miconazole oral gel 20mg/g 2.5ml qds po nb: systemically absorbed, must be discontinued when prescribing other systemic azole or imidazole antifungal treatment and should be reviewed with other types of systemic antifungal treatment. avoid using miconazole gel in pregnant and breastfeeding patients, patients with liver impairment and in patients taking anti-epileptics, anti-coagulants, sulfonylurea anti-diabetics, statins, anti-rejection/immune suppression medications, anti-psychotics, mizolastine, reboxetine. treatment should be continued for at least 7 days after lesions have resolved. administer after meals/drinks, advise the patient to retain the gel in their mouth near to lesions before swallowing, remove any dental devices at night and brush with gel to remove contamination and avoid reinfection. mild acute infection treatment acute infections should normally resolve with good oral hygiene and topical treatment. name: Useful Clothing
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook settling in to exeter life useful clothing useful clothing useful clothing 

Useful Clothing

It is important to wear what you feel comfortable in, but you will probably find that most people in the UK have these key items listed below in their wardrobes.
Winter coats – choose one that will keep you warm and dry in all weather, although you can also keep a separate, lighter raincoat for those spring showers. And definitely take an umbrella.
Gloves, a scarf and a warm hat or earmuffs.
Trainers (sneakers), or any shoes comfortable enough for running.
Waterproof shoes such as Wellington or hiking boots, for rainy and snowy days. Remember: rain can damage leather, so treat any leather boots, gloves and bags with a protective waterproof spray.
Jumpers (sweaters), cardigans or hoodies.
Clothes you don’t mind getting dirty. You might be taking part in a charity race, a baking competition, a muddy obstacle course or a game of paintball for a special occasion – and you’ll be grateful for that old tank top and tracksuit bottoms.
A smart shirt, blouse or dress, and smart shoes for evenings out – it’s common to wear jeans and trainers to pubs, bars and restaurants, but you might want to dress more elegantly once in a while. Dress appropriately for a formal event – this usually means a suit and tie for men and a cocktail dress or gown for women. Don’t be afraid to ask if you’re not sure about the dress code.
For parties and nights out, people dress in many different styles, from casual jeans to glamorous outfits. If it is a more formal evening event, such as a university ball, this might mean wearing a dress
The UK is a multicultural place and it is common for people to wear clothing associated with their culture and religion, for example a head scarf, kippah, turban, sari or long skirt. In major towns and cities, you will find specialist shops selling clothing from around the world, particularly from Asia and the Middle East.

name: General Surgery F1
searchField:
general surgery f1 foundation year 1 posts general surgery f1 general surgery f1 post
introduction
this section includes the following information on the f1 post in general surgery at nddh useful resources,
a brief explanation of the post and what it entails - please help us to keep this up to date and relevant by sending suitable content to rduh.eolas@nhs.net.
an example of an f1 workplace training schedule (wts) for the specialty to help you plan your learning and development throughout the post, and
a curriculum mapping guide (cmg) to illustrate what might be achieved in the post and how these achievements can be mapped in your eportfolio. this section has not been updated for a while and some content may be out-of-date. if you are an f1 in this specialty or have recently completed this post, please help us to make this section useful and relevant to those that will come after you - get in touch with the medical editor and clinical lead at rduh.eolas@nhs.net with your ideas for content.
useful resources for the post
1. there is an excellent general surgery junior's handbook which can be accessed as follows* for the most up-to-date version go to the g-drive and click on general surgery timetables then junior doctors. 2. a collection of intranet pages and documents relevant to a surgical post can be found together on the general surgery page on the hub - search 'general surgery' and click on northern services.
* if your first f1 post at ndht is surgery and you do not yet have access to the hub or g-drive, please email the medical education centre on rduh.meded@nhs.net and a copy of the junior doctor handbook will be sent to you.
f1 to f1 job description
in the table below is a brief summary of the f1 post in general surgery written by one of your colleagues. are you the current f1 in surgery or have you recently completed the post? if yes, why not write some top tips on the role and your duties to help the next doctor taking up the post? what do you wish you had known before starting the job? contact us at rduh.eolas@nhs.net with your ideas for content. base locations aaa (ambulatory assessment area) for emergency assessment
kgv ward for short-stay inpatients
lundy ward and lundy bay on capener (lboc) for urology and long-stay inpatients
day surgery unit (dsu) for elective day cases
caroline thorpe ward for paediatrics - under 16y rota shift times are as follows normal day monday to friday - 0800–1630
on call long day - 0800–2030
night shifts - 2000–0830 annual leave requests must go through eroster. job summary f1s will be allocated to one of the two firms, colorectal and upper gi. there is also a ‘breast bleep’ which is usually carried by an upper gi f1 or someone with a particular interest in the field. general responsibilities include ensuring the list is updated in a timely manner,
preparing notes for morning round,
reviewing patients on the wards,
requesting investigations,
assisting in theatre when the opportunity arises. supervision the f1 will be allocated a surgical consultant as their clinical supervisor. this will usually be the consultant from their allocated firm.
day to day supervision on the ward is variable. after the consultant ward round in the morning, you will work alone or with another junior doctor or physician associate. senior support is available from the other doctors on the ward if present, or via the on call team. week day timetable 0800 ward round - usually consultant or spr led it's worth arriving a little early to ensure that the list is up-to-date. 0900 prioritise jobs, request scans, take bloods that haven’t been completed by the phlebotomy team
1200–1300 approximately chase bloods and scans ensure copies of reports are in the notes 1400 complete jobs then assist the take team if your firm isn’t busy
1530–1630 update list and request investigations for the next morning. on-call duties 1630 second post-take round and day handover from internal firm teams.
1630–2000 clerk patients, complete jobs aiming to avoid the handover of routine jobs to the night team.
2000–2030 handover to night team. week day meetings colorectal grand round - wednesday 1330 in the doctors office. upper gi team meeting - thursday 1330 in the consultant office (level 3 beside theatre) both of these meetings involve presenting patients so having an up-to-date list of patients and their conditions is paramount. these meetings are good opportunities for wbas. rota details may also be discussed. weekend handover - every friday at 1330 in the clinical skills suite in the medical education centre. clinics clinics provide a great opportunity for observation, supervised learning and the completion of wbas. the upper gi sas doctor is responsible for allocating duties on the rota. alternatively, you can approach consultants directly. formal & group teaching tuesday 1230–1430 bleep free teaching for the f1s in the mec.
sometimes there are teaching sessions after friday handover.
small group teaching these sessions usually take place on the 3rd thursday of the month at the bull & bear. they are led by a consultant or sas doctor and a certificate of attendance for your portfolio is provided. the physician's associate (pa) offers teaching in the afternoons if there are specific learning requests. informal teaching there are many opportunities for informal learning including assisting in theatre
attending clinics
assisting the on-call team the f1 can do any of the above when rota'd to be on a normal day as long as the ward isn't busy and the bleep is covered by another doctor. top tips keep the list up to date in a timely manner.

name: Organisms
searchField:

The predominant aerobic and facultative bacteria in abdominal infections associated with diverticulitis include Escherichia coli and Streptococcus spp. Frequently associated anaerobes include Bacteroides spp. (B. fragilis group), Peptostreptococcus, Clostridium and Fusobacterium spp.

paediatric antimicrobial treatment guidelines paediatric antimicrobial prescribing guidelines – introductory notes to use the royal devon university nhs foundation trust, which includes rduh and nddh, are now adopting the uk paediatric antimicrobial stewardship network (ukpas) guidelines which can be found at: link for trust computers link if in eolas app link to uk-pas website (pdf) please use ukpas guidelines for: general paediatric wards, all ages (do not use on the neonatal unit) guide for tips on good antimicrobial prescribing practice antimicrobial stewardship section for the uk national antimicrobial stewardship algorithm ‘start smart then focus’.
iv to oral switch (ivos) guidance guidance on antimicrobial choices use trimethoprim as first line treatment in uti as local resistance rates are low. iv vancomycin is our glycopeptide antibiotic of choice, information on dosing and monitoring can be found at paediatric therapeutic drug monitoring table iv gentamicin is our aminoglycoside antibiotic of choice, information on dosing and monitoring can be found at paediatric therapeutic drug monitoring table use alternatives to fluoroquinolones, see mrha alert. if ciprofloxacin treatment is required, please counsel parents/carers and provide the following counselling sheet ciprofloxacin for bacterial infection – medicines for children use flucloxacillin rather than the broader spectrum alternatives.
use iv ceftriaxone as our cephalosporin of choice. use hospital mrsa decolonisation guidelines for paediatric patients on co-amoxiclav the patient information leaflet outlines diarrhoea as a side effect and what to do co-amoxiclav for bacterial infections – medicines for children. additional guidelines
