name: Treatment
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology viral retinitis treatment treatment 
HSV or VZV requiring IV treatment initially

Aciclovir 10mg/kg TDS IV (ideal body weight) for 5-7 days

Then

Valaciclovir 1g TDS PO for 6 weeks



If taking a vitreal or aqueous fluid sample, then inject Foscarnet* 2.4mg in 0.1mL intra-vitreally stat dose in theatre under supervision of consultant ophthalmologist

* NB. Foscarnet is a special-order item and may take some time to obtain from Pharmacy. Contact Pharmacy as soon as possible if unable to transfer out to a specialist ophthalmic centre for this procedure.


CMV   

Ganciclovir* 5mg/kg BD IV for 2-3 weeks

After resolution of initial infectious episode, continued suppressive therapy may be required: Ganciclovir implants may be considered. Discuss with microbiology consultant.
 
* Ensure women of childbearing age are not pregnant or breastfeedingand that men and women take adequate contraceptive precautions during and for at least 90 days after treatment with ganciclovir due to genotoxic and teratogenic potential.

name: Treatment
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment cns meningitis bacterial treatment treatment 
Penicillin resistant pneumococci are uncommon in the UK but more prevalent in eg Spain and US. Inform Microbiology if patients from the continent are admitted with meningitis, as this may change their management.

First Line empirical treatment
 
 Ceftriaxone 2g BD IV*

  


    Immunocompromise or adults 60 years and over, consider adding in cover for Listeria:  


 Amoxicillin 2g six times daily IV

 Second Line empirical treatment (in cases of anaphylaxis to cephalosporins) 
   

 Chloramphenicol 25mg/kg QDS IV*


      
  

    Adults 60 years and over and immunocompromised, consider adding in cover for Listeria:

 

 Co-trimoxazole 30mg/kg QDS IV. (NB . Ampoules are 480mg/5mL, dose may need rounding for safe measurement. Patients with GFR under 30mL/min give 50% dose. Avoid use in patients with blood disorders, discontinue if rash or blood disorders develop).


      
  
 AND


      
  

    For eradication of throat carriage in all cephalosporin allergic patients (in addition to treatment with chloramphenicol IV) 
  

      
  
 Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO  single dose


      
  

    OR, if contra-indicated, 
  

      
  
 Rifampicin 600mg BD PO  for 2 days.

  

  *  If resistance is suspected e.g. patient has recently arrived from a country where resistant pneumococci are prevalent (contact microbiologist for advice), add in Vancomycin IV  as per local protocol - but target trough levels at upper limit (20mg/L), or  Rifampicin 600mg BD PO/IV .

 


Consider adding Dexamethasone 10mg 6 hourly IV for 4 days to prevent neurological sequelae, if commencing at the same time or up to 4 hours after the first dose of antibiotics.
 
NB. Some guidelines recommend dexamethasone may be stopped if causative organism is not either:  H.influenzae or S.pneumoniae but others advise continue irrespective of isolate. Exercise clinical judgement based on severity of initial symptoms and risks of neurological sequelae.

Where source control not possible / collections known / suspected, liaise with microbiology for further antibiotic treatment advice



Duration depends on likely organism: 
 
Neisseria meningitidis: 7 days
Streptococcus pneumonia: 10-14 days
Listeria monocytogenes: 21 days (minimum, may require longer depending on clinical signs)
Haemophilus influenza: 7-10 days
Staphylococcus aureus: 14 days (minimum, may require longer depending on clinical signs)
Culture negative, but CSF results suggest bacterial meningitis: Usually 2 weeks duration
 
There is generally no oral switch.
 
Assess patient’s clinical condition when making the decision to stop, some patients may require longer courses if source control is not achieved.

name: Quick reference list: Classic UTI Signs and Symptoms
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment genito-urinary urinary tract infection lower uti, no sepsis, no catheter quick reference list: classic uti signs and symptoms quick reference list: classic uti signs and symptoms 
Diagnose urinary tract infection (UTI) in patients with 3 or more symptoms in total, which must include at least one of the highlighted symptoms:

Dysuria — discomfort, pain, burning, tingling or stinging associated with urination.
Frequency
Urgency
Incontinence.
Urinary retention
Changes in urine appearance or consistency: Urine may appear cloudy to the naked eye, or change colour or odour. 
    
Haematuria may present as red/brown discolouration of urine or as frank blood.
 
Nocturia
Suprapubic discomfort
Fever
Raised inflammatory markers


Atypical – generalized non-specific clinical features more usual in elderly patients with underlying cognitive impairment or indwelling urinary catheter (particularly if more than 7 days) in situ — consider UTI if the patient presents with:

Acute delirium / behavioural change
Lethargy
Resistance to care activities being carried out
Reduced ability to independently carry out activities of daily living
Falls
Temperature 1.5ºC above normal twice in 12 hours
Anorexia.
description: Quick reference list: Classic UTI Signs and Symptoms

name: If No Better...
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment respiratory community acquired pneumonia curb 3-5 if no better... if no better... 
Antibiotic Failure is Uncommon
If patients fail to respond to first-line treatments then first consider alternative diagnoses.
If lung aetiology still likely, consider investigating for:



Empyema – all effusions in febrile patients should be tapped (see Empyema and lung abscess guidance).
Viral infection, PCP, tuberculosis
Post-infectious inflammatory response
Other diagnoses: especially pulmonary embolus, pulmonary oedema
Metastatic infection from another source such as endocarditis
Lung abscess
Take a travel history (if applicable)
Recent exposure to birds – to exclude Psittacosis
 


In patients who are fit for follow-up post discharge, and with consolidation on original CXR: a repeat CXR at 6 weeks is required to investigate other aetiology (referral to follow-up clinic should be decided during inpatient admission).

name: Acute aspiration – hospital acquired (> 5 days hospital admission)
searchField:
acute aspiration – hospital acquired (> 5 days hospital admission) acute aspiration – hospital acquired (> 5 days hospital admission) acute aspiration
hospital acquired aspiration pneumonitis is a chemical lung injury caused by inhalation of gastric contents. many of these patients have resolution of symptoms within 24 to 48 hours and require only supportive treatment, without antibiotics.
antibiotics should only be used for patients who fail to improve 48 hours post aspiration or who develop a septic picture/pneumonia.
anaerobes are uncommon in patients hospitalized with suspected aspiration. patients do not require empiric anaerobic coverage for suspected aspiration pneumonia unless patients have risk factors such as loss of consciousness (e.g. alcohol usage, drug overdose, seizures) with concomitant gingival disease or oesophageal mobility. it is important that anaerobic coverage is included when patients with aspiration pneumonia are at particularly high risk of anaerobic infection, e.g. those with obvious dental/periodontal disease, putrid sputum production or those in whom lung abscess/empyema is suspected.hospitalised patients or those with frequent healthcare contact are more frequently colonised with gram negative organisms in the upper respiratory tract and therefore empirical antimicrobial therapy for these patients differs to reflect this. the severity of the pneumonia should be guided by clinical review and review of appropriate investigations (e.g. cxr findings, inflammatory markers). severity scoring systems such as curb-65 and a-drop can be utilised as part of the overall assessment. input from a senior stroke physician should be sought for all patients with acute stroke.
hospitalised patients or those with frequent healthcare contact are more frequently colonised with gram negative organisms in the upper respiratory tract and therefore empirical antimicrobial therapy for these patients differs to reflect this. n.b. hospital acquired pneumonia also applies to patients who have had a recent prior hospitalisation (including community hospital or rehab unit), long-term dialysis patients and those that reside in a nursing or care home. 1st line treatment for acute aspiration (hospital acquired) mild/moderate: doxycycline 200mg od po/ng (or co-trimoxazole 960mg bd iv if unable to swallow or does not have an ng tube) plus stat gentamicin 5mg/kg iv
severe: co-amoxiclav 1.2g iv tds plus stat gentamicin 5mg/kg iv treatment for acute aspiration in penicillin allergy (hospital acquired) mild/moderate: doxycycline 200mg od po/ng (or co-trimoxazole 960mg bd iv if unable to swallow or do not have an ng tube) plus stat gentamicin 5mg/kg iv
severe: chloramphenicol 12.5mg/kg (max 1gram) iv qds duration of treatment for acute aspiration 5 days only.
where there is lack of clinical response and evidence of treatment failure, please seek advice from microbiology. extended courses of antimicrobials for aspiration pneumonia must be discussed with microbiology. when prescribing, please state whether pneumonia is mild, moderate or severe.

name: Follow-up, re-dosing and course lengths
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial gentamicin and vancomycin help gentamicin in adults standard gentamicin dosing protocol follow-up, re-dosing and course lengths follow-up, re-dosing and course lengths 
Step 4: How to decide if monitoring of levels is required
 

 Monitoring of levels is not required if:

 

  eGFR is greater than 60 ml/min/1.73m2,

 


    Duration of treatment is expected to be for less than or equal to 5 days, 
  


    AND no significant change in renal function (classed as an increase in serum creatinine of more than 150% over baseline at the time of initiation). 
  


    In these patients give the calculated dose every 24 hours. 
  

In all other patients, monitor levels as in step 5. Note that gentamicin levels are only usually measured in the laboratory between 10am and 10pm.

Step 5: How to take gentamicin levels

Take a level 6-14 hours after the first dose only
 
Routine monitoring of gentamicin levels subsequently is not required (unless changes in renal function measured or suspected)
 
Place sample (minimum volume 2mL) in a yellow-topped bottle (clotted blood) and document on the microbiology request form the following:
 



EXACT time and date the last dose was given (see prescription chart);
EXACT time and date the sample was taken.
 

 
The specimen bottle and form must be labelled according to the Trust Specimen Acceptance Policy.
 
Do not take the blood sample from the IV line used for gentamicin administration.
 
Assays will usually be processed between 10am and 10pm. Levels will not be processed outside this window. Assay results will generally be available within one hour of processing. If assay results are required outside this window, then the case must be discussed with the duty Consultant Microbiologist.
Step 5½: What to do if 6-14 hours post-dose window is missed for taking levels
Take a trough level at 22-24 hours post-dose, after the first dose only.

See instructions in "patients excluded from normal dosing protocol" for follow-up and re-dosing instructions

Step 6: How to re-dose gentamicin when a 6-14 hour post-dose level has been sent
Interpret the blood gentamicin level using the nomogram below.
For subsequent dosing, there should be no change to the initial dose. The interval between doses is adjusted according to the Urban-Craig nomogram below.

Step 7: Duration of treatment and repeated monitoring

Review all prescriptions on a daily basis and contact microbiology for advice on alternative agents in on-going infection.

Courses should not exceed 5 days in duration without microbiology input.
 
Toxicity is very uncommon with short courses (less than 5 days) of gentamicin. Toxicity is more likely to occur in patients receiving other nephrotoxic drugs (e.g. NSAIDs, cyclosporin, glycopeptides).
 
U&Es need to be checked at least every 48 hours in all patients on once daily (or extended interval) gentamicin. Re-check levels as per step 5-6 if significant change in renal function.

name: General Interest and Other Points
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment skin and soft tissue breast abscess and mastitis general interest and other points general interest and other points 
Advise lactating women to continue breastfeeding if possible (including from the affected breast).
If this is too painful, or the infant refuses to breastfeed from the affected breast, advise the woman to express the milk (by hand or with a breast pump) until she is able to resume breastfeeding from that breast.
Because milk stasis is often the initiating factor in lactational mastitis, the most important management step is frequent and effective milk removal. Sudden cessation of breastfeeding in women with lactational mastitis increases the risk of abscess formation

name: Community Acquired Pneumonia - Clinical diagnosis
searchField:
community acquired pneumonia - clinical diagnosis and diagnostic test recommendations community acquired pneumonia - clinical diagnosis community acquired pneumonia,clinical diagnosis,cap community aquired pneumonia (cap) clinical diagnosis and diagnostic test recommendations diagnosis is made in patients presenting with acute respiratory tract symptoms and should be supported by the chest x-ray (cxr) findings. however, it is recognised that in early pneumonia the cxr may be normal in approximately 10% of patients and therefore correlation with clinical symptoms is key. there can be initial diagnostic uncertainty in certain populations, especially the elderly. care should be taken not to assume that there is pneumonia when there is no clear x-ray evidence of consolidation as gram negative sepsis of urinary tract or gut origin can present with some features that may be confused with chest infection, e.g. crackles in chest. it is important that these patients have gram negative cover, a stat dose of iv gentamicin 5 mg/kg (hyperlink to eolas page for gentamicin prescribing), in addition to the antimicrobial agents outlined in the tables below, will broaden cover to include common causes of gi and urinary infections, while the source is being determined. clinical judgement is required, especially when treating younger patients for example, as the curb-65 score may significantly underestimate cap severity. recurrent cap (two or more episodes in 12 months) and invasive pneumococcal disease are both listed as human immunodeficiency virus (hiv) indicator conditions in the 2020 bashh guidelines – an hiv test should be offered to all patients presenting with either of the above diagnoses. ensure a recent travel history is taken, this is important in terms of determining the risk of an infection from a variety of pathogens including legionella species, middle eastern respiratory syndrome (mers), or other bacterial/viral/fungal pathogens directly linked to travel. this is also important in determining the appropriate infection prevention measures required for febrile returning travellers. immunocompromised patients: in patients with acquired immunodeficiency (malignancy – solid or haematological, hiv, prescribed immunomodulatory drugs or steroids) or primary immunodeficiency, the underlying infectious aetiology is broad. consider bacterial, viral, fungal & atypical infection e.g. pneumocystis jirovecii and discuss investigations & treatment with a microbiologist as required. in undifferentiated disease where the source of infection is unclear, please use the guideline on eolas for treatment of sepsis of unknown site. community aquired pneumonia (cap) test recommendations for clinical diagnosis radiological microbiological histology blood tests initial cxr and 6 week follow up (please see below) a respiratory viral “quad” swab for influenza a+b, covid and rsv will help to distinguish viral and bacterial infections.
viral infections do not require antibiotics unless a superadded bacterial infection is suspected.
viral testing is routinely performed during the winter, unless a patient has additional risk factors for viral aetiology such as travel or immunocompromise.
please discuss with a microbiologist if testing outside of this time is required for patients with curb65 >/=2, sputum for mc&s and blood cultures should be sent, ideally prior to the initiation of antimicrobial therapy. if atypical pathogens are suspected, please obtain a thorough history to support this, and consult microbiology about the appropriateness of further investigations. testing for mycobacterium species (including m. tuberculosis - tb) is not routinely undertaken unless requested and risk factors are identified which include but are not limited to: household contact with person(s) with confirmed diagnosis of tb
history of immunosuppression (including hiv)
persons who injected drugs
history of previous tb
birth or residence in country of high tb burden (as defined by who).
radiology suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis. where clinical concerns exist for tb please consult either respiratory team or microbiology. where tb is suspected please refer to trust isolation policy for ipc advice including the use of ffp3 facemasks for staff (link to policy). n/a fbc, u+es, crp, lfts curb-65 score
the curb-65 score is widely used for risk stratifying a patient’s risk of death following admission for cap1. a patient with a score <2 has <3% mortality risk. this rises to an intermediate risk of 3-15% mortality risk with a score of 2. patient with a score >2 have more than 15% mortality risk.
caution: the curb-65 score is not a substitute for good clinical judgement and clinicians should also take account of other prognostic factors such as severe hypoxaemia, multilobar involvement on cxr, and raised early warning score (ews) prior to deciding on treatment pathway. the curb-65 score can seriously underestimate the severity of pneumonia in young previously fit adults.

name: Chest Radiograph (CXR)
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook clinical support requesting and interpreting radiographs chest radiograph (cxr) chest radiograph (cxr) requesting and interpreting a chest radiograph (cxr)
introduction
this section gives some guidance on the indications for requesting a cxr and how to interpret them.
it is important to remember that all test results should be interpreted in the clinical context.
normal test results do not always rule out pathology - do not be falsely reassured.
any abnormal or unexpected test results should be escalated to seniors.
any test result you don't fully understand should be escalated to seniors. requesting a cxr see under the radiology tab in my hospital life here for your responsibilities regarding the exposure of patients to ionising radiation under ir(me)r rules.
it is also your responsibility to ensure that all request forms are fully completed - again, there is guidance under the radiology tab.
remember that the request form is a legal document. it must be legible and include your name, designation as well as contact details.
on the form, be sure to ask the appropriate clinical question. the more information you give on the form, the more likely the radiographer will perform the optimum views to answer your question.
make sure that you understand the reason for requesting the radiograph and how it will change or influence management. if you are writing the form you need to be sure that the radiation exposure is justified. if you are not sure, ask your seniors before requesting it.
regardless of the reason for requesting a cxr, always include the patient's smoking history. this is important information which is relevant when reporting a film and will impact on any recommendations made.
cxr interpretation
this guidance provides a systematic way of reviewing radiographs to minimise the risk of missing abnormalities especially when you are tired!
you will soon develop your own method but this is good to get you started and to refer to when you are unsure.
get into the habit of checking the following on all radiographs before you start looking for clinical clues. patient name/ and nhs number – is it the correct patient?
date – is it the most recently requested image or an older one?
correct image – has the correct view and/or side that was requested been imaged.
type of image taken – is it pa or ap, supine or lateral - this may be important to interpretation
are there older images available that may be useful for comparison to optimise your interpretation, view the radiograph on the highest resolution screen possible. rotate your device to view the following table. adequacy a pa film is the standard and preferred projection as this allows optimal assessment of the cardio-mediastinal contour. however, this may not be possible if your patient is very unwell as it requires the patient to stand. the whole of the lung fields should be visible from the apices to the costophrenic angles penetration lower thoracic vertebrae should be just visible through the heart rotation the medial ends of the clavicles should be equidistant from the thoracic spinous process inspiration the right side of the 9th posterior rib should meet the right hemi-diaphragm look always use a systematic approach to reduce the chance of missing the unexpected - look at the whole radiograph and the radiograph as a whole.
keep in mind the clinical picture and the clinical question you are asking - actively look for likely pathology - you won't see what you don't look for airway the trachea should be central. it bifurcates at t6 with the hilum at t6/7 breathing lung fields – check for equal radiolucency when comparing both sides. bearing in mind the clinical picture, think about and look for aymmetry and abnormalities, specifically 1. collapse, consolidation or contusion 2. shadowing interstitial (fibrosis),
nodular
“fluffy” – oedema 3. air pneumothorax – no lung markings between the lung edge & the chest wall
pneumo-mediastinum - air outlines the mediastinum
bullae – darker areas typically seen in copd
air below the diaphragm indicating a ruptured viscus 4. effusion fluid meniscus at the costophrenic angles 5. masses round – tumour, tb, abscess, cyst etc
plaques – thickened pleura
nipple shadow bowel/gi tract bowel loops above the diaphragm - present in diaphragmatic rupture
gas shadow +/- fluid level behind the heart - suggestive of hiatus hernia bones rib fractures and metastases are easier to identify with the radiograph rotated through 90°
check spine alignment
check upper limb girdle e.g. clavicle, humeral head etc circulation look at mediastinum and heart
on a pa film the normal width of the mediastinum is less than 8cm
on a pa film, the normal cardiothoracic ratio (ctr) is less than 50%
the normal position of the heart is 1/3 to right of midline and 2/3 to left of midline calcification look at thoracic aorta diaphragm the right hemi-diaphragm is usually higher than left
a flattened diaphragm suggests hyperinflation of the lungs
air under the diaphragm could be free air, a stomach bubble or a bowel loop extras lines e.g. ngt, ett, ecg
evidence of previous surgery, eg clips soft tissues subcutaneous emphysema – air in soft tissues special areas check apices
behind the heart
cardiophrenic angles
costophrenic angles potential pitfalls pneumothorax mimics reservoir bag
bullae
edge of scapula
air trapped in skin folds rib fractures in children – consider nai
supine images may miss pneumothorax or haemothorax- compare radiolucency of lung fields free air under diaphragm – patient needs to be well enough to sit up for 5-10mins image ​ credit tor ercleve/litfl references & elearning cxr - a systematic approach. an article by dr laurence knott, last reviewed aug 2012, hosted on patient.info, available here. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 chest radiograph (cxr) requesting and interpreting a chest radiograph (cxr)

name: Sleep
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my health and wellbeing sleep sleep sleep sleep see also the section on night shift working here.
good sleep is essential for health and wellbeing. it can be difficult to get into a healthy routine of sleep when you are starting a new job, are working in shifts and have just moved to a new area.
here are some tips from the sleep council (see below) on how to get a good night's sleep. restful environment
bedroom just for sleep and rest ideally
avoid screen time
avoid alcohol as this upsets sleep patterns
avoid smoking as this disturbs sleep patterns
try some relaxation before bed e.g. bath, relaxation app etc.
deal with worries before bed or write them down to deal with tomorrow.
if you can’t sleep change something rather than worrying that you can’t sleep. try something that you find relaxing and then when you feel rested go back to bed. click here for more information on sleep and sleep hygiene (also useful for your patients!) in this patient info website article 'insomnia, poor sleep'. updated by dr doug mckechnie, 24/06/24, peer reviewer dr colin tidy. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 sleep sleep

name: Treatment - Fungal
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology keratitis treatment - fungal treatment - fungal 
Voriconazole 1% preservative-free eyedrops

1 drop instilled into affected eye(s) every 5 minutes for 30 minutes, then hourly for 24-48 hours, then TDS for 7 days.


If severe or unresponsive disease, add a second topical agent:

Amphotericin B 0.15% eyedrops preservative-free (special order from other hospital manufacturing unit)

1 drop instilled into affected eye(s) every 5 minutes for 30 minutes, then hourly for 24-48 hours, then TDS for 7 days.

Plus systemic treatment if severe disease (e.g. deep stromal lesions, threatened perforation, endophthalmitis), and in All immunocompromised patients.
 


Candida

Fluconazole* 50-100mg OD PO for 7-14 days (if PO route unavailable, IV doses are equivalent)



Resistant Candida, or Aspergillus

Voriconazole* 400mg BD PO for 2 doses, then 200mg BD PO thereafter, OR 6mg/kg BD IV for 2 doses, then 4mg/kg BD IV thereafter



* NB Systemic antifungals are associated with significant side effects, including renal dysfunction (voriconazole), hepatotoxicity (fluconazole, voriconazole), and blood disorders (voriconazole). Monitoring should include FBC, urea and electrolytes (U+E), and liver function tests (LFTs), prior to starting treatment and at least weekly during treatment. In addition, dosing may need to be reduced in the presence of renal dysfunction

name: Abdominal Radiograph (AXR)
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook clinical support requesting and interpreting radiographs abdominal radiograph (axr) abdominal radiograph (axr) requesting and interpreting an abdominal radiograph (axr)
introduction
this section gives some guidance on the indications for requesting an abdominal radiograph (axr) and how to interpret them.
it is important to remember that all test results should be interpreted in the clinical context.
normal test results do not always rule out pathology - do not be falsely reassured.
any abnormal or unexpected test results should be escalated to seniors.
any test result you don't fully understand should be escalated to seniors. requesting an axr see under the radiology tab in my hospital life here for your responsibilities regarding the exposure of patients to ionising radiation under ir(me)r rules.
it is also your responsibility to ensure that all request forms are fully completed - again, there is guidance under the radiology tab.
remember that the request form is a legal document. it must be legible and include your name, designation as well as contact details.
on the form, be sure to ask the appropriate clinical question. the more information you give on the form, the more likely the radiographer will perform the optimum views to answer your question.
make sure that you understand the reason for requesting the radiograph and how it will change or influence management. there are very few indications for an abdominal film. if you are writing the form you need to be sure that the radiation exposure is justified. if you are not sure, ask your seniors before requesting it.
if you are not sure, ask your seniors before requesting it.
remember, the radiation exposure from an axr is approximately 50 times that of a cxr. if you are worried about obstruction and/or perforated viscus, you should also request an erect cxr to look for air under the diaphragm. the patient will need to be well enough to sit up for 5–10 minutes.
if you are concerned about a surgical cause for abdominal pain, it may be more appropriate to get a senior surgical opinion early.
following review, the preferred imaging modality may be a ct or ultrasound scan
a non-diagnostic axr exposes the patient to unnecessary radiation and may delay definitive care. interpreting an axr
this guidance provides a systematic way of reviewing radiographs to minimise the risk of missing abnormalities especially when you are tired!
you will soon develop your own method but this is good to get you started and to refer to when you are unsure.
get into the habit of checking the following on all radiographs before you start looking for clinical clues. patient name/ and nhs number – is it the correct patient?
date – is it the most recently requested image or an older one?
correct image – has the correct view and/or side that was requested been imaged.
type of image taken – is it pa or ap, supine or lateral - this may be important to interpretation
are there older images available that may be useful for comparison to optimise your interpretation, view the radiograph on the highest resolution screen possible. rotate your device to view the following table. adequacy the standard axr is taken supine. the radiograph should include the diaphragm and extend to the hernia orifices and should include the lateral fat stripe breathing if lung bases are visible – is there consolidation? (referred pain to abdomen) bones check spinal alignment and any other bones that are visible bowel bowel patterns small bowel dilatation central
valvulae conniventes complete bands.
abnormal if > 3 cm in diameter sentinel loops small length of bowel that dilates because of extrinsic inflammation e.g. pancreatitis large bowel dilatation periphery of abdomen
haustra – partial bands
abnormal if > 6 cm for large bowel or > 9 cm for caecum & sigmoid volvulus twisting of bowel on mesentery
sigmoid “coffee bean” shape
caecal “whirl” shape risk perforation if > 12 cm mucosal thickening thumb printing pattern on bowel wall rigler’s sign air on both sides of the bowel wall always check for free air calcification calcification of various structures in the abdomen can indicate pathology which may or may not be relevant to the presenting complaint calcified blood vessels a calcified aaa is often diagnosed incidentally on an abdominal film renal stones - 90% are radiopaque and may be seen references & elearning plain abdominal x-ray. an article by dr roger henderson, last reviewed february 2016, hosted on patient.info, available here. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 abdominal radiograph (axr) requesting and interpreting an abdominal radiograph (axr)

name: Treatment
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment respiratory exacerbation of copd treatment treatment  Doxycycline 200mg OD PO on day 1, then 100mg OD PO usually for a total of 5 days. 
  
If exacerbation is particularly severe, may be increased at discretion of senior to 7 days duration.

 Patients may also require corticosteroids – dose and course length as per respiratory physicians. (Usually prednisolone 30mg once daily for 5-7 days) 
  
 
  NB. Ensure emergency steroids and antibiotics for self-start at home are replenished via TTAs at discharge or have been ordered via the GP to collect at home Pharmacy if patients have used own supply prior to admission

name: CCU (Acute Cardiology Unit)
searchField: ccu (acute cardiology unit) external referrals ccu,coronary care unit,acute cardiology unit,stemi,mi,pci ccu (acute cardiology unit) please discuss all external referrals with a senior prior to referring. exeter also uses epic. initial referrals to exeter specialties are still by phone but epic message and uploading clinical images is useful for further discussion reason for referral: 1. stemi (st elevation myocardial infarction) 2. acs (acute coronary syndrome) with ongoing pain despite adequate treatment 3. patient needs urgent pacing before referral: • ensure patient has ecgs loaded onto epic as rd&e can see this • you may be put through to the nurse in charge (nic) of ccu, not a doctor so remember to clarify this when you call referral process: • call direct to ccu in rd&e – number available via index sheets printed and placed around ed • often if you speak to the nic rather than the consultant they will call you back with a decision on transfer. ensure you get a time frame for this and call back if you have not had a response • if they want a number to call you back on, it is better to give the hub number. guidance review committee/group guidance review date eolas page date created & editor number 25/5/25, jb, 009 eolas page date last checked & editor number 25/5/25, jb, 009

name: Wernicke's Encephalopathy & Hepatic Encephalopathy
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services alcohol, smoking & illicit drugs alcohol withdrawal wernicke's encephalopathy & hepatic encephalopathy wernicke's encephalopathy & hepatic encephalopathy wernicke's encephalopathy & hepatic encephalopathy wernicke's encephalopathy & hepatic encephalopathy guidance available on hub search "alcohol withdrawal" wernicke's encephalopathy (we) when inappropriately managed, wernicke's encephalopathy (we) carries a mortality rate of over 15% and results in permanent brain damage (korsakoff`s psychosis) in 85% of survivors.
the classical triad of signs acute confusion,
ataxia,
ophthalmoplegia only occurs in 10% of patients.
therefore, the triad cannot be used as the basis of diagnosis and a high index of suspicion is needed.
the presence of only one of the signs should be sufficient to assign a diagnosis and commence treatment.
hepatic encephalopathy
it is very important to distinguish patients with hepatic encephalopathy from those with alcohol withdrawal.
care should be taken in using any form of sedation in patients with hepatic encephalopathy as this can lead to rapid onset of coma and respiratory compromise.
check guidance and bnf for further advice. last checked 23/2/24 review date wernicke's encephalopathy & hepatic encephalopathy wernicke's encephalopathy & hepatic encephalopathy

name: Antibiotic Guidelines
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment skin and soft tissue animal and human bites antibiotic guidelines antibiotic guidelines only patients fitting these criteria should be offered the antibiotics listed below, see “diagnosis and things to watch out for…” and “always remember to..” sections for further advice: treat for 5 days unwell patient, or obviously infected wound from human, cat, dog or traditional pet or farm animal (excluding pigs) bite - e.g. increased pain, inflammation, fever, lymphangitis, discharge or an unpleasant smell. nb. if cardiovascular compromise, take blood cultures and wound swabs prior to antibiotics
offer antibiotic prophylaxis 3 days for cat or human bite with broken skin if any of: wound has drawn blood
high-risk area: hands, feet, face, genitals, skin overlying cartilaginous structures or area of poor circulation (even if no bleeding)
serious infection risk due to co-morbidities - diabetes, immunosuppression, asplenia, decompensated liver disease (even if no bleeding).
wound could be deep offer antibiotic prophylaxis 3 days for dog or other “traditional” pet or farm animal (excluding cats and pigs) which has broken the skin and drawn blood only if any of: high-risk area: hands, feet, face, genitals, skin overlying cartilaginous structures or an area of poor circulation
serious infection risk due to co-morbidities - diabetes, immunosuppression, asplenia, decompensated liver disease
penetration of bone, joint, tendon or vascular structures
deep, puncture or crush wound, or has caused significant tissue damage

name: Intensive Care Empirical Treatment Guidance
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines intensive care unit intensive care empirical treatment guidance intensive care empirical treatment guidance intensive care unit prescribing guidance take appropriate samples, before antimicrobial therapy is commenced.
check mycare and recent culture results e.g. wound cultures, mrsa screens, history of esbl coliforms, before prescribing antimicrobials.
antibiotic treatment must be reviewed in light of significant cultures and targeted appropriately.
severe penicillin allergy (type i hypersensitivity): symptoms occur within 72hrs of administration: pruritus; flushing; urticaria (hives); angioedema; laryngeal oedema; bronchospasm; hypotension. for non-severe infections avoid all penicillins, cephalosporins and carbapenems. in life threatening infections if use of a non-beta-lactam antibiotic is suboptimal seek senior advice. idiopathic reactions to antibiotics e.g. stevens-johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis or other exfoliating dermatoses: do not give a related antibiotic again (e.g. any beta-lactam antibiotic, including aztreonam, after reaction to penicillins) because re-exposure can trigger recurrence. patients with erythema multiforme minor or drug fever are also usually managed with avoidance.
doses are for guidance only and apply to adults of average size with normal renal and hepatic function.

name: SOP for Nurseless Transfer of Patients from ED to SAU
searchField:
hospital guidelines emergency medicine management sop for nurseless transfer of patients from ed to sau sop for nurseless transfer of patients from ed to sau nurseless transfer of patients from ed to abbey ward/sau standard operating procedure introduction
it is acknowledged that nurse-accompanied transfers from the emergency department to the surgical assessment unit/abbey ward are time consuming and labour intensive. purpose
the purpose of this standard operating procedure is to provide staff in the emergency department and abbey/esac guidance as to the expectations when transferring a patient to abbey/esac without a nurse escort.
exclusions patients who have an infusion of medication
patients who require continuous monitoring
patients with a news score of above 3
patients who might cause harm to themselves or others during the transfer process
any patient deemed on clinical grounds, would benefit from a nurse led transfer
patients with uncontrolled epistaxis
the list above is not exhaustive and clinical judgement must also be applied process the patient will be referred to general surgery or appropriate speciality by the usual referral processes on my care (epic) and via a bleep to the sho.
the nurse in charge of the area (e.g. ham, lam) will identify whether the patient fits the criteria for a nurse less transfer
ed nic to contact abbey nic prior to transfer to ensure space available for patient
any specific requirements of the patient, for example, side room, infection control, learning disabilities should be flagged as early as possible using the tools available on my care admission order.
if the patient is suitable for nurse less transfer then the nurse in charge to decide whether the patient needs to be transferred on a bed or trolley or would be suitable for transfer in a chair.
if the patient is on a bed, two support workers are required to transfer the patient to sau/abbey. if the patient can be transferred on a trolley or a chair then single support worker transfer is appropriate. the patients care must not be compromised by moving them onto a chair in order to have single support worker transfer.
if the support workers have concerns about moving a specific patient they should flag this to the nurse in charge for discussion and mitigation as required.
there is no handover required as the staff on sau/abbey will access the information directly from epic. please ensure appropriate nursing and mar documentation is completed before transfer. governance author of clinical guideline clinical project manager ed redesign division/ department responsible for clinical guideline emergency department - medical division abbey ward/esac - surgical division guideline reviewed by approving body and date approved ed governance, april 2024 expiry date april 2027 sop for nurseless transfer of patients from ed to sau nurseless transfer of patients from ed to abbey ward/sau

name: First Seizure
searchField:
hospital guidelines emergency medicine medicine neurology first seizure draft first seizure first seizure patients who are suspected to have had a first seizure and are aged ≥18 should be referred to the 'first fit clinic'. exclusion criteria: patients with alcohol withdrawal seizures
pregnancy
patients presenting from out of area investigations all patients should have the investigations in the 'collapse/seizure' order set on epic. the ecg must have been reviewed by an ed senior to exclude prolonged qt. patients with any of the following features should be referred for an acute cth: new focal neurological deficit
persistent altered mental status
fever or persistent headache
recent head trauma
history of cancer or hiv infection
patients whose follow-up cannot be ensured
anticoagulation or increased risk of bleeding
past history of stroke or tia discharge/follow-up admit those who have abnormal cth, persistent neurology or remain post-ictal. otherwise discharge patients with referral to the 'first fit clinic' - go to 'dispo' and click on 'common op clinic referrals from ed' and then 'neurology - first fit clinic'. ask patients to bring a relative along to their clinic appointment if they witnessed the seizure. if they are unable to make it to the appointment ask the witness to write a written account to send with the patient. discharge patients with the 'patient advice following a presumed first seizure' leaflet. advise them that they cannot drive (usually for 6 months after a first seizure) and that they must inform the dvla. the contact details for the dvla are on the advice leaflet. also advise them that they should not do any activities that may put them in danger (e.g. going swimming alone, having a bath, using power tools, climbing ladders). governance author of clinical guideline neurology consultant division/ department responsible for clinical guideline emergency department guideline reviewed by neurology consultant approving body and date approved expiry date first seizure first seizure patients who are suspected to have had a first seizure and are aged ≥18 should be referred to the 'first fit clinic'. exclusion criteria: patients with alcohol withdrawal seizures

name: Overtime
searchField:
overtime all about my pay overtime overtime
all full-time members of staff on agenda for change contracts (who are contracted 37.5 hours per week) will automatically be entitled to overtime (time and a half) rate of pay.
please note that if worked during an unsocial period the staffs are entitled to receive the enhancement due too.
to know if you are eligible for overtime or if you want to book a shift, please contact your line manager.
the overtime shifts are booked through the trust rostering system and can be booked by authorised staff.
unsocial hours
unsocial hours payments are additions to basic pay. these apply for staff whose work in standard hours, within the normal 37.5 hour working week, is undertaken at the times, and on the days, specified in the national agreement.
unsocial hours payments are worked out using basic salary including long-term recruitment and retention premiums (rrps), but excluding short-term rrps, high cost area supplements, and all other supplements and payments.
this system of payment is used to pay for unsocial hours worked in the previous month (retrospectively) when shifts and payments vary from month to month or, when shifts are fixed, prospectively, so that a fixed level of payment is made each month based on a forecast of the number of unsocial hours which will be worked in future months.
find out more by searching the hub 'unsocial hours enhancements'.
nhs professionals (nhsp)
looking for extra income? you can join nhs professionals (nhsp) and book shifts to suit you.
they deliver bespoke recruitment solutions to bank members and trusts, which can include local shift cover at short notice.
to join nhsp, please find more details on the hub by searching ‘nursing, midwifery, administrative and clerical staff bank’.
benefits
if you are a new member joining the nhsp bank, you will have access to some fantastic perks.
the bank offers lots of benefits, such as: competitive pay rates
first choice of shifts
flexible working to suit your lifestyle.
take control of when you want to work.
work this week, get paid next week.
build holiday allowance for every shift worked.
book and manage shifts online.
support when you need it 24/7 365 days/ call on 03332 407 552
stakeholder pension scheme available nhs professionals at the eastern services
opening hours: 0800-1700
you can contact by emailing rduhct@nhsprofessionals.nhs.uk or by calling 0333 014 3655.
please note that you may need to be signed off by your line manager to join nhsp. microguide page created 10/07/2024 added to handbook north 23/02/2025

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment urinary tract infection see ukpas guidance - section archived 1st aug 2025 [probable cystitis] diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials typically, patients are: systemically well
dysuria, frequency, urgency, incontinence reported
no loin pain
temperature less than 38oc, low grade fever
age over 3 months in younger patients, it can be difficult to differentiate between cystitis and pyelonephritis. features more suggestive of deeper infection include: fever
malaise
vomiting
poor feeding
irritability
loin pain
renal tenderness on examination > if present, follow "probable pyelonephritis" guideline initially until upper uti ruled out table with other diagnostic signs and symptoms for any uti (including pyelonephritis) age group symptoms and signs most common ———-> least common infants younger than 3 months fever vomiting lethargy irritability poor feeding failure to thrive abdominal pain jaundice haematuria offensive urine infants and children, 3 months or older preverbal fever abdominal pain loin tenderness vomiting poor feeding lethargy irritability haematuria offensive urine failure to thrive verbal frequency dysuria dysfunctional voiding changes to continence abdominal pain loin tenderness fever malaise vomiting haematuria offensive urine cloudy urine assess illness severity in infants and children using the “fever in under 5s” nice tool when making decisions around whether to admit for inpatient care (requires internet access): https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng143/resources/support-for-education-and-learning-educational-resource-traffic-light-table-pdf-6960664333 lower uti and cystitis can be associated with sexual activity. if history volunteered by underage child or their accompanying adult does not ring true, or signs of genital trauma on examination / catheter insertion discuss with senior team members regarding safeguarding on the ward and ensure appropriate follow-up if needed. additional microbiological samples may be required, discuss with microbiology / gum specialist if sexually transmitted infection suspected other types of uti which are not suitable for treatment using this guideline: atypical uti includes: seriously ill (see link above to nice guideline on fever in under 5s)
poor urine flow
abdominal or bladder mass
raised creatinine
septicaemia
failure to respond to treatment with suitable antibiotics within 48 hours
infection with non-e. coli organisms recurrent uti: 2 or more episodes of uti with acute pyelonephritis/upper urinary tract infection, or
1 episode of uti with acute pyelonephritis/upper urinary tract infection plus one or more episode of uti with cystitis/lower urinary tract infection, or
3 or more episodes of uti with cystitis/lower urinary tract infection sexually transmitted infections

name: Ongoing antimicrobial therapy
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial antimicrobial prescribing policy policy ongoing antimicrobial therapy ongoing antimicrobial therapy 
Review:
The ongoing need for antimicrobials must be reviewed and documented at every clinical assessment of the patient. 
Senior clinicians must be involved in the review process (ST3 or above). 
In general daily review is expected, but certainly within the first 48 hours of initiating antibiotic treatment (or 72 hours in exceptional circumstances, which must be documented in the patient clinical record). 
If antibiotics continue, the reason for continuation must be clearly documented in the patient clinical record.  
MyCare electronic prescription record will prompt review at 48 hours with a pop-up.
It is recognised that there is sometimes diagnostic doubt when patients attend acute care, and also that there is a need to start antimicrobial therapy promptly to avoid deterioration in the unwell patient.

If the initial “possible” or “probable” diagnosis is revised and infection is consequently felt unlikely, antimicrobial therapy should be discontinued, as per the principles of ARK.

There is no need to continue the antimicrobial to ‘complete a course’.

Intravenous antimicrobials must be reviewed daily. The reason to continue / discontinue or switch to an oral antimicrobial agent must be clearly documented in the patient clinical record.

When switching to the oral route, follow the guidance above to guide selection of appropriate agent(s). The ACED acronym may be used:

Afebrile >24 hours
 Clinically well

 Eating

 Drinking

 
When culture results and sensitivities are received, antibiotics are to be reviewed and treatment modified according to microbiological sensitivities, or where empirical treatment has been initiated, broad spectrum antibiotics are de-escalated to narrow spectrum agents where possible.
 

Clinical pharmacists in inpatient and outpatient settings must support prudent prescribing/use of antimicrobials by:


Reviewing antimicrobial prescriptions for appropriateness and guideline compliance – any concerns must be raised urgently with the clinicians caring for the patient, or where necessary escalated to the Antimicrobial Pharmacists or the Medical Microbiologists.
Reviewing duration and indication on the MAR for every antimicrobial prescription. If the duration and indication are not documented/correct on the medicine chart or in the notes, contact the prescriber and request this information and then endorse the MAR accordingly. Inform the prescriber that the national standard is to include a stop/review date or duration and indication every time an order for an antimicrobial agent is made. If the prescriber is unavailable, write in the notes requesting a stop/review date or duration and indication be written on the MAR and in the medical notes.

a member of the team may be able to review patients on the ward and there will always be a doctor or specialist nurse who can give advice out of hours is a telephone only advice service which is covered by clinicians from across the south-west peninsula region.
the out of hours hiv service runs at all times (including bank holidays) except monday – friday 9am-5pm
the rota and all relevant contact numbers are held by the plymouth hospitals nhs trust main switchboard and can be obtained by calling 01752 202082. please consult out of hours in the following situations: a newly diagnosed hiv positive patient
any admission thought to be related to the underlying hiv and/or treatment
any concerns about potential drug interaction(s) with anti-retrovirals the out of hours service is not for use in the following situations: any situation not involving an hiv positive patient

name: Antifungal Prophylaxis
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines haematology/oncology antifungal prophylaxis antifungal prophylaxis antifungal prophylaxis in adults prophylaxis for oral candidiasis this is only for patients not receiving systemic antifungal prophylaxis (see below) nystatin liquid only, starting from the first day that neuts <1.0 x109/l and stopping on first day neuts >1.0 or after 2 days of neut >0.5 x109/l there is no need to use more than one agent. systemic antifungal prophylaxis systemic antifungal prophylaxis should be given according to the ecil guidelines table below. systemic antifungals in this context means: posaconazole tablets 300mg twice daily on first day, then 300mg once daily this should
a) start on the day of chemotherapy
b) stop when neutrophils>0.5 x109/l for two consecutive days or neutrophils >1.0x 109/l, whichever is the sooner
c) not be interrupted during this period if patients cannot swallow tablets then posaconazole liquid can be used instead (dose is not equivalent); consideration should be given to checking of levels - target for prophylaxis is >0.7 mg/l at steady state or 0.35 mg/l 48 h after initiation of therapy - measurement of trough serum concentration 7 days after initiation of therapy and following dose adjustment. warning:
patients receiving vinca alkaloids (e.g. vincristine or vinblastine) are an exception and should not receive posaconazole in the 2 days following or the 5 days preceeding vinca alkaloid administration systemic antifungal prophylaxis is not indicated for any other patient; where the clinician feels antifungal prophylaxis is required outside of this guideline, oral itraconazole liquid should be used, at a dose of 200mg daily. consider monitoring dose with trough level measurements: target trough concentration for prophylaxis is 0.5 mg/l; measurement of trough serum concentrations 5–7 days after initiation of therapy or dose adjustment.[1] [1] therapeutic drug monitoring (tdm) of antifungal agents: guidelines from the british society for medical mycology ecil guidelines table antifungal prophylaxis antifungal prophylaxis in adults prophylaxis for oral candidiasis this is only for patients not receiving systemic antifungal prophylaxis (see below) nystatin liquid only, starting from the first day that neuts <1.0 x109/l and stopping on first day neuts >1.0 or after 2 days of neut >0.5 x109/l there is no need to use more than one agent. systemic antifungal prophylaxis systemic antifungal prophylaxis should be given according to the ecil guidelines table below. systemic antifungals in this context means: posaconazole tablets 300mg twice daily on first day, then 300mg once daily this should

this section includes information on the im&t service desk
the digital technology office
nhs email getting an nhs.net email account
registering your nhs email account with ndht the im&t service desk the easiest way to contact the im&t service desk is electronically - access the it portal icon from the help & support tab on the zenworks window, log on (using your usual user name and password) and click on the new request option. use this option for new requests and non urgent incidents
for tracking, updating or closing logged requests
for logging requests outside of core im&t service desk hours (your request will be picked up the following day). alternatively, if you would prefer to speak to someone, you can call the im&t service desk on 0300 123 1722 between 0830–1700 monday–friday.
requests for urgent support out of office hours should go via the site manager (bleep 500) or the on-call hospital manager. digital technology office the digital technology office (dto) supports clinical and administration functions across the trust by providing it equipment and services to staff. they are also responsible for the trust's infrastructure (data-communications network and main computer systems), personal computer systems and peripheral equipment, information analysis services, information governance and clinical coding.
the dto provides it help, advice and support, primarily through the service desk, see the tab above for more information and contact details.
nhs email

getting an nhs.net email account
contact the im&t service desk and request an account - see above. first time accounts cannot be set up from the nhs mail or portal websites. registering your nhs email account with ndht this section is relevant if you already have an nhs.net email account registered to another organisation. to change your registration to ndht open & log into your nhs.net account using the nhs mail icon on the web links tab of the zenworks window on any trust computer.
directly by clicking here.
select profile at the top and choose the option for self service. nominate yourself as a leaver.
log in to the it portal via the icon on the help & support tab of the zenworks window on any trust computer (using your usual user name and password).

name: Football
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook outdoor activities football football football 

   Football
  
Introduction
The football season in the UK runs from August to May each year. Playing football is very popular in the UK, as is supporting a team.
The Devon County Football Association are committed to governing and developing the game at all levels, see their website HERE.
Professional Football
The local professional team is Exeter City Football Club, and they play in the English Football League One. The club is owned and run by the Exeter City Supporters' Trust, and their website is HERE.
Playing Football
For Men Click HERE for a list of all football teams that play in the Devon and Exeter Football league, click HERE.
For Youth Players The Exeter Panthers have teams for all age groups up to 16, see HERE.

name: FRCA Exam Structure
searchField:
hospital guidelines anaesthetics and intensive care anaesthetics at nddh for anaesthetic trainees frca (fellow of the royal college of anaesthetists) examination frca exam structure frca exam structure the fellowship of the royal college of anaesthetists (frca) examination introduction
information on the frca from the royal college of anaesthetists website is available here.
for learning opportunities and courses aimed at the frca click here.
primary frca
the first part of the primary frca exam is a multiple choice (mcq) and single best answer (sba) paper. sittings are in march, september and november.
september is a sensible time for your first attempt – only enter for the march sitting if you are going to start revising almost straight away. it is possible but there is a lot to get through so don’t underestimate it!
the primary part of the fellowship of the frca examination comprises three separate summative examinations a multiple choice (mcq) examination - click here for more information including changes to the examination due in september 2023.
an objective structured clinical examination (osce) - see here. a structured oral examination (soe) - see here. the rcoa has produced a number of videos on the primary frca available here. to achieve an overall pass in the frca primary, a candidate must pass all three components. a pass in the primary frca is valid for seven years as part eligibility towards the final frca examinations.
exam resources at ndht
in addition to the resources held in the library, there is also a collection of mcq, soe, and osce books in the anaesthetic department, and learning materials on the g-drive in anaesthetics non-confidential - trainee learning materials. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 22/08/2024 frca exam structure the fellowship of the royal college of anaesthetists (frca) examination

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment genito-urinary urinary tract infection lower uti, no sepsis, catheterised diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Classic signs and symptoms of uncomplicated lower UTI in a patient who is otherwise systemically well:

Dysuria, frequency, urgency, urinary incontinence, changes in urine appearance or consistency (cloudy/turbid, haematuria), nocturia, suprapubic discomfort, low-grade fever, raised inflammatory markers.

 
Additional atypical signs and symptoms of lower UTI in frail or elderly patients who are otherwise being optimally managed and who are systemically well:
 

Delirium/acute behavioural change (depends on an accurate pre-morbid history), lethargy, resistance to care activities being carried out, reduced ability to independently carry out activities of daily living, falls, temperature 1.5ºC above normal twice in 12 hours, anorexia.


Patients who show 3 or more classic signs and symptoms (including at least 1 of the highlighted symptoms) are likely to have a lower UTI.
Patients who show 2 or less classic signs and symptoms may have a UTI, but more attention should be paid to possible differentials and in particular avoiding unnecessary antibiotic use
Differentials include:

Delirium: drug-induced, dehydration, disturbed sleep
Lower abdominal pathology: constipation, diverticular disease, appendicitis
Cystitis (inflammatory, non-infectious), caused by: 
    
Trauma due to genitourinary procedures incl. traumatic catheter insertion/removal, sexual intercourse, sexual abuse or physical activity
Adverse drug effects — some drugs such as cyclophosphamide, opioids, and nifedipine can cause urinary tract symptoms.
Irritant contact dermatitis
 
Thrush
Gender-specific: 
    
Women: Fibroids, atrophic vaginitis, lichen sclerosis, lichen planus, PID, cervicitis, gynaecological malignancy (for example ovarian cancer) may present with persistent or frequent increased urinary urgency and/or frequency.
Men: benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), acute prostatitis, epididymitis, testicular torsion
 
Urolithiasis
Urological malignancy
Other infections such as sexually transmitted infections (for example Chlamydia, Gonorrhoea, genital herpes simplex), intestinal worms

name: Foundation Year 1 ePortfolio Advice by Placement
searchField:
foundation year 1 eportfolio advice by placement foundation year 1 eportfolio advice by placement eportfolio foundation year 1 eportfolio advice by placement
this section contains important information on completing the eportfolio during the foundation year 1 and what you must do in order to successfully complete the year.
eportfolio linking- link contemporaneously throughout each placement
there are 13 professional capabilities – you can link a maximum of ten items to each domain, although linking three to five is recommended.
avoid overlinking – too much and the excellent work gets lost.
each piece of evidence can be used only three times.
professional capability number 2 (clinical prioritisation) requires that you upload evidence of completion of an als or ils course along with evidence of direct clinical involvement.
ensure your evidence reflects the physical, social, and mental health aspects of healthcare in both acute and community settings.
link your tab (team assessment of behaviour) when complete.
do not link anything which has not been signed off – it won’t count. first placement
at the beginning induction meetings - you must meet with your educational supervisor (es) and your clinical supervisor (cs) (this may be the same person) ideally within the first two weeks of the rotation. complete your pdp at this meeting using the work schedule for the placement as a guide.
quality improvement (qi) project decide on which qi project you want to be involved in early as it will take longer than you think. developing the clinical teacher decide when you will complete this teaching session - either your es or cs must be present to assess and sign this off. sign declarations
upload your prescribing safety assessment (psa) certificate as soon as you have passed it. during this placement attend and log all mandatory f1 teaching as well as other specialty teaching opportunities that you attend.
upload five to ten pieces of evidence (including supervised learning events) throughout the placement and map them to the curriculum.
reflections – aim for at least three per placement.
carefully choose your best evidence to link to your curriculum - don't link everything. mid placement review
you should have mid placement reviews with both your cs and es. while the former should be in person, the latter could be online or via email. at your review you should review your progress to date, consider the evidence already uploaded, and discuss what else needs to be added.
agree the members of your placement supervision group (psg) to inform your end of placement report.
start a summary narrative to explain why the evidence you have chosen supports each higher level outcome (hlo). this is to be completed at the end of the f1 year. at the end of the placement ensure your summary narrative is up-to-date prior to your end of placement meetings.
ensure all teaching is logged and that your curriculum mapping is up to date.
have a meeting with your cs and an end of placement meeting with your es. if your cs and es are the same person, you need only have one meeting, but there should be two reports. second placement
at the beginning arrange a meeting with your clinical supervisor (cs).
arrange a meeting with your educational supervisor (es). this meeting could be at the same time as your es end of placement meeting for your first placement, but needs to be recorded separately.
complete your pdp using the work schedule for the placement as a guide during this placement attend and log mandatory f1 teaching as well as other specialty teaching opportunities that you attend.
upload five to ten pieces of evidence (including supervised learning events) throughout the placement and map them to the curriculum.
reflections – aim for at least three per placement.
carefully choose your best evidence to link to your curriculum - don't link everything.
ensure that you have a plan to complete a teaching session for the developing the clinical teacher assessment.
ensure that you will complete your qi project before the end of this placement. team assessment of behaviour (tab)
the tab is a multi-source feedback tool used to collect views from your colleagues. you must complete one tab during the year. the tab window opens on the 1st october and closes on the 31st january so be sure that you have started your tab within this window.
mid placement review
you should have mid placement reviews with both your cs and es. while the former should be in person, the latter could be online or via email. at your review you should review your progress to date, consider the evidence already uploaded and discuss what else needs to be added.
agree the members of your placement supervision group (psg) to inform your end of placement report.
start a summary narrative to explain why the evidence you have chosen supports each higher level outcome (hlo). this is to be completed at the end of the f1 year. at the end of the placement ensure your summary narrative is up-to-date prior to your end of placement meetings.
ensure all teaching is logged and that curriculum mapping is up to date.
have a meeting with your cs and an end of placement meeting with your es. if your cs and es are the same person, you need only have one meeting, but there should be two reports. final placement
important the deadline for evidence for the annual review of competence progression (arcp) is end of may, so all assessments for the entire year must be completed by the end of may, and
all end of placement and end of year meetings must be completed before the end of may. at the beginning arrange a meeting with your clinical supervisor (cs).
arrange a meeting with your educational supervisor (es). this meeting could be at the same time as your es end of placement meeting for your first placement, but needs to be recorded separately.
complete your pdp using the work schedule for the placement as a guide. during this placement - to be completed prior to the end of may attend and log mandatory f1 teaching as well as other specialty teaching opportunities that you attend.
upload five to ten pieces of evidence (including supervised learning events) throughout the placement and map them to the curriculum.
reflections – aim for at least three per placement. before the end of may finish your curriculum mapping and carefully review all the evidence you have linked to your curriculum - don't link everything.
complete and fine tune your summary narrative.
ensure that you have completed an observed teaching session for the developing the clinical teacher assessment.
ensure that you have completed your qi project.
complete your personal learning log - this requires you to collect evidence of your attendance at mandatory teaching from the mec in may. arrange your clinical supervisor end of placement meeting in good time.
arrange your educational supervisor end of year meeting in good time.
form r – complete this ahead of your arcp. see the advice on completing form r in the next section here. microguide page created 2021 microguide page content updated june 2024 microguide page last checked 28/08/2024 eolas medical first publish layout/links checked 12/09/2024

name: Requesting Imaging on Epic
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life departments - clinical radiology requesting imaging requesting imaging on epic requesting imaging on epic requesting imaging via epic
introduction
all doctors and referrers for procedures that involve ionising radiation must be able to justify the planned exposure and know their obligation and roles under the ionising radiation (medical exposures) regulations 2017. for more information, click here.
a list of imaging modalities available at nddh can be seen here.
click here for information on inpatient imaging requesting and reporting, both in and out of hours.
requesting imaging via epic
all imaging requests (except on meadow view) should be made using epic via the 'orders' function and must include the following referrer information - your name, designation and contact details so the liaison radiologist or radiographer can contact a doctor who knows the patient if further discussion is required.
clinical information - you should give as much clinical information as is necessary to justify the request - there are 256 characters available to you! please include the reason for requesting the imaging and the clinical question you want answering
details of previous diagnostic examinations and/or medical records relevant to the medical exposure requested. include a recent creatinine or egfr (estimated glomerular filtration rate) if intravenous contrast may be given. if you are unsure of the most appropriate imaging for the patient, please discuss with the liaison radiologist or radiographer patient details for follow-up and out-patient referrals, please include the patient’s telephone number.
where the patient’s identity is unknown, standard trust identification procedures must be followed. the trust's patient identification policy (review date oct 2020) can be accessed via the hub. timing - choose the correct option based on the appropriate timing of the imaging being requested. send to radiology now - for imaging that needs to be done now
walk in pre-clinic today - for imaging prior to their clinic appointment later today
standalone future imaging request - to be used for non-same day appointments
walk in pre-clinic - to be used for future dates when the patient will be seen at their next clinic appointment and imaging is needed prior to clinic all imaging requests for scans and radiographs will be vetted, based on the supporting clinical information given, by the liaison radiologist and the liaison radiographer, respectively. without adequate information, the request may be rejected or sent back to the referrer for more details, delaying the patient's imaging. when completing the request, remember that the radiologists and radiographers do not have routine access to all of the other patient information on epic, so it is important to include all relevant clinical information.
important - out of hours, a request on epic will not be looked at by a radiologist until the next working day unless phoned through. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 requesting imaging on epic requesting imaging via epic

name: Style Guide & Editing Rules
searchField:
style guide & editing rules style guide and editing rules style guide editing rules editing guidance / style guide
only in date guidance is to be added
content added to this platform should only be guidance that has been approved through the specialty governance/divisional governance/clinical effectiveness committee depending upon how many specialties are involved in the approval. the formal trust document on the template with full frontsheet should be uploaded to the hub as part of the business continuity plan. this aspect will be audited
do not add documents to other specialties areas - share the document via email with the other specialty's governance lead only registered admins are permitted to upload contents - each admin/editor will have a unique number assigned which they can use in the governance box at the end of the guidance page.
admins are not to add other admins as a central record of admins and assigned editing numbers needs to be kept.
review date for the guidance document should be added to each piece of guidance to set up email alerts. suggest alert date is 3 months prior to actual guidance review date to allow time to for the review to take place. each specialty is responsible for maintaining their guidance on both hub and eolas and ensuring it is up to date - remember other specialties may link to your guidance. summary of changes prior to publication - please add a brief summary as this will help when going back through version histories. style guide use eolas editor
use the template below with the governance box
internal eolas links use bold here
external links use bold green here
using "here" for links makes them easier to search for to check they are working
no names only job titles check any mobile numbers with the owner prior to adding to this platform - assume the general public/press can read anything that appears on this app
font size 12 for text font size 10 in tables font nunito (some legacy pages will have helvetica or arial these will be updated as they are reviewed)
abbreviations all abbrevations must be explained especailly to assist overseas colleagues (sti - sexual transmitted infection or soft tissue injury!)
avoid too many capitals, bold, red as these require more effort to read and will lose impact.
images will look better if uploaded as a picture rather than a screenshot or cut and paste.
there isn't a spellcheck function so suggest checking on original word document prior to cut and paste over.
front sheet can be included at the end of your guidance but ensure all names, email addresses and phone numbers are removed.
references should be added as they appear on the trust guidance document and ideally vancouver style e.g. 1. lokelma spc via emc website. https://www.medicines.org.uk/emc/product/10074/smpc accessed 21/11/19 finish each section with a solid line below which the governance box should appear - insert then choose horizontal line guidance review committee/group guidance review date eolas page date created & editor unique number eolas page date last checked & editor unique number

name: Electronic Staff Record (ESR)
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my royal devon life (eastern services) all about my pay electronic staff record (esr) electronic staff record (esr) 

   Electronic Staff Record (ESR)
  
The Electronic Staff Record (ESR) is an integrated Human Resources (HR) and payroll system for NHS organisations in England and Wales. Personal information, annual leave, sickness, professional development reviews (PDRs), ending employment function can all be managed from here.
ESR allows NHS employees to view and maintain their personal information and develop their professional knowledge. Royal Devon staff receive their payslips via ESR, and hence they should also be able to access their Total Reward Statement via ESR.
To login to ESR please click HERE. Please note one can request username/password by clicking the ‘request username/password’ on the ‘login to ESR’ tab on the above-mentioned website
My ESR Mobile App can also be download on Google play and Apple store.

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment cns encephalitis diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Viruses are the most common cause of encephalitis; however, consider combining with treatment for bacterial meningitis pending CSF results.
Consider encephalitis if any of: 
  


Altered conscious state
Behavioural change
Seizures
Meningeal signs/headache may be present
Fever history and a prodromal illness of a few days (present in most patients with HSV)
 

Note: Bacterial meningitis may present atypically with confusion and an absence of meningeal signsConsider non-infectious aetiologies, including ADEM
Consider: Syphilis and Lyme disease (encephalitis may be an early feature shortly after erythema)
Consider Rickettsial disease in patients with history of travel to endemic areas. Discuss with microbiology and consider starting doxycycline.

Travel-associated viruses include: Japanese encephalitis, West Nile viruses, Tick-borne encephalitis, rabies.
