name: Handover Checklist
searchField:
hospital guidelines obstetrics & gynaecology induction handover list handover checklist handover checklist handover checklist handovers take place at 0900 and 2100 every day on delivery suite.
the night junior doctor prepares the 0900 handover sheet.
the long day junior doctor prepares the 2100 handover sheet
checklist is the handover list up-to-date including bed/ward swaps?
are any inpatients seriously unwell?
has every new admission or patient returning for uss been added to the handover list?
are there any outstanding jobs?
have the most recent blood results been added to the handover for inpatients?
are there any patients waiting to be seen in ed/aaa?
are the available epac slots at the bottom of the handover list up-to-date? things to include on the handover list
obstetric patients are an - antenatal, pn - postnatal or iol - induction of labour
include any scans and blood results.
include any elective caesarean sections (c/s) and any inductions for the following day.
include elective gynaecology patients that will arrive on kgv ward from recovery during the evening after theatre.
early pregnancy assessment clinic (epac) - time slots are at the bottom of the spreadsheet. keep this updated if you book anyone in, epac will let you know if they book anyone in so you can update the spreadsheet. this is to avoid double booking!
cannot stress this enough, obstetric patients seem to bed hop all the time. keep track of what is going on in labour ward and bassett ward so you do not have a mad rush before handover trying to work out where all your patients have been moved to. document review date committee approving microguide page created microguide page last checked 22/8/24 handover checklist handover checklist

name: Tax & National Insurance
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my royal devon life (eastern services) all about my pay tax & national insurance tax & national insurance 

   Tax & National Insurance 
  
 Tax and National Insurance are both taken out directly from your pay under the Pay as you Earn (PAYE) scheme. See the Understanding Your Payslip section in this app HERE to understand the deductions on your payslip. 

   Income Tax
  
Income Tax is a tax you pay on your income and funds different public services, including the NHS.You pay tax on

Money you earn from employment
Profits you make if you’re self-employed
Some state benefits
Most pensions, including state pensions, company and personal pensions and retirement annuities
Property rental income (unless you’re a live-in landlord and get less than the rent a room scheme limit)
Benefits you get from your job
Income from a trust fund
Interest on savings over your savings allowance

Income Tax rates and Personal AllowancesHow much Income Tax you pay in each tax year depends on:

How much of your income is above your Personal Allowance
How much of your income falls within each tax band

To find out the current Personal Tax Allowance, visit the Government website HERE.
Paying less Income TaxYou may be able to claim Income Tax reliefs if you are eligible for them click HERE. If you are married or in a civil partnership: You may be able to claim Marriage Allowance to reduce your partner’s tax if your income is less than the standard Personal Allowance. Click HERE.

   National Insurance
  
You pay National Insurance contributions to qualify for certain benefits and the State Pension. Find out more HERE. 
How much you payThe amount of National Insurance you pay depends on your employment status and how much you earn click HERE for information.
Your National Insurance numberYou have a National Insurance number to make sure your National Insurance contributions and tax are recorded against your name only. It’s made up of letters and numbers and never changes.
You can find your National Insurance number:

on your payslip
on your P60
on letters about your tax, pension or benefits
in the HMRC app, which you can download HEREin the National Insurance section of your personal tax account, which you can set up HERE
a UK biometric residence permit or card

Keeping your National Insurance number safeTo prevent identity fraud, do not share your National Insurance number with anyone who does not need it.
These organisations may need to know what your number is:

HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC)
your employer
the Department for Work and Pensions (which includes Jobcentre Plus and the Pension, Disability and Carers Service), if you claim state benefits, or in Northern Ireland the Department for Social Development
 your local council, if you claim Housing Benefit, or the Northern Ireland Housing Executive
Electoral Registration Officers (to check your identity when you register to vote)
the Student Loans Company, if you apply for a student loan
your pension provider if you have a personal or stakeholder pension
your Individual Savings Account (ISA) provider, if you open an ISA
authorised financial service providers who help you buy and sell investments like shares, bonds and derivatives - you can check if your provider is authorised HERE
Veterans UK

name: Sepsis Diagnosis
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines sepsis sepsis diagnosis sepsis diagnosis sepsis diagnosis sepsis diagnosis
see sepsis six screening tool for more information are any two of the following sirs criteria present and new to the patient? temperature <36 or >38.3oc
heart rate >90bpm respiratory rate >20/min
white cells >12 or <4 x 109 /l if yes, does your patient have a history or signs suggestive of a new infection? for example: cough/sputum/chest pain/new cxr infiltrates
abdo pain/distension/diarrhoea
line infection endocarditis dysuria headache with neck stiffness cellulitis/wound infection/septic arthritis if yes to both above, then patient has sepsis (sirs + infection = sepsis) start prompt effective antimicrobial treatment urgently and within one hour collect appropriate cultures before starting antibiotic therapy check mycare for infection control alerts & recent cultures/sensitivities results
review mycare for previous resistant organisms, and discuss urgently with microbiology if unsure.
review all patients on post-take round and/or within 24 hours with a consultant.
empiric antibiotics should be reviewed at 24-72 hours and a plan made for continuation, duration and oral switch, in light of investigation results and likely source of infection. can you identify a source of infection?
if you can identify the source of infeciton follow sepsis (with known site)
if you cannot identify a source of infection follow sepsis of unknown site
diagnostic criteria for severe sepsis (i.e. sepsis induced tissue hypoperfusion). for example are any of the following present and new to the patient? acute kidney injury (aki): creatinine >50% above baseline and/or urine output <0.5ml/kg/hour for 2 hrs hypotensive: systolic bp<90 or mean <65mmhg lactic acidosis: lactate > 2mmol/l bone marrow failure: platelets <100 x 109 /l hypoxia: new or increased need for o2 to keep spo2 >90% liver failure: bilirubin >34 µmol/l and/or inr >1.5 or aptt>60s sepsis diagnosis
see sepsis six screening tool for more information are any two of the following sirs criteria present and new to the patient? temperature <36 or >38.3oc
heart rate >90bpm respiratory rate >20/min
white cells >12 or <4 x 109 /l if yes, does your patient have a history or signs suggestive of a new infection?
for example: cough/sputum/chest pain/new cxr infiltrates
abdo pain/distension/diarrhoea
line infection endocarditis dysuria headache with neck stiffness cellulitis/wound infection/septic arthritis if yes to both above, then patient has sepsis (sirs + infection = sepsis) start prompt effective antimicrobial treatment urgently and within one hour collect appropriate cultures before starting antibiotic therapy check mycare for infection control alerts & recent cultures/sensitivities results
review mycare for previous resistant organisms, and discuss urgently with microbiology if unsure.
review all patients on post-take round and/or within 24 hours with a consultant.
empiric antibiotics should be reviewed at 24-72 hours and a plan made for continuation, duration and oral switch, in light of investigation results and likely source of infection. can you identify a source of infection?
if you cannot identify a source of infection follow sepsis of unknown site
diagnostic criteria for severe sepsis (i.e. sepsis induced tissue hypoperfusion). for example are any of the following present and new to the patient? acute kidney injury (aki): creatinine >50% above baseline and/or urine output <0.5ml/kg/hour for 2 hrs hypotensive: systolic bp<90 or mean <65mmhg lactic acidosis: lactate > 2mmol/l bone marrow failure: platelets <100 x 109 /l hypoxia: new or increased need for o2 to keep spo2 >90% liver failure: bilirubin >34 µmol/l and/or inr >1.5 or aptt>60s sepsis diagnosis

name: Odour absorbent dressings
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care choosing a wound dressing odour absorbent dressings odour absorbent dressings odour absorbent dressings 
Wound malodour may be a major concern for patients and their carers. 
Charcoal is often used as it is a powerful deodoriser.
Clinisorb
Clinisorb: sterile activated charcoal cloth. Exudate will reduce the dressing's effectiveness. 
Suitable for malodourous wounds such as fungating wounds, pressure ulcers, leg ulcers and diabetic foot ulcers.

this section includes information on the medical education centre (mec).
a list of regular teaching available at ndht. see also the section on the library in this guide here. medical education centre location - the medical education centre is on level 1
contact - reception telephone number 01271 311846 or ext 3846
email - ndht.meded@nhs.net
the medical education centre team offers a warm welcome to our doctors. our aim is to provide a high quality educational environment for you.
your first point of call will be our foyer. information on current hot topics is displayed and the reception desk provides users with a personal point of contact to help them locate what they need. an informal area in the foyer, with a vending machine, newspapers and pc’s with internet access, has been developed for your use.
the majority of the education programme takes place here in one of our five teaching rooms. all of the trust’s cancer mdt sessions have been moved into the medical education centre to allow more staff to access this as a regular educational session.
we have developed a clinical skills laboratory within the centre and have a wide variety of models and procedural trainers that can be used.
we have an experienced team in the centre who can provide a wide range of advice and information on all aspects of medical education. the mec team will also provide you with your induction programme, portfolio and appraisal and assessment paperwork to ensure that you settle in quickly and make the most of your stay with us.
the director of medical education and foundation programme training director are based here and doctors in training are welcome to call in to see them, although appointments may sometimes be necessary.

name: Prescribing Insulin
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies prescribing guidance northern services insulin prescribing sop prescribing insulin prescribing insulin prescribing insulin northern services' getting insulin right' standard operating procedure' (review date may 2024) on hub search "getting insulin right"
please note - it is now out of date use with caution and check with the bnf medicines reconciliation follow the medicines reconciliation standard operating procedure on hub
the patient summary care record (scr) may not be up to date and is unlikely to record the dose of insulin that the patient is currently taking.
confirm dose of insulin prior to admission with patient/carer, diabetes nurse records, recent discharges, insulin passport as per medicines reconciliation standard operating procedure
prescriber to review and decide if this dose is still appropriate, document in patient record any changes to be made and rationale prescribing insulin this information is out of date since epic - awaiting new images.
insulin should be prescribed in the specific insulin section of the drug chart, and a notation placed in the regular medication section to highlight this. only insulin should be prescribed in the insulin section, all other antidiabetic medication, including other injectables, must be prescribed in the main chart.
many insulin preparations have similar names. ensure that prescriptions are clear and precise.
always prescribe insulin by brand and write the full name of the insulin.
insulin now comes in multiple strengths and dose must be prescribed in units.
never abbreviate the word 'units', always write it out in full to avoid unintentional overdoses.
review insulin doses on a regular basis according to clinical need.
when changing a patient’s insulin requirement, generally alter one prescription at a time by approximately 10% of the dose. seek advice if unsure. be aware of the effect on blood sugars of other medicines and conditions e.g. steroids and follow relevant available guidelines.
care should be taken when rewriting drug charts that insulin is transcribed correctly.
all patients prescribed insulin should be referred to the diabetes specialist nurse team here.
this is the responsibility of the registered nurse looking after the patient.
for patients with insulin pumps please refer to 'insulin pumps' on hub. prescribing insulin prescribing insulin

name: Anaphylaxis, Perioperative
searchField:
hospital guidelines anaesthetics and intensive care a to z anaphylaxis, perioperative anaphylaxis, perioperative anaphylaxis, perioperative perioperative anaphylaxis introduction
the perioperative anaphylaxis plan for the north is available on the hub - search 'perioperative anaphylaxis'. this includes instructions and resources for arranging appropriate follow up of patients.
the following external documents may be of interest.
initial acute emergency management
click here for the association of anaesthetists guideline on the management of anaphylaxis, section 3-1 in the quick reference handbook.
nice guidance drug allergy and management
further guidance on the diagnosis and management of a drug allergy is available in the nice clinical guideline cg183 'drug allergy and management' and is available here. this guideline covers diagnosing and managing drug allergy in all age groups. it aims to make it easier for professionals to tell when someone is having an allergic reaction, by specifying the key signs and patterns to look out for. it also makes recommendations on improving people’s understanding of their drug allergies, and ensuring these are recorded properly in their medical records. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 25/08/2024 anaphylaxis,perioperative perioperative anaphylaxis

an aching body
a headache
a sore throat
a blocked or runny nose
loss of appetite
diarrhoea

name: General Interest and Other Points
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment respiratory community acquired pneumonia curb 0-1 general interest and other points general interest and other points cxr to diagnose pneumonia within 4 hours of presentation to hospital. consolidation should be present to support the diagnosis of pneumonia, but cxr may occasionally be normal in the first 24 hours – repeat if diagnosis of pneumonia is still likely. start treatment promptly after diagnosis, within 4 hours – sooner if patient systemically unwell.
clarithromycin interacts with statins, some anticoagulants, carbamazepine and is a potent cyp inhibitor with many other interactions. statins can usually be safely stopped for the duration of the course, consider other medications on a case-by-case basis.
to see a list of specific interactions with clarithromycin: click on the interactions tab in the bnf monograph.
safety-netting e.g. with british lung foundation leaflet (ask ward staff to print off on demand) for patients who are being treated on an outpatient basis after presentation to the hospital for assessment.
consider ambulatory management if patient has fewer than 2 of the following abnormal signs present currently and for the previous 24 hours: (nice cg191): temperature higher than 37.5°c
respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute or more
heart rate over 100 beats per minute
systolic blood pressure 90 mmhg or less
oxygen saturation under 90% on room air (if normally breathing air)
abnormal mental status

name: Viral Retinitis
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment ophthalmology viral retinitis viral retinitis 
Herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2, Varicella zoster virus, Cytomegalovirus and Epstein-Barr virus are known to cause retinitis. Note – both HSV and VZV may cause acute retinal necrosis in immunocompetent individuals. 
See adult guidelines for diagnosis / red flags / follow-up advice
 Diagnosis and Differentials
 Always Remember To
 Watch Out For (red flags)
 If No Better...
 General Interest and Other Points
 Organisms
Treatment Advice 
HSV or VZV requiring IV treatment initially

Aciclovir TDS IV for 5-7 days



Child 3 months–11 years
500 mg/m2 every 8 hours
Body surface area calculator can be found at (internet connection required): https://www.medicinescomplete.com/#/calculators?calcId=body-surface-area


Child 12–17 years (dose as per ideal body weight) 
10 mg/kg every 8 hours



Then, caution - depending on age of child, oral switch varies:

 Child 1–23 months

 

 Aciclovir 100 mg 5 times a day PO  for 6 weeks


 Child 2 - 11 years old


 Valaciclovir 20mg/kg (max. 1000mg or 1 gram per dose) TDS PO for 6 weeks 
  

 Child 12 years and over

 

 Valaciclovir 1g TDS PO for 6 weeks 
  


If taking a vitreal or aqueous fluid sample, then inject Foscarnet* 2.4mg in 0.1mL intra-vitreally stat dose in theatre under supervision of consultant ophthalmologist

* NB. Foscarnet is a special-order item and may take some time to obtain from Pharmacy. Contact Pharmacy as soon as possible if unable to transfer out to a specialist ophthalmic centre for this procedure.


CMV   

Ganciclovir* 5mg/kg BD IV for 2-3 weeks

After resolution of initial infectious episode, continued suppressive therapy may be required: Ganciclovir implants may be considered. Discuss with microbiology consultant.
 
* Ensure women of childbearing age are not pregnant or breastfeeding and that men and women take adequate contraceptive precautions during and for at least 90 days after treatment with ganciclovir due to genotoxic and teratogenic potential.

name: Treatment
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment gastrointestinal biliary infections treatment treatment  IV treatment
  
  1 st line empirical treatment 
    
   Co-amoxiclav 1.2g TDS IV


 Penicillin allergy Check for recent admissions and treatment with gentamicin – risk of toxicity increases with repeat courses. Monitor creatinine trends closely and signs of tinnitus/ototoxicity symptoms. Gentamicin IV as per protocol
plus
Metronidazole 500mg TDS IV Consider adding vancomycin (as per Trust protocol) for Enterococcal cover in penicillin-allergic patients with more severe disease, especially post-ERCP or if there is a stent in situ.
IV treatment should not exceed 5 days due to risk of gentamicin toxicity
When patients pass ACED – review for oral switch Afebrile 24 hoursClinically improvedEatingDrinking

Oral switch  Co-trimoxazole 960mg BD PO
  
  Patients with GFR under 30mL/min give 50% dose. Avoid use in patients with blood disorders, discontinue if rash or blood disorders develop. 
    
  Or 
    
 Co-amoxiclav 625mg TDS PO 
  
   Uncomplicated acute cholecystitis with successful source control – usual duration 24 hours post procedure. 
  
 Duration of up to 4 days treatment (IV+PO) is usually sufficient after source control is achieved for more complicated cases.
  
 Where abscesses/collections are present, discuss case with microbiology to determine duration of treatment
  
   Patients in whom surgical / radiological intervention is not possible may require more extended antibiotic management, discuss antibiotic choices with microbiology. Switch to oral treatment as soon as they pass ACED.

name: Payroll
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my royal devon life (eastern services) all about my pay payroll payroll 

   Payroll Team
  
The payroll team is responsible for the prompt and accurate payment of salary and benefits for all staff, and they offer a supportive service to both managers and colleagues.
The team are responsible for the monthly and weekly payroll which is in excess of 15,000 assignments.
Each month, the team adheres to tight deadlines for the submission of information, changes and amendments in order to achieve payment of salary on the 27th. These deadlines, which managers and colleagues must observe, are published before each month commences.
Payroll carries out a monthly and end-of-year reconciliation of payroll information in order to make the necessary payments of tax and NI contributions to HMRC.
The team also produces the annual total reward statements as well as the P11Ds and P60s for all colleagues.
All payments are made directly into bank or building society accounts.
Additional payments for unsociable hours, overtime hours, etc. are made a month in arrears. Weekly paid staff, receive their wages a week in arrears and the payment is credited to their bank or building society accounts on a Friday.
Find more about payroll and their contact details on the ‘Payroll’ page on the HUB.

name: Meet the SP & EoL Care Team
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life departments - clinical specialist palliative and end of life care (sp & eolc) team meet the sp & eol care team meet the sp & eol care team the specialist palliative & end of life care team what is palliative care
palliative medicine is the medical specialty that concentrates on improving the quality of life for those with advanced illness, and their families.
the focus of palliative care is to maximise quality of life by providing relief from pain and other distressing symptoms and to support the patient to live as actively as possible until death. it may even influence the course of the illness. palliative care takes a holistic approach and integrates psychological and spiritual aspects of patient care and family support.
palliative care and active treatment are not mutually exclusive. a palliative approach to care can be introduced early in the illness process alongside other therapies that are intended to prolong life.
palliative care can be provided by anyone in any setting and is not exclusively for patients with cancer.
the team at nddh
what they do - the specialist palliative & end of life care team provides support and advice for the care of people with life-limiting illness in hospital. this is mainly for inpatients, but can be as an outpatient when this would be most beneficial to patients and families. they work closely with the north devon hospice to tailor care to the needs of the patient.
contact details - phone 01271 311642 or extension 3642
email rduh.specialistpalliativecare@nhs.net specialist palliative care clinical nurse specialist - the cns is usually available on-site mon–fri, from 0830–1700 for telephone advice or face-to-face assessments – bleep 401.
out-of-hours clinical advice – please phone north devon hospice bedded unit on 01271 347214 (or 01271 344248) and ask to speak to the duty doctor. see also, useful clinical resources here.
for further information, access the specialist palliative care resources page on the hub and click on north. the north devon hospice
the website of the north devon hospice is here.
for more information on the hospice in this app, click here. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 meet the sp & eol care team the specialist palliative & end of life care team

searchField: hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology ocular toxoplasmosis read this tab first read this tab first  Toxoplasmosis is caused by Toxoplasma gondii, an intracellular protozoan parasite. Its main host is the cat. Ocular toxoplasmosis is a common cause of posterior uveitis and must immediately be referred to the consultant ophthalmologist if suspected.

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment respiratory hospital acquired pneumonia diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Develops more than 48 hours after hospital admission and was not incubating at the time of admission. Epidemiology probably similar to CAP in the absence of recent prolonged intubation (although atypical infection will be even less common.)



Most patients with pneumonia have fever, purulent sputum and dyspnoea.
Elderly patients with Gram negative sepsis may present with “chest infections”, take urinary history e.g. catheterisation.
Routine investigation of mild pneumonia is rarely helpful.
Rule out fluid overload as a cause for chest symptoms in the absence of infective symptoms (i.e. fever / rigors, purulent sputum)

name: Wet, sloughy wound
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care choosing a wound dressing wet, sloughy wound wet, sloughy wound wet, sloughy wound 
Sloughy wounds that have a high volume of exudate require management that involves promoting autolytic debridement, controlling the exudate and protecting the surrounding skin. 
To protect the surrounding skin use Medi derma-S barrier cream (available in sachets on the ward)
Aquacel
Aquacel Extra: soft, sterile, hydrofibre dressing which absorbs wound fluid and transforms into a soft gel. 
Suitable for leg ulcers, pressure ulcers, diabetic ulcers, surgical wounds, abrasions/lacerations, 1st or 2nd degree burns, wounds that are prone to bleeding. 
Aquacel is suitable for cavities/deep wounds as it is able to maintain integrity and does not dissolve within the wound cavity. Therefore it is easy to change the dressing frequently. 

Sorbsan
Sorbsan Flat: highly absorbent alginate dressing made from seaweed and come in the form of soft sterile pads. 
They absorb excess exudate, turning in to a gel which keeps the wound bed moist and promotes healing.
They can be used on infected wounds. 
Not to be used in cavities or wounds where you can not see the base.
This is because due to the nature of this dressing it will gel and fall apart within the wound. It may require irrigation to fully remove, which can cause trauma to the wound bed and increase discomfort for the patient.  

Negative Pressure Wound Therapy
Non-invasive technology comprising of a negative pressure pump connected by a tube to a dressing that occupies a wound. 
There are two pumps available in the tissue viability office:
Talley Venturi: Negative pressure wound therapy device comprising of pump unit and canister for exudate storage. 
For a wide range of chronic and acute wounds (including infected wounds), burns and grafts. 

PICO: A disposable and portable system designed to kick start wound healing. 
For chronic, acute, traumatic, sub-acute, or dehisced wounds, as well as partial-thickness burns, ulcers, flaps, grafts and surgically closed incision sites.

name: How to access
searchField:
hospital guidelines obstetrics & gynaecology induction handover list how to access how to access how to access the handover list! the handover list can be found on g drive which you can access from any computer using your login. public (g) drive obsgynae handovers select the correct month e.g. february 2023 do not delete old handovers. when editing, start a new am or pm list, making sure to save as the current date.
you cannot edit the list whilst someone else has it open. if someone is editing the list, wait a while and try again later. if that person has gone home, it’s likely they have accidentally left it open – in which case make a copy. patients can get missed otherwise.
for pregnant patients, please specify whether they are antenatal or postnatal (an or pn), their gravidity/parity and current gestation.
for any postnatal ladies, please update the handover with the mode of delivery and ebl (estimated blood loss)
at the top of the list, we have added lots of useful telephone and bleep numbers, as well as some door codes. please feel free to add anymore that you find useful.
there is a separate list for obstetrics and for gynaecology. this is to reduce the confidential information for the gynaecology patients being available to the midwives on the handover sheet. document review date committee approving microguide page created 2023 microguide page last checked 22/8/24 how to access how to access the handover list!

making clear and adequate documentation in mycare patient notes during the consultant-led ward rounds a variety of jobs contributing to patient care including requesting investigations such as blood tests, imaging etc.

this f1 workplace training schedule should help you to plan your learning and development throughout your placement. hlo 1 - an accountable, capable and compassionate doctor clinical assessment review patients during on call shifts and whilst on the ward, being able to recognise clinical signs
information gathering, communicating effectively with the patient i.e. admitting patients and gaining collateral histories from next of kin how to evidence: reflections, cbds and mini-cexs clinical prioritisation recognising the need for urgent intervention i.e. acute presentation of respiratory conditions e.g. pe, acute sob, and exacerbations of asthma and copd, and cardiac conditions such as acs. knowing who and when to escalate for further advice
beginning to recognise patients nearing end of life and the measures taken to keep them comfortable

name: Places to Worship at NDDH
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my health and wellbeing spiritual support places to worship at nddh places to worship at nddh spiritual support this section is reproduced in 'my hospital life'. places to worship at nddh there is a small faith centre on level 1 at nddh. this comprises of a very small prayer room, a quiet room and a small christian chapel, which opens on to a courtyard garden. this area is open at all times, and available to all.
wednesday service a short christian time of prayer and stillness, primarily for staff, is held in the chapel on level 1 each wednesday at 1.00 pm. for more information and to join the mailing list for this time please contact the chaplain.
there is no sunday service. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 places to worship at nddh spiritual support

name: Definitions
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services sick patients & resuscitation patients at risk of deterioration definitions definitions definitions patients at risk of deterioration the trust's patients at risk of deterioration policy northern services (review date mar 2022) is available on the hub search for 'deterioration'.
this guidance is now out of date please use with caution and check all drug doses with the bnf - a new resuscitation policy is currently being ratified. the purpose of this policy is to ensure that staff identify and respond to seriously ill patients (adults and paediatrics) and those at risk of deterioration, early. this has been shown to significantly reduce morbidity and mortality and is in line with national guidance related to responding to acute and severe illness.
patients in active labour, on icu, on paediatric hdu or in recovery are excluded. the tabs below contains the definitions used in the policy. definitions of emergency and support teams adult cardiac arrest team to call any of the emergency teams dial 2222 and state your location and the team you require. for more information on all emergency teams see the section my hospital life in the handbook app.
a team of qualified individuals which responds to all adult medical emergencies when summoned via the bleep system. the team consists of medical f1, medical f2, anaesthetic junior, outreach & resuscitation officer/ senior on-call nurse and a porter. there is a detailed list of all expected attendees to an adult cardiac arrest call and their bleep numbers in the handbook app - look in the 'my hospital life' section under 'bleep numbers' or search 'emergency team members' on your device. early warning score (ews) an early warning score (ews) is a track and trigger system derived from the patient observation chart, which is then used to recognise patients at risk of a cardio-pulmonary arrest. the trust uses the following national adult early warning score (news2)
modified early obstetric warning score (meows)
paediatric early warning score (pews)
newborn early warning score (news) for more information on these scores, see the individual tabs on early warning scores. hospital @ night (h@n) the hospital at night (h@n) concept is a way to achieve effective clinical care by having one or more multi-professional teams, who between them have the full range of skills and competencies to meet patients’ immediate needs. it allows comprehensive and timely handover between teams of any patients deemed to be at an increased clinical risk, ensuring that they are appropriately reviewed during the night time period. outreach & resuscitation officer a qualified individual, who is responsible for providing staff with education on the early warning score and other signs of deterioration, as well as the appropriate response.
for more information on the role of the outreach & resuscitation team, see their section in the handbook app - look in the 'my hospital life' section under 'clinical departments' or search 'outreach' on your device. paediatric arrest team a team of qualified individuals which responds to all paediatric emergencies when summoned via the bleep system. the team consists of a paediatric register, a neonatal sho, an anaesthetic junior, an outreach & resuscitation officer or senior on-call nurse and a porter.
there is a detailed list of all expected attendees to a paediatric cardiac arrest call and their bleep numbers in the handbook app - look in the 'my hospital life' section under 'bleep numbers' or search 'emergency team members' on your device. par - patients at risk of deterioration those patients that trigger on an early warning system or whose condition causes concern to medical, nursing or midwifery staff. treatment escalation plans (tep) there is a tep for adults and a separate paediatric tep for those under 16.
the tep is a written and signed order of treatment limitations. it is a devon-wide document that can be used by gps, nurses and consultants in charge of the patient’s care. it has a section on the resuscitation status of the individual.
the tep is part of the northern resuscitation policy (review date feb 2024) which can be accessed on the hub - search for resuscitation policy definitions patients at risk of deterioration

    How to use mulitple daily dosing 
  


    Monitoring and redosing 
  


 Levels should be taken before the 3rd dose, and this dose should then be given without awaiting the result.

Indwelling catheter

name: Community Acquired pneumonia - Follow up
searchField:
community acquired pneumonia - follow up community acquired pneumonia (cap) - follow up community acquired pneumonia,cap,cap follow up community acquired pneumonia (cap) - follow up delayed clinical improvement antibiotic failure in community acquired pneumonia is uncommon. if patients fail to respond to first-line antimicrobials, first consider alternative diagnoses.
clinical improvement should be observed within 72 hours of receiving appropriate antimicrobial therapy. if there is sustained pyrexia, lack of improvement in oxygen requirement or deterioration in oxygenation, radiological deterioration or persistently elevated or rising inflammatory markers consider discussion with a microbiologist.
if respiratory tract aetiology is still likely, consider investigating for:
empyema/lung abscess/necrotising pneumonia
bacteria which may not be covered by first line empirical therapy e.g. legionella species, m. tuberculosis or pneumocystis jirovecii (in immunocompromised hosts). please discuss with microbiology for further advice.
respiratory viruses which will not respond to antimicrobials and will require alternative investigations.
travel-associated infections and zoonoses where applicable, e.g. legionella or q fever
post-infectious inflammatory response
non-infectious diagnoses, e.g. pulmonary embolus, pulmonary oedema
metastatic infection from another source, e.g. endocarditis with septic emboli to the lungs iv to po switch see guidance in eolas
safe discharge from hospital (taken from nice cg191) do not routinely discharge people with cap if in the past 24 hours they have had 2 or more of the following findings: temperature > 37.5°c
rr > 24 breaths per minute
hr > 100 beats per minute
sbp < 90 mmhg
spo2 < 90% on room air without known chronic lung disease
abnormal mental status
inability to eat without assistance recovery from community acquired pneumonia
explain to patients with cap that after starting treatment their symptoms should steadily improve, although the rate of improvement will vary with the severity of the pneumonia, and most people can expect that by (nice cg191): 1 week: fever should have resolved
4 weeks: chest pain and sputum production should have substantially reduced
6 weeks: cough and breathlessness should have substantially reduced
3 months: most symptoms should have resolved but fatigue may still be present
6 months: most people will feel back to normal. complications of cap & management e.g. empyema complications e.g. parapneumonic effusion, empyema or abscess, may not respond to abx alone.
referral should be made to the respiratory team for consideration of dedicated lung imaging e.g. uss and consideration for thoracocentesis.
those found to have an empyema or clear pleural fluid with ph <7.2 should have early and effective pleural fluid drainage. follow up chest xray in line with the 2025 national institute for clinical excellence (nice) pneumonia guidelines (ng250), do not routinely offer follow-up chest x rays to people discharged from inpatient care after an episode of pneumonia chest x-ray should be repeated at six weeks for patients with: risk factors for lung cancer or other underlying respiratory disease, for example, people who smoke or are over 50 years. persisting or deteriorating symptoms unexplained weight loss if a follow-up chest x-ray is being considered, make a shared decision with the person considering: recent imaging, presence of any comorbidities or frailty, the patient's prognosis and treatment options and the patient’s preference. where a decision is made not to repeat the cxr at six weeks after the initial infection, the rationale for this decision should be clearly documented in the patients’ medical notes.

name: If No Better...
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment skin and soft tissue breast abscess and mastitis if no better... if no better... 
If symptoms fail to settle after 48 hours of first-line antibiotic treatment:



Check that the woman has taken the antibiotic correctly.
Consider the possibility of an alternative diagnosis (such as breast cancer or a breast abscess).

If an abscess is suspected, be aware that malaise and fever may have subsided if antibiotics have been started.
 
 
If mastitis recurs, manage as for treatment failure. In addition:

Identify and manage any predisposing factors, such as:

Nipple damage from skin conditions such as psoriasis, eczema, infection, or Raynaud's disease of the nipple .
 
Ensure that the woman is aware of measures to prevent recurrence, such as stopping smoking and maintaining good hygiene.

name: Suspected DVT
searchField:
hospital guidelines emergency medicine other minors presentations suspected dvt draft suspected dvt suspected deep vein thrombosis (dvt) pulmonary embolism is the major complication of dvt. anticoagulation significantly reduces the risk of embolism, but leg dvt can be difficult to diagnose clinically.
the modified wells prediction rule and d-dimer blood test are used to assess the probability of dvt, and ultrasonography is used to confirm the diagnosis.
patients with a wells score of 2 or more (dvt likely) require ultrasonography to confirm the diagnosis and anticoagulation if ultrasound cannot be done within 4 hours. a d-dimer is still useful in this population group as if the ultrasound is negative, but the d-dimer is positive a follow up ultrasound will be arranged.
patients with a wells score of 0 or 1 (dvt unlikely) but a positive d-dimer (>230 ng/ml if age less than 50 or > age x5 if over) require ultrasonography to rule out dvt.
a wells score 0 or 1 (dvt unlikely) combined with a negative d-dimer (<230 ng/ml if age less than 50 or age x5 if above) rules out dvt. clinical features
typical presenting features of dvt include: leg oedema, swelling, principally unilateral
pain and tenderness, usually confined to the calf muscles or over the deep veins of the thigh
venous distension or superficial thrombophlebitis without varicose veins or other obvious cause
warmth, skin discolouration
pyrexia, usually low grade history/examination
all patients with suspected dvt should have a thorough history and examination including the following as a minimum:
history: nature and duration of symptoms
pmh and current medication
allergies examination: record temperature, pulse, respiratory rate, blood pressure, weight
calf measurement 10 cm below tibial tuberosity
thigh measurement 10 cm above patella
examination of chest note - dvt can be a presentation of systemic disease (e.g. malignancy) or pregnancy. please consider this when undertaking history taking and examination.
management anticoagulation decision tree apixaban standard dose = 10mg bd for 7/7 then 5mg bd
if creatinine clearance 15-29ml/min use with caution
if creatinine clearance <15 it is contraindicated
apixaban alert card can be found here. dalteparin standard dose = 200u/kg s/c (maximum 18,000 units) governance author of clinical guideline division/ department responsible for clinical guideline guideline reviewed by approving body and date approved expiry date suspected dvt suspected deep vein thrombosis (dvt) pulmonary embolism is the major complication of dvt. anticoagulation significantly reduces the risk of embolism, but leg dvt can be difficult to diagnose clinically.

name: Staffside
searchField:

staffside
staffside is the voice of all the employees of the royal devon. they work in partnership with management to ensure staff are consulted in a meaningful way. this can include consultations, policy rewriting and any changes to terms and conditions.
find more by searching 'staffside' on hub.
unions
unison is the largest trade union representing, campaigning and negotiating for workers across the royal devon university healthcare nhs foundation trust and private employers.
unison members are people working in the public services, for private contractors providing public services and the essential utilities. they include frontline staff and managers working full or part time in local authorities, the nhs, the police service, colleges and schools, the electricity, gas and water industries, transport and the voluntary sector.
unison campaigns and lobbies on key issues affecting their members and the public at large. they are working to protect and improve public services, win equal pay and employment rights for everyone, improve safety in the workplace and end discrimination and harassment at work.

name: Alginate Dressings
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care choosing a wound dressing absorbent dressings alginate dressings alginate dressings 
Alginate dressings are made from seaweed and come as soft sterile pads or ropes.
They absorb excess exudate, turning in to a gel which keeps the wound bed moist and promotes healing.
They can be used on infected wounds. 
Sorbsan Flat  Sorbsan Flat: highly absorbent  
  Suitable for moderately to heavily exuding wounds. May be used to manage minor bleeding wounds. 
Not to be used in cavities or wounds where you can not see the base.
This is because due to the nature of this dressing it will gel and fall apart within the wound. It may require irrigation to fully remove, which can cause trauma to the wound bed and increase discomfort for the patient.

name: 1. Decision to Admit, Self-Care and Signposting
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment sepsis see ukpas guidance - section archived 1st aug 2025 [paediatric sepsis] 1. decision to admit, self-care and signposting decision to admit, self-care and signposting consider the following factors when deciding whether to admit a child with fever to hospital: social and family circumstances
other illnesses that affect the child or other family members
parental anxiety and instinct (based on their knowledge of their child)
contacts with other people who have serious infectious diseases
recent travel abroad to tropical/subtropical areas, or areas with a high risk of endemic infectious disease
when the parent or carer’s concern for their child’s current illness has caused them to seek healthcare advice repeatedly
where the family has experienced a previous serious illness or death due to feverish illness which has increased their anxiety levels when a feverish illness has no obvious cause, but the child remains ill longer than expected for a self-limiting illness. safety-netting if it is decided that the child does not need to be admitted to hospital, but no diagnosis has been reached, provide a safety net for parents and carers if any “red” or “amber” features are present (see age-specific sections for risk stratification tables). the safety net should consist of one or more of the following: verbal or written information on warning symptoms and how further healthcare can be accessed
arrange further follow-up at a specified time and place
liaising with other healthcare professionals, including out of hours providers, to ensure direct access for the child if further assessment is required. children with “green” features and none of the “amber” or “red” features can be cared for at home with appropriate advice for parents and carers, including advice on when to seek further attention from the healthcare services. self-care advice managing temperature: antipyretic drugs do not prevent febrile convulsions and should not be used specifically for this purpose
tepid sponging is not recommended for the treatment of fever
children with fever should not be underdressed or over-wrapped
use paracetamol (or ibuprofen if child is over 3 months) in children with fever and who appear distressed. do not use antipyretic agents with the sole aim of reducing body temperature in children with fever. when using antipyretic agents: continue only as long as the child appears distressed
[children over 3 months in whom ibuprofen can be used] consider changing to the other agent if the child’s distress is not alleviated
[children over 3 months in whom ibuprofen can be used] do not give both agents simultaneously
[children over 3 months in whom ibuprofen can be used] only consider alternating these agents if the distress persists or recurs before the next dose is due
be aware that antipyretic agents mask changes in temperature. if the child appears more unwell than the last time the parents/carer sought healthcare advice but has a normal temperature and is taking regular antipyretics, this warrants further healthcare assessment. managing hydration offer the child regular fluids (where baby or child is breastfed, the most appropriate fluid is breastmilk)
signs of dehydration to monitor for: dry mouth
sunken eyes
absence of tears
poor overall appearance
little or no urine output encourage the child to drink more fluids and consider seeking further advice if they detect signs of dehydration, or if the child is unable to manage any oral intake other self-care advice give parent/carer tips on identifying a non-blanching rash
ensure the parent/carer knows to check on the child regularly during the night
keep the child away from school / nursery / other children or babies whilst the fever persists, but to notify the school or nursery of the illness
seek further help if the child has any of the following: fits
non-blanching rash develops
child is less well than when the parent/carer previously sought advice
fever lasts longer than 5 days
parent/carer is distressed or concerned that they are unable to look after their child decision to admit,self-care and signposting consider the following factors when deciding whether to admit a child name: Referral to Paediatric Rapid Access Clinic
Treatment - Bacterial
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology keratitis treatment - bacterial treatment - bacterial 
Non sight-threatening ulcer/infiltrate (corneal scrape not attempted if less than 1mmx1mm keratitis)

Ofloxacin 0.3% eye drops OR levofloxacin P/F  0.5% eye drops

(Last resort if other drops unavailable: use ciprofloxacin 0.3% eye drops)

1 drop instilled into affected eye(s) every 5 minutes for 30 minutes, then hourly for 24-48 hours, then TDS for 7 days.



Alternative, if resistance to quinolones, OR first line treatment if lesion over 1mmx1mm sight-threatening ulcer/infiltrate (patient usually admitted):

Cefuroxime 5% preservative-free eye drops

1 drop instilled into affected eye(s) every 5 minutes for 30 minutes, then hourly for 24-48 hours, then TDS for 7 days.

Plus

Gentamicin forte 1.5% preservative-free eye drops

(If unavailable: use gentamicin 0.3% eyedrops [contain benzalkonium chloride])

1 drop instilled into affected eye(s) every 5 minutes for 30 minutes, then hourly for 24-48 hours, then TDS for 7 days.

N.B. Start gentamicin after the 30-minutes initial cefuroxime dosing, then alternate cefuroxime and gentamicin every 30 minutes for 24-48 hours.


Drops do not need to be preservative-free when stepping down to TDS treatment.

   Royal Devon Chaplaincy Service RD&E (Wonford)
  
We are a small hospital department responsible for supporting the pastoral, spiritual, and religious care of patients, their carers, and our staff.
The chaplaincy centre is situated on Level 2 Area O and is always open for use as a quiet space for patients, visitors, and staff. There is an area set aside with prayer mats and there are ablution facilities for the use of Muslims who wish to pray.
To find out more about the team and the service they offer search 'chaplaincy' on the HUB.

name: General information
searchField: hospital guidelines antimicrobial prophylaxis (surgical and other) general information general information general information general information antibiotic guidelines for surgical prophylaxis v1.1 published july 2018 general information all intravenous antibiotics to be administered by the anaesthetist at induction nb. prophylaxis may be altered if evidence of resistant flora. name: Always Remember To
searchField: hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment skin and soft tissue animal and human bites always remember to always remember to ascertain blood-borne virus status of human bite donors if possible, refer to the post-exposure prophylaxis guidance in the management of inoculation injuries policy on bob. inform microbiology consultant if bat bite, or animal bite acquired abroad and follow phe advice regarding rabies. referral to microbiology consultant for advice if wild or exotic animal (incl. pets), marine life or bird bite/peck wounds. refer to safeguarding guidance on bob if concerns about bite wounds or injuries in vulnerable patients. take a swab if wound is purulent or discharging frank pus to send for mc&s. send 2 sets of blood cultures if febrile or rigors, or otherwise systemically unwell (e.g. gi upset). always remember to ascertain blood-borne virus status of human bite donors if possible, refer to the post-exposure prophylaxis guidance in the management of inoculation injuries policy on hub. inform microbiology consultant if bat bite, or animal bite acquired abroad and follow ukhsa rigs advice regarding rabies. referral to microbiology consultant for advice if wild or exotic animal (incl. pets), marine life or bird bite/peck wounds. refer to safeguarding guidance on hub if concerns about bite wounds or injuries in vulnerable patients. take a swab if wound is purulent or discharging frank pus to send for mc&s. send 2 sets of blood cultures if febrile or rigors, or otherwise systemically unwell (e.g. gi upset).

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment skin and soft tissue breast abscess and mastitis diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Lactational and non-lactational mastitis are managed differently

In women with a breast abscess, antibiotics alone without removal of pus are unlikely to be curative. However, a thoroughly performed, ultrasonography-guided aspiration may be curative, and culture of pus may enable optimisation of antibiotic treatment.
Even when clinical examination shows signs of an abscess, an ultrasound is useful because it may identify more than one collection of pus that may otherwise be missed

name: Severe Hyponatraemia - Emergency Management
searchField:
hospital guidelines adult clinical guidelines medicine endocrinology severe hyponatraemia - emergency management severe hyponatraemia - emergency management severe hyponatraemia - emergency management severe hyponatraemia - emergency management emergency management of severe hyponatraemia: the role of hypertonic saline pathway approved 11/23 review date 11/2027 iv - intravenous na+ - sodium vbg - venous blood gas itu - intensive care unit amu - acute medical unit nacl - sodium chloride / saline additional information indications
management decisions should be made on the history of progression and symptoms of the hyponatraemia. symptoms of vomiting, seizures, reduced gcs (</=8) or cardiorespiratory arrest in the context of acute hyponatraemia (na<130) and no other contributory cause, should be considered severe hyponatraemia and escalation of care to hypertonic saline should be discussed. patients with severe symptoms require immediate treatment with hypertonic saline, irrespective of the cause of the hyponatraemia.
patients with acute hyponatraemia without correction, despite best care, in the presence of nausea, confusion or headache can also be considered for hypertonic saline.
hypertonic saline should ideally not be administered on the basis of one-off sodium, but on the trend of a few. the decision to treat with hypertonic saline should be made by the senior treating physician. out of hours, this should be the on-call medical consultant or itu consultant. the consultant endocrinologists do not do out-of-hours on calls, however are happy to be contacted for discussion if needed and available. administration
administer 150ml of 2.7% hypertonic saline over 20 minutes to attain 5mmol/l increase in sodium in an hour as per schematic below. this should ideally be via a central line, if in place, or, a large peripheral vein with monitoring of the phlebitis score.
the patient should ideally be located in a close monitoring environment e.g. amu or itu. if this is not feasible it is essential that the patient is in an area where neurological state and fluid balance can be monitored carefully.
2.7% nacl comes in 500ml polyfusor therefore it is essential that this is given by a volumetric pump programmed for 150ml over 20mins. it is kept in the emergency drug cupboard. monitoring
after initial treatment, sodium levels should be monitored on vbg analysis (see below) and formal serum bloods at least 6 hourly in the first 24 hours after administration. if the sodium is rising more than 10 mmol/24 hours, 5 % at 125ml/hr dextrose should be initiated to prevent over treatment and osmotic demyelination. endocrinology review or discussion with an endocrinologist within 24 hours (this may be virtual if no endocrinologist available on site). pathway the majority of patients with hyponatraemia can be managed conservatively whilst timely investigations and treatments are initiated based on the clinical picture and underlying aetiology. guidelines for investigating hyponatraemia are outside the remits of this document. read additional information above in conjunction with this pathway iv - intravenous na+ - sodium vbg - venous blood gas itu - intensive care unit amu - acute medical unit nacl - sodium chloride / saline references ball, s., barth, j., miles, l., and the society for endocrinology clinical committee endocrine connections 5 g4-6 injectable medicines guide document review date november 2027 committee approving amu/medicine/endocrinolgy governance microguide page created 4/24 microguide page last checked 1/8/24 severe hyponatraemia - emergency management severe hyponatraemia - emergency management

name: Urinalysis for Specific Pathogens
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life departments - clinical pathology microbiology microbiology tests urinalysis urinalysis for specific pathogens urinalysis for specific pathogens microbiology - urinalysis specific pathogens
introduction contaminating bacteria from the external genitalia may give rise to misleading results.
therefore we can only accept the following specimens for routine culture: catheter or cystoscopy specimens
mid-stream urine specimens
supra-pubic aspirates please note that urinary catheter tips will not be processed as they do not provide helpful microbiological information.
for information on the correct procedure for urine collection click here.
the majority of urine results will be available the following day.
for more information on the microbiology department and the tests they provide, see their page on the pathology laboratory website here.
cytomegalovirus (cmv) use a plain sterile (white top) universal container and send or take to the laboratory immediately accompanied by a completed blue and white microbiology request form. legionella & pneumococcal antigens if legionella or pneumococcal antigen is to be excluded, please send a urine sample in a red top boric acid container (a plain universal container is acceptable only if the sample can be taken to the laboratory and processed immediately). mycobacterium tuberculosis (tb) urine culture for mycobacterium tuberculosis is only performed when white cells are present in the urine.
submit an msu specimen (see above) in a plain, white topped container, indicating that mycobacterium tuberculosis culture is required.
this will be examined as a normal msu.
if white cells are present, 3 x 250 ml urine containers (available from pathology) should be used so that three consecutive early morning specimens can be collected for examination for tb.
if there are no white cells present in the msu, mycobacterium tuberculosis culture is not indicated. schistosomiasis collect the urine sample in a plain (non-boric acid) container between 1000 and 1400, as this is when the highest concentration of eggs is found.
in patients with haematuria, eggs may be found trapped in the blood and mucus in the terminal portion of the urine specimen.
it is therefore preferable to obtain a complete urine sample.
alternatively, submit terminal stream urine collected over an entire 24 hour period - 24 hour urine containers (brown container with a yellow top) are available from pathology stores.
if the urine cannot be examined within an hour of collection, it is advisable to add 1 ml of undiluted formalin to preserve any eggs that may be present. microguide page created 2021 microguide page last checked 28/08/2024 urinalysis for specific pathogens microbiology - urinalysis specific pathogens

name: Organisms
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment skin and soft tissue necrotising fasciitis organisms organisms 
Type 1 NF (synergistic gangrene) and is caused by anaerobes and Gram negative organisms (usually originating from the bowel)

Type 2 NF is caused by toxin producing Group A Streptococcus

Type 3 NF (Gas gangrene) is a deeper infection of the muscle tissues, usually caused by Clostridium perfringens and is treated in the same manner as necrotising fasciitis. Debridement is by necessity much more extensive. It is comparatively rare.

name: TURBT
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial prophylaxis (surgical and other) surgery urology turbt turbt 

    Urology 
  
Screen all patients’ urine before a procedure. Always check pre-operative urine results for evidence of resistant organisms
For non-stone procedures, microbiology advice will be tailored to ensure that isolated organisms are covered with alternative antibiotics if standard prophylaxis will not suffice.
For stone procedures, microbiology advice as for non-stone procedures but patients should also be pre-treated for 3 days before the procedure with an antibiotic that covers the isolated organisms.

    Surgery 
  
Antibiotic Guidelines for Surgical Prohpylaxis v1.1 published July 2018

    TURBT 
  


    1st Line 
  

Gentamicin 160mg IV (N.B. See also general advice above)

name: Secondary prophylaxis
searchField:

name: Acute Scrotal Pain / Torsion
searchField:
acute scrotal pain / torsion acute scrotal pain / torsion guidance asp,asp,torsion,testicle,scrotum,scrotal acute scrotal pain pathway - clinical guidance
acute scrotal pain pathway - clinical guidance (northern) review date 1/4/27
this document provides guidance for the diagnosis, management and follow up of acute scrotal pain. it is based on the pathway in use at eastern and adapted for use in northern and to incorporate recent publications from ncepod and girft. there is a summary flow chart here (appendix 1) which can be used as a quick-reference guide and will be displayed in relevant clinical areas.
key points acute scrotal pain (asp) should raise suspicion of a possible testicular torsion. testicular torsion is a surgical emergency and suspicion warrants urgent scrotal exploration. any child/young male presenting with abdominal pain should have a testicular examination performed.
in a testicular torsion the blood supply to the testis is cut off due to a twist of the testis on its axis. the chance of the testis surviving decreases the longer it is left, with high likelihood of the testis dying after 6 hours.
recent guidelines from girft and ncepod emphasise the important of prompt assessment by a surgical decision maker and urgent surgery if appropriate.
all referrals for acute scrotal pain should be made via bleep to the on call surgical registrar (bleep 900)
intra-operative management depends on pathology found and the decision-making tool from the burst trial can aid in this process.
all patients who undergo orchidopexy for confirmed testicular torsion should be followed up. introduction & background
this clinical guideline is designed to provide advice, guidance and direction to staff whilst leaving room for professional judgement, and adaptation, to fit individual circumstances.
this guideline applies to patients of all ages presenting with acute scrotal pain in the northern site.
background
this is a new guideline to improve care for patients with acute scrotal pain. it aims to provide clarity about who to contact and to aid with decision making.
acute scrotal pain (asp) should raise suspicion of a possible testicular torsion. testicular torsion is a surgical emergency and suspicion warrants urgent scrotal exploration. in a testicular torsion the blood supply to the testis is cut off due to a twist of the testis on its axis. the chance of the testis surviving decreases the longer it is left, with high likelihood of the testis dying after 6 hours.
whilst there is a bimodal distribution of torsion (neonate and more commonly early adolescent boys) torsion needs to be ruled out at any age. definitions & abbreviations orchidopexy – surgical fixation of the testis within the scrotum to prevent further episodes of torsion
orchidectomy – surgical removal of the testis
asp - acute scrotal pain
aaa - ambulatory assessment area kgv - king george v ward
sti - sexually transmitted infection
twist score - see acute scrotal pain pathway northern services cct - certificate of completion of training
assessment & management of acute scrotal pain
referral
patients with asp may be referred from their gp or from the emergency department (ed). referrals from a gp should be advised to attend kgv aaa (over 18) or ed (under 18) as soon as possible and stay nil by mouth. on arrival the ed triage team or aaa team should contact the surgical registrar on bleep 900 immediately.
for patients self-presenting to ed with less than 48-hour history of acute unilateral testicular pain the triage team should make a direct referral to the surgical registrar on bleep 900 as soon as possible.
assessment
the surgical registrar should see the patient within 1 hour of arrival. if they are unavailable they should send a junior doctor (ideally bleep 333) to assess the patient and report back to them.
history
testicular torsion commonly presents with acute unilateral scrotal or abdominal pain which may or may not be associated with nausea or vomiting. the history should also include time of onset, any previous episodes (to suggest intermittent torsion), any history of trauma, urinary symptoms and a sexual history if the patient is sexually active. a twist score should be calculated.
examination
examine the abdomen, groin and scrotum including checking the cremasteric reflex.
investigations
urine dip. send for sti screen if patient is sexually active. bloods are not usually helpful.
imaging should only be considered if there is a more likely alternative pathology that would change management or if the pain has been present for more than 48 hours. it should not delay surgery if surgery is indicated.
transfer to a tertiary centre (bristol children’s hospital) is not usually indicated and should not occur without a consultant to consultant discussion. telephone advice can be sought if there is diagnostic uncertainty.
management
all patients with suspected testicular torsion need surgical exploration within 1 hour of deciding to operate. fasting status is not relevant. scrotal exploration can be performed by either general surgery or urology teams – both specialties mandate proficiency for cct. if there is not an available theatre within an appropriate time there should be consideration for opening another theatre for timely surgery. liaise with the theatre co-ordinator on bleep 256 to discuss this.
consent
consent the patient for orchidectomy, decreased fertility, infection (in the testicle or the wound), haematoma that may require drainage, contralateral testicular injury and anaesthetic complications including the risks of not being adequately fasted.
recommend or provide a copy of the baus information leaflet: here
operative findings and decision making
a recent consensus document (see references)has made the following recommendations for management of testes according to the operative findings:
testicular torsion
untwist testis, wrap in warm saline soaked gauze & fix contralateral side. if testis is not viable proceed to orchidectomy. if viability is uncertain then fix – do not perform orchidectomy.
if there is no evidence of torsion, the presence or absence of a bell-clapper abnormality determines subsequent management: no torsion, no other pathology seen
if no torsion found and no other abnormalities are seen to account for presentation do not fix testis.
if no torsion found and no other abnormalities are seen, but bell-clapper abnormality is present then fix the testis and explore contralateral side and fix if bell-clapper abnormality is seen. no evidence of testicular torsion but other abnormality seen to account for presentation
examples of other pathology include epididymo-orchitis, torted testicular appendage, mumps orchitis and testicular tumour. torted appendage can be excised.
if bell-clapper abnormality is present then fix the testis and explore contralateral side (unless there is epididymo-orchitis) and fix if bell-clapper abnormality is seen.
see appendix 2 for detailed decision-making flow chart here
for children either a dartos pouch or suture fixation can be performed (depending on surgeon experience). adults should undergo a suture fixation – either 3 or 4 point fixation is acceptable. either non-absorbable or prolonged-absorption sutures can be used. patients with epididymo-orchitis should be treated according to the trusts antibiotic guidelines (on hub or eolas app). patients with risk factors for sexually-transmitted infections should be advised to attend the sexual health clinic. all patients should see their gp within 2-4 weeks to assess resolution and need for ultrasound or other investigations. all patients must be advised to return to ed for re-assessment at once if the condition worsens or they have any concerns. follow up
all patients with confirmed torsion that undergo fixation should be followed up at 6 months to assess for delayed atrophy.2
patients under age 18 – order a face to face follow up with “paediatrics – surgery”
patients age 18 and over – order a face to face follow up with the responsible urologist (who is on call when the patient is admitted) patients who have an orchidectomy should be given patient-initiated follow up (pifu) in case they require further information or support. prosthetics can be discussed in post-pubertal patients.
patients who do not have confirmed torsion do not require a follow up.
discharge advice should include details about wound care and sutures, follow up arrangements and who to contact if they have concerns. patients should also be given advice regarding prosthetics – these cannot be offered until puberty is complete. if the patient is still on pifu they can request an appointment, otherwise their gp can refer them to either paediatric surgery (if under 18) or urology (if over 18). references & compliance monitoring references 1. keiran d. clement, alexander light, aqua asif, vinson wai-shun chan, sinan khadhouri et al. a burst-baus consensus document for best practice in the conduct of scrotal exploration for suspected testicular torsion: the finding consensus for orchidopexy in torsion (fix-it) study bju international 2022; 130 (5): 662-670. available at: - https://bjui-journals.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/bju.15818 (accessed 16th july 2024)
2. getting it right first time (nhs impact). girft children and young people: testicular torsion pathway. available at https://gettingitrightfirsttime.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/paediatric-testicular-torsion-pathway-guide-final-v1-february-2024.pdf (accessed 16th july 2024)
3. british association of paediatric surgeons, british association of paediatric urologists. commissioning guide: management of paediatric torsion. available at: https://www.england.nhs.uk/mids-east/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2018/03/torsion-commissioning-guide.pdf (accessed 16th july 2024)
4. british association of urological surgeons. scrotal exploration for suspected torsion of the testis. available at: https://www.baus.org.uk/_userfiles/pages/files/patients/leaflets/torsion%20of%20testis.pdf (accessed 16th july 2024) compliance monitoring compliance with this guideline will be monitored with regular audit – particularly regarding time to review by surgical decision maker and time to theatre. approving committee/group surgical care group review date 1/4/27 eolas page created & editor number 6/12/24 001 eolas page checked & editor number 6/2/25 001

name: Maximum body weight table and administration options
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial gentamicin and vancomycin help vancomycin in adults maximum body weight table and administration options maximum body weight table and administration options 
Vancomycin in Adults Guideline
Version 3.0 published Jan 2022
Administration Options

To reconstitute vancomycin add 10ml of water for injection to every 500mg vancomycin powder.
Dilute doses up to and including 1 g in 250mL sodium chloride 0.9%
Dilute doses above 1 g and up to 2 g in 500mL sodium chloride 0.9%.
Dilute doses above 2 g in 1 litre sodium chloride 0.9%
The duration of the infusion should be 1 hour for every 500mg of vancomycin.
In fluid restricted patients, consider diluting doses into 100mL sodium chloride 0.9% and giving through a large, fast flowing vein. Note this is an unlicensed administration method. The duration of the infusion should still be 1 hour for every 500mg of vancomycin.

name: Surgical prophylaxis

Antibiotic prophylaxis must be prescribed according to local surgical prophylaxis guidance, where available.
Usually, antimicrobial surgical prophylaxis should be limited to single dose therapy administered pre-operatively on induction of anaesthesia, unless otherwise stipulated in local surgical prophylaxis guidance.

name: Certifying an Adult Death
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life when a patient dies certifying an adult death certifying an adult death when a patient dies introduction
the section 'when a patient dies' has three strands was updated in march 2023. information on the medical examiner service and how they can help and support you. read this first here!
confirming an adult death - this strand contains practical advice and step-by-step guidance on the process of examining the body and confirming a death and is here.
certifying an adult death - see below. this strand contains information on completing the necessary paperwork, and the role of the medical examiner. 3. certifying an adult death certifying a death is the process of completing the medical certificate of cause of death (mccd) and the forms related to cremation, if required.
the medical examiner (me) team and the information below will guide you through this process. writing the death certificate following the death of a patient, a doctor from the team looking after the patient will be contacted by the medical examiner (me) team and asked for a proposed cause of death, and whether the team had any concerns regarding the patient’s medical care.
the me will then scrutinise the case and complete an independent review of the episode of care. the me officers (meos) will let you know the cause of death which the me has suggested, and the me may also contact you direct to discuss the case.
the meos will assist you to complete the mccd, and the cremation form if required if the case needs to be referred to hm coroner, the me will do this.
see below for more information on the role of the me.
more information on coroners & inquests is available on hub - search 'coroner'. cremation forms the medical examiner team will inform you if there is to be a cremation.
doctors’ responsibilities when completing cremation forms are governed by the cremation acts of 1902 and 1952, and the cremation regulations 1930 (as amended). the cremation form (medical certificate - cremation form 4) must be signed by a doctor who attended the deceased before death. it is usually completed by the same doctor as the medical certificate of cause of death (mccd). the national medical examiner system
in 2019, nhs improvement introduced the national medical examiner system across england & wales. the purpose of the new medical examiner system is to provide greater safeguards for the public by ensuring proper scrutiny of all non-coronial deaths, ensure the appropriate direction of deaths to the coroner,
provide a better service for the bereaved and an opportunity for them to raise any concerns they have to a doctor not involved in the care of the deceased,
improve the quality of death certification, and
improve the quality of mortality data. for more information from the nhs improvement web page click here. the role of the medical examiner medical examiners are senior medical doctors who are contracted for a number of sessions a week to undertake medical examiner duties, outside of their usual clinical duties. they are trained in the legal and clinical elements of death certification processes.
the role of the medical examiner at rduh, is to agree the proposed cause of death and the overall accuracy of the medical certificate cause of death (mccd),
discuss the cause of death with the next of kin/informant and establishing if they have any concerns with care that could have impacted/led to death,
act as a medical advice resource for the local coroner, and
inform the selection of cases for further review under local mortality arrangements and contributing to other clinical governance procedures. working with the medical examiner when the death of a patient occurs, the team looking after the patient will be contacted by the medical examiner team to organise a time to come to the office to write the medical certificate of cause of death (mccd) and cremation form if needed.
if you wish to speak to the medical examiner, the medical examiner offiecers (meos) will be able to help you. certifying an adult death when a patient dies
