name: The 14 Allied Health Professions
searchField:
the 14 allied health professions the 14 allied health professions ahp,allied health professions the 14 allied health professions
roles in bold are available at the royal devon. art therapists: use art as a medium to address emotional issues which may be confusing and distressing.
dietitians: translate the science of nutrition into everyday information about food and advise people on their food and nutrition choices
drama therapists: use role play, voice work, movement and storytelling to help people explore and solve personal and social problems.
music therapists: use the power of music to help people deal with feelings they cannot put into words.
occupational therapists: help people lead their best lives. they help people with mental and physical health needs and learning disabilities do things they want and need to in everyday lives.
operating department practitioners: are highly skilled members of the surgical team providing expert care and support during operations in theatres. flexible and adaptable their skills are also being valued in critical and emergency care and playing a team role in organ transplant services.
orthoptists: help improve the quality of people’s lives by treating eye disorders and spotting serious neurological conditions.
osteopaths: are experts in the musculoskeletal system. they detect, treat and prevent health problems by moving, stretching and massaging a person’s muscles and joints.
paramedics: are best known for their blue light ambulance responder skills in emergency pre-hospital settings. they also work in the community, urgent, primary, and secondary care, and play an important role in public health.
physiotherapists: help people affected by injury, illness or disability and frailty through movement, exercise, manual therapy and advice. they enable people to improve their physical health and activity and play an important role in public health.
podiatrists: treat and care for people whose feet and lower limbs have been affected by injury or illness.
prosthetists and orthotists: prosthetists work autonomously to assess, prescribe, design, supply and review prosthetic limbs which enable and enhance the lives of people of all ages. they work closely with the patient and other healthcare professionals, using advanced technology to replicate the structural and functional characteristics of the absent limb. orthotists work with people of all ages, to understand their individual needs in many different settings and often collaborate with other healthcare professionals. they autonomously prescribe, design and supply orthoses (splints or braces) which support or enhance the neuro-muscular and skeletal systems and protect existing body parts.
radiographers: diagnostic radiographers use advanced imaging equipment to look inside a person’s body to help diagnose and understand an illness or injury. they use communication and patient care skills to help achieve high quality images of human body. therapeutic radiographers use complex and advanced equipment to plan and deliver radiation treatment to treat cancer patients. they support patients throughout their radiotherapy treatment pathway.
speech and language therapists: provide life-changing treatment, support and care for children and adults who have difficulties with communications, eating, drinking and swallowing. for more information on ahps please click here. microguide page created 10/07/2024 microguide page last checked 05/08/2024 added to handbook north 03/03/2025

name: General Interest and Other Guidelines
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology penetrating globe injury general interest and other guidelines general interest and other guidelines 
Observational studies have shown some patterns to types of globe injury sustained in the population:

Younger children  - laceration injuries with sharp objects such as scissors, knives, plant material, stationery.

Teenage boys – BB gun injuries, or blunt trauma during sports, motor vehicle collisions or fights.

Young men – workplace injuries due to improper use of PPE when hammering/cutting metal or stone; blunt trauma sustained during sports, motor vehicle collisions or assault; illicit drug use or alcohol frequently have a role in trauma injuries. Gunshot wounds are less common but typically cause severe injuries with poor visual outcome.

Elderly patients – blunt force trauma due to falls or motor vehicle accident. The mechanism for open globe injury is usually trauma precipitating a breakdown of previous eye surgery site.

If abuse is suspected, in children contact the paediatricians who can refer onwards. For adults, see advice on the “Safeguarding Adults” pages on BOB, http://ndht.ndevon.swest.nhs.uk/safeguarding-adults/what-do-i-do-if-i-have-a-concern/

bacterial pneumonia should be considered : fever >38.5 degrees c
history of rigors / uncontrolled shivering inappropriate to the room /environment temperature
chest recession, difficulty in breathing or tachypnoea : infants > 50 breaths per minute
preschool > 40 breaths per minute
school aged > 30 breaths per minute if wheeze is present in a preschool child, bacterial pneumonia is unlikely

strep. pneumoniae is the commonest bacterial cause in childhood, followed by mycoplasma and chlamydia

name: Mild Facial Skin and Soft Tissue Infection
searchField:
mild facial skin and soft tissue infection ear, nose and throat (ent) facial cellulitis
mild face mild facial skin and soft tissue infection
mild facial skin & soft tissue infection can be treated like cellulitis at other upper body sites and does not usually require specialist input. in contrast, pre-septal, and especially post-septal (orbital) cellulitis are serious infections which require ent/ophthalmology input. post-septal cellulitis can be differentiated clinically from pre-septal infections be the presence of proptosis, painful ocular motility, impaired visual acuity +/- colour vision, or a relative afferent pupillary defect. if diagnosis uncertain, treat for post-septal cellulitis empirically and request prompt senior review. take mrsa screen, conjunctival swab if eye discharge ct head/orbits if clinical diagnosis of orbital cellulitis 1st line
1st line: flucloxacillin 1g qds po
duration: 7 days penicillin allergy penicillin allergy: clarithromycin 500mg bd po duration: 7 days

name: CAP Prescribing Guidance
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult antimicrobial treatment guidelines respiratory cap patients under 65 years cap prescribing guidance cap prescribing guidance community aquired pneumonia (cap) prescribing guidance notes community aquired pneumonia (cap) these guidelines are only applicable to adults with community acquired pneumonia (cap), and are not applicable to patients with a normal chest x-ray and patients with an exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (copd). see separate guidance for children. all patients with cap should be assessed using the curb-65 severity index. see guidelines below on making a curb-65 assessment. care should be taken to use clinical judgement when using the curb-65 score, especially in younger fit adults, as it is possible that the score may be low in patients who nevertheless have severe illness. hiv testing should be offered to all patients presenting with cap. bacterial pneumonia is a hiv clinical indicator condition.
all critically ill patients should be seen by a senior physician and icu staff if ventilatory support is likely to be required. antibiotic advice can be obtained from duty medical microbiologist ( medical microbiology contact details). in elderly patients care should be taken not to assume that there is pneumonia when there is no clear x-ray evidence of consolidation as gram negative sepsis of urinary tract or gut origin can present with some features that may be confused with chest infection, e.g. crackles in chest. it is important that these patients have gram negative cover, for example gentamicin. atypical infections are very unusual in the elderly, so that even in severe pneumonia (curb-65 3 or more) a second agent such as a macrolide may be unnecessary. this should be considered at the first consultant review round after admission. specific pathogens in community aquired pneumonia (cap) it is not possible to categorically identify a microbiological cause of pneumonia from clinical features. however additional antimicrobials may be considered in patients with severe pneumonia and microbiological advice sought. consider: mrsa in patients especially elderly, known to be currently or previously positive, and those with chronic illness or recent hospital attendance. legionnaires’ disease in middle aged, smokers, absence of co-morbidity, diarrhoea, neurological symptoms, more severe infection and evidence of multi-system involvement (e.g. abnormal liver function tests, elevated serum creatine kinase). in uk about 50% are travel associated. pvl positive staphylococcus aureus causes a rare necrotising pneumonia – severe illness, bloodstained sputum, multi-organ failure, and lung cavitation. post influenza streptococcus pneumoniae is the commonest cause of secondary bacterial pneumonia, but staphylococcal infection is relatively more likely, and may be mrsa in patients with risk factors. microbiological investigations in community aquired pneumonia (cap) all patients with moderate to severe pneumonia should have blood and sputum cultured and crp measured. in severe pneumonia (curb-65 ≥ 3) request urinary tested for pneumococcal antigen and in selected cases legionella antigen. serum should be stored in case serological investigation is required. the recommendations in this guideline are based on those made in the bts guideline, nice guideline, bhiva guideline and on local epidemiology plus expert opinion. severity assessment of community aquired pneumonia (cap)
these severity assessment guidelines relate to the inpatient management of communityacquired pneumonia (cap). they are not applicable to the management of infective exacerbations of chronic lung conditions such as copd, bronchiectasis and asthma. for a more detailed overview of the evidence please refer to the british thoracic guidelines on community acquired pneumonia (www.brit-thoracic.org.uk , guidelines section in nonmembers section). clinical assessment of severity of community aquired pneumonia (cap) all patients with cap should be assessed using the curb-65 severity index. this is a six point scale (0-5) where patients are given one point for the presence of each of the following proven markers of severity: confusion
blood urea > 7mmol/l respiratory rate ≥ 30/minute low blood pressure (systolic < 90 mmhg and/or diastolic ≤ 60 mmhg) age ≥ 65 years interpretation patients with a score of 3 or more should be treated as a severe cap. patients with a score of 2 require inpatient treatment and close observation. patients with a score of 0 or 1 can be considered for treatment as an outpatient. caution the curb-65 score is not a substitute for good clinical judgement and clinicians should also take account of other prognostic factors such as severe hypoxaemia, multilobar involvement on cxr, and raised early warning score prior to deciding on treatment pathway. curb-65 score can seriously underestimate severity of pneumonia in young previously fit adults.
additional points blood cultures, and where possible a sputum sample for culture, should be obtained prior to treatment. out of hours, samples should be transported to the laboratory in the normal way. for urgent specimens, the duty biomedical scientist should be contacted. all patients with severe cap should be transferred to culm ward depending on bed availability (if no bed is available on either culm, the patients should be reviewed by a respiratory consultant or spr). all antibiotic prescriptions should be reviewed within 24 hours by a consultant or spr. many patients can be changed to oral antibiotic regimens on post-take ward rounds – see guidelines for changing to oral antibiotics. antimicrobial considerations start antibiotic treatment as soon as possible after establishing a diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia, and certainly within 4 hours (within 1 hour if the person has suspected sepsis). doses are for guidance only and apply to adults of average size with normal renal and hepatic function. co-trimoxazole : if crcl ≤30mls/min reduce dose by half. discuss with microbiology if pregnant or breastfeeding. levofloxacin : levofloxacin is an alternative for all indications only in patients under 65, if there is renal impairment or non-resolution, and requires approval from micro as is associated with an increased risk of clostridium difficile diarrhoea, tendonitis, muscle pain, peripheral neuropathy, aortic aneurysm rupture / dissection, and prolonged qt interval. tendonitis risk increases with age and steroid use (may occur within 48 hours of starting). stop treatment at the first signs of serious adverse effects and discuss alternative treatment with microbiology. see mhra alert for further information. the absorption of doxycycline may be impaired by concurrent administration of antacids containing aluminium, calcium, magnesium (or other drugs containing these cations), oral zinc, iron salts, or bismuth – dosages should be maximally separated (ideally withhold oral iron supplements while on doxycycline). doxycycline should be taken whilst upright with ≥100mls water & well before bedtime. counsel patients on risks of photosensitivity. doxycycline is contra indicated in pregnancy & breastfeeding – contact microbiology for alternative options. cap prescribing guidance community aquired pneumonia (cap) prescribing guidance

name: Imaging/Scans
searchField:
imaging/scans investigations, medications and procedures imaging,scans,x-ray,mri,ct,ultrasound,bladder scan,radiography,radiology,imaging requests,radiological investigations,investigations,radiology,scan,clinical imaging,medical photography,clinical photography,medica,proc imaging/scans please write a proper clinical indication, radiographers will reject requests that just say “.” or “stroke”. in hours there is a local on-call radiologist; call switch to be put through via radiology secretaries or go to radiology department to discuss in person. out of hours (ooh) on-call radiology is via medica (1700 – 2100) or proc (2100 – 0800); call switch to be connected. irefer this can guide you as to the indications for imaging based on presentation. search irefer on the hub. there is a link to external page use “organisational sso”.
imaging modalities x-ray – request on epic. ed radiographers bleep: #042 (x-rays normally performed routinely without needing to bleep). ct - requests need to be vetted by on-call radiologist unless it is a ct head under nice guidelines. after discussing with radiology inform ct radiographer in hours: 4130, ooh: #294. note: if the ct head is for a stroke please request the specific 'ct stroke' order on epic. mri – needs discussion with on-call radiologist. usually cannot be performed ooh, patients may need transfer to exeter if mri urgently required. if cauda equina syndrome is a differential, discuss with exeter spinal (see external referrals). mri radiographers: 3276 / 3183 (in hours only).
ultrasound – some bedside ultrasounds are performed in ed by clinicians, departmental scans are only in-hours. if patient needs urgent uss, usually either needs admission or referral to specialty to arrange this e.g. in surgical emergency clinic.
bladder scan – you can do this yourself! bladder scanner in store room by hub. if unsure how, ask a nurse or another ed clinician to show you. please note scan volume on epic and whether it was pre- or post-void.
clinical images e.g. wounds / burns – upload direct to epic with a rover or a mobile device. burns images will also need to be uploaded to mdsas (see external referrals burns section for more details). note when you log on to a rover it will ask you about which printer you want, click default at the top and this will then bring you to the list of your patients to choose from. then click in the top left corner and you will be able to photograph and upload clinical media - after found on the media part of the ‘chart review’ tab on epic. guidance review committee/group guidance review date eolas page date created & editor number 22/07/2025, editor number 009 eolas page date last checked & editor number 27/07/25 008

name: Endogenous Endophthalmitis
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment ophthalmology endogenous endophthalmitis endogenous endophthalmitis endogenous endophthalmitis is severe inflammation of the anterior and/or posterior chambers of the eye, arising from bacteraemic or fungaemic seeding of the eye. it is considered an ocular emergency and should be referred to a consultant ophthalmologist for investigation urgently. treatment advice offered below is suitable for patients 1 month and over. see separate guideline for exogenous endophthalmitis, including prophylaxis after trauma.
see adult guidelines for diagnosis / red flags / follow-up advice diagnosis and differentials always remember to watch out for (red flags) if no better... general interest and other points organisms
treatment advice
all cases - child 1 month and over
combined intravenous and intra-ocular treatment are necessary for adequate treatment. intra-ocular injection to be administered by suitably qualified practitioners (arrange ward visit if patient cannot be transported to eye clinic). vancomycin 1mg/0.1ml intra-vitreal injection one dose following vitreous sampling, then observe for 24 hours and repeat if necessary or transfer out for vitrectomy plus ceftazidime 2mg/0.2ml intra-vitreal injection one dose following vitreous sampling, then observe for 24 hours and repeat if necessary or transfer out for vitrectomy plus vancomycin iv (target trough level 10-20mg/l)
child 1 month–11 years
10–15 mg/kg every 6 hours adjusted according to plasma-concentration monitoring, duration should be tailored to type and severity of infection and the individual clinical response—consult product literature for further information, doses higher than 60 mg/kg/day cannot be generally recommended as the safety of increased dosing has not been fully assessed.
child 12–17 years
15–20 mg/kg every 8–12 hours (max. per dose 2 g) adjusted according to plasma-concentration monitoring, duration should be tailored to type and severity of infection and the individual clinical response—consult product literature for further information, in seriously ill patients, a loading dose of 25–30 mg/kg (usual max. 2 g) can be used to facilitate rapid attainment of the target trough serum-vancomycin concentration. plus cefotaxime (from 1 month of age) 50mg qds iv, max. 12g total per day if suspected fungal infection fluconazole (from 1 month of age) 12 mg/kg daily (max. per dose 800 mg) od po/iv plus amphotericin b 5 micrograms/0.1ml intra-vitreal injection stat dose (normally only given in tertiary care centre at time of vitrectomy – special discussion with pharmacy required if needed in nddh) version control antibiotic guidelines for ophthalmology v2.0 160519 endogenous endophthalmitis endogenous endophthalmitis is severe inflammation of the anterior and/or posterior chambers of the eye, arising from bacteraemic or fungaemic seeding of the eye. it is considered an ocular emergency and should be referred to a consultant ophthalmologist for investigation urgently. treatment advice offered below is suitable for patients 1 month and over. see separate guideline for exogenous endophthalmitis, including prophylaxis after trauma.

name: Mental Capacity Act & Liberty Protection Safeguard
searchField:
hospital guidelines handbook my hospital life safeguarding mental capacity act & liberty protection safeguard mental capacity act & liberty protection safeguard mental capacity act & liberty protection safeguard
the mental capacity act (mca) provides a framework to empower and protect people who may lack capacity to make some decisions for themselves i.e. are unable to provide informed consent.
the mca makes it clear who can take decisions in which situations, and how they should go about this. anyone who works with or cares for an adult who lacks capacity must comply with the mca when making decisions or acting for that person.
this applies whether decisions are life changing events or more every day matters, and is relevant to adults of any age, regardless of when they lost capacity.
the underlying philosophy of the mca is to ensure that those who lack capacity are empowered to make as many decisions for themselves as possible and that any decision made, or action taken, on their behalf is made in their best interests.
the liberty protection safeguard (lps) has taken the place of the deprivation of liberty safeguard (dols). patients who are deprived of their liberty and lack capacity must have mental capacity assessment undertaken and a lps application should be made on their behalf.
all advice and support around mental capacity and liberty protection safeguards (previously dols) can be found on the hub - search mca, or by calling the mca/lps team on 01271 341521 or by email on rduh.dols@nhs.net
more information on the mca/lps team is on the hub, search mca team.
making an assessment of mental capacity
more information on the 'mental capacity act' is available on the hub - click on policies in the blue band at the top of the page, then search mca. the policy contains further details on the assessment process, roles and responsibilities, and contains helpful appendices to support assessments of capacity and best interest decisions. day to day assessments of capacity may be relatively informal and can be documented within the person’s case notes. more serious decisions have greater consequences/risks for the person who, it is thought, may lack capacity, and justify a more formal assessment of capacity using the mental capacity assessment and best interest decision form, available on epic. the following mca decision-making flowchart has been taken from the mental capacity act policy document is designed to assist all staff within the processes of the mental capacity act. it should be followed in conjunction with the policy and the mca code of practice here. microguide page created 2021 last updated 03/05/2023 microguide page last checked 29/08/2024 mental capacity act & liberty protection safeguard mental capacity act & liberty protection safeguard

name: Collecting samples for lab processing
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care collecting samples for lab processing collecting samples for lab processing collecting samples for lab processing  Wounds will become heavily colonised with microorganisms and these do not usually lead to infection. Only swab on clinical suspicion of cellulitis. Cellulitis should only be suspected if there are hot, red, painful margins to the wound. Increased slough or odour are not markers of infection, but rather suggest a change in approach to general wound management is required.

Points to consider
Ideally collect sample before starting antibiotics. 
If the patient is already on antibiotics, inform the lab by filling in the form appropriately. 
Provide sufficient sample: culturing wound material is complex and often requires several procedures. 
If pus is present, aspirate as much as possible with a sterile syringe and needle and place in a sterile container. 
Before collecting a sample thoroughly clean the wound with sterile saline or water. 
This is because microorganisms causing infection will be present in the wound bed rather than on surface debris, so cleaning the wound will allow better access to the tissue containing the microorganisms responsible for infection. 
Labelling samples

Correct patient details
If multiple samples taken from same wound, identify site sample was taken from (e.g. external surface, wound floor etc.)
Fill in the request form with as much clinical information as possible:

e.g. site of wound, cause of wound, any underlying pathology, if the patient is clinically unwell and in what way, current or intended antibiotic therapy, and any topical treatment being administered. 

Taking a wound swab
If the wound is moist: swab can be used directly from sterile packaging
If the wound is dry: swab tip should be moistened with sterile saline. 
Take a sample in a 'zig-zag' pattern along the entire surface wound be, whilst simultaneous rotating the swab. 
If a gentle pressure is applied during the procedure, this allows fluid to be expressed from the tissue.

name: Always Remember To
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology peri-orbital (preseptal) cellulitis always remember to always remember to 
Refer all suspected cases to Ophthalmology and where diagnostic doubt exists or not improving within 24 hours, refer to ENT for review, and to rule out the more serious orbital cellulitis

Send a blood culture before commencing antibiotics

If frank pus is able to be caught in a bottle, send for MC&S. Otherwise, swabs unlikely to be useful.

Discuss imaging with Radiology, particularly if unclear whether the infection is orbital or peri-orbital cellulitis.

Ensure admission MRSA swabs have been sent and check for previous history of MRSA on pathology system.

name: Diagnosis and Differentials
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology endophthalmitis (endogenous) diagnosis and differentials diagnosis and differentials 
Diagnosis
Endogenous infection involving spread from contiguous structures, or haematogenous dissemination. It is a rare complication of systemic disease.
Bacterial endophthalmitis
Bacterial endophthalmitis is usually an acute infection presenting with pain, a red eye and progressive blurring of vision.
Presentation may initially be atypical: patients may show symptoms of their bacteraemia or may only complain of eye pain and decreased vision. Progression is usually faster than for fungal endophthalmitis.
Ocular examination, and positive blood cultures, or vitreous/aqueous cultures are used to form the diagnosis. Some patients may have transient bacteraemias, and blood cultures are negative.
Any intravenous access poses a risk, particularly indwelling catheters and illicit drug use. Endoscopy is also a risk factor.
Infections such as endocarditis, intra-abdominal abscess, UTI (especially catheter-associated), meningitis. For patients from Eastern Asia, a common pathogen is Klebsiella pneumoniae associated with liver abscess.
Fungal endophthalmitis
Presentation may be more insidious, loss of vision can occur days to weeks after the fungaemia. Patients often suffer minimal pain until infection is advanced. It is usually clinically evident on eye examination.
Usually unilateral eye infection, although both eyes can be affected
Candida endophthalmitis may present as a fever of unknown origin, or as fungaemia without clear focus. Slit lamp examination is essential.
Some patients, particularly those with a history of illicit IV drug use or other IV access (e.g. in healthcare settings) only have transient fungaemia which seeds infection in the eye. They present with eye symptoms, but are otherwise systemically well, with normal white cell count and negative blood cultures. Differential Diagnosis
Non-infectious uveitis

name: Diagnosis and Things to Watch Out For
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment bone and joint septic arthritis (hot joint) prosthetic joint diagnosis and things to watch out for diagnosis and things to watch out for 
Prosthetic joint infection usually presents as a sub-acute problem
A high index of suspicion should be maintained for any patient complaining of recurrent pain in a prosthetic joint. Fever and leucocytosis may be absent.
Signs and symptoms may include:

Pain in the affected joint(s)
Discharge from wound site(s)
Loosening of the prosthesis
Raised inflammatory markers
‘Never right since the operation’

Management of prosthetic joint infection always involves debridement of infected tissue
A joint revision is often required
Management of these patients should be tailored to individual circumstances
description: Diagnosis and Things to Watch Out For

take appropriate samples, before commencing antimicrobials, if patient condition allows.
check mycare for recent culture results e.g. wound cultures, mrsa screens, history of esbl coliforms, before prescribing empirical antimicrobials (note, esbls confer resistance to cephalexin, cefuroxime and co-amoxiclav).

name: Severe Hyponatraemia Emergency Management
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies adult clinical guidance northern services fluid & electrolytes severe hyponatraemia emergency management severe hyponatraemia emergency management severe hyponatraemia emergency management severe hyponatraemia - emergency management emergency management of severe hyponatraemia: the role of hypertonic saline pathway approved 11/23 review date 11/2027 search hub "emergency management of severe hyponatraemia" iv - intravenous na+ - sodium vbg - venous blood gas itu - intensive care unit amu - acute medical unit nacl - sodium chloride / saline additional information indications management decisions should be made on the history of progression and symptoms of the hyponatraemia. symptoms of vomiting, seizures, reduced gcs (</=8) or cardiorespiratory arrest in the context of acute hyponatraemia (na<130) and no other contributory cause, should be considered severe hyponatraemia and escalation of care to hypertonic saline should be discussed. patients with severe symptoms require immediate treatment with hypertonic saline, irrespective of the cause of the hyponatraemia.
patients with acute hyponatraemia without correction, despite best care, in the presence of nausea, confusion or headache can also be considered for hypertonic saline.
hypertonic saline should ideally not be administered on the basis of one-off sodium, but on the trend of a few. the decision to treat with hypertonic saline should be made by the senior treating physician. out of hours, this should be the on-call medical consultant or itu consultant. the consultant endocrinologists do not do out-of-hours on calls, however are happy to be contacted for discussion if needed and available. administration administer 150ml of 2.7% hypertonic saline over 20 minutes to attain 5mmol/l increase in sodium in an hour as per schematic below. this should ideally be via a central line, if in place, or, a large peripheral vein with monitoring of the phlebitis score. the patient should ideally be located in a close monitoring environment e.g. amu or itu. if this is not feasible it is essential that the patient is in an area where neurological state and fluid balance can be monitored carefully. 2.7% nacl comes in 500ml polyfusor therefore it is essential that this is given by a volumetric pump programmed for 150ml over 20mins. it is kept in the emergency drug cupboard. monitoring after initial treatment, sodium levels should be monitored on vbg analysis (see below) and formal serum bloods at least 6 hourly in the first 24 hours after administration. if the sodium is rising more than 10 mmol/24 hours, 5 % at 125ml/hr dextrose should be initiated to prevent over treatment and osmotic demyelination. endocrinology review or discussion with an endocrinologist within 24 hours (this may be virtual if no endocrinologist available on site). pathway the majority of patients with hyponatraemia can be managed conservatively whilst timely investigations and treatments are initiated based on the clinical picture and underlying aetiology. guidelines for investigating hyponatraemia are outside the remits of this document. read additional information above in conjunction with this pathway iv - intravenous na+ - sodium vbg - venous blood gas itu - intensive care unit amu - acute medical unit nacl - sodium chloride / saline references ball, s., barth, j., miles, l., and the society for endocrinology clinical committee endocrine connections 5 g4-6 injectable medicines guide last checked 28/8/24 review date nov 2027 severe hyponatraemia emergency management severe hyponatraemia - name: General Interest and Other Points
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment gastrointestinal appendicitis & other intra-abdominal infection, incl. perforation general interest and other points general interest and other points general interest and other points    Diagnostic signs: 
  
Pregnancy — Right lower quadrant pain and tenderness dominate in the first trimester. In the latter part of pregnancy, right upper quadrant or right flank pain may occur.
A retrocaecal/retrocolic appendix may present with right loin pain and tenderness and a positive psoas test. Muscular rigidity and tenderness to deep palpation are often absent because of protection from the overlying caecum.
A pre-ileal and post-ileal appendix may present with vomiting and diarrhoea.
A subcaecal and pelvic appendix may present with suprapubic pain and urinary frequency; diarrhoea and tenesmus may be present due to rectal irritation; abdominal tenderness may be lacking, but rectal or vaginal tenderness may be present on the right side; microscopic haematuria and leukocytes may be present on urinalysis.
A long appendix with tip inflammation in the left lower quadrant may cause pain in that region.

name: Supply of Insulin
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies prescribing guidance northern services insulin prescribing sop supply of insulin supply of insulin supply of insulin northern services' getting insulin right' standard operating procedure' (review date may 2024) on hub search "getting insulin right"
please note - it is now out of date use with caution and check with the bnf supply of the correct insulin does the patient have their own insulin that can be used for administration?
each ward has a specific stock of insulin vials, approved by the diabetes specialist nurse team and pharmacy
insulin administered by the ward staff can be given from stock vials.
within pharmacy opening hours, insulin can be ordered on a named patient basis for self-injecting patients by the medicines management team and will be prioritized as a high risk medicine.
vials will only be issued for named patients for discharge and/or where infection control measures require this.
orders must state the form of insulin required; vial, cartridge or disposable pen device
outside of pharmacy opening hours, if the insulin required is not stocked by the ward, the required insulin can be obtained from another ward. the ‘pharmacy tracker’ app on zenworks can be used to locate insulin in the hospital. a pharmacy tracker user guide is available in the zenworks help & support tab here.
where the required insulin is not available conversion to an available alternative should be used - see alternative insulins & stock locations below.
insulin must never be omitted unless explicitly requested/documented by the diabetes nurse specialist, consultant or senior doctor
if a suitable conversion cannot be made or the patient is on a concentrated formulation/insulin pump contact the on-call pharmacist out of hours via bleep 500. alternative insulin & stock locations supply of insulin supply of insulin

name: When and how?
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial wound care assess the wound when and how? when and how? 
When asked to review a wound, ask the patient how long they have had this wound and what caused it. 
e.g. surgical wound, pressure ulcer etc. 
History
1. How long have they had the wound?
2. What caused the wound initially? 
3. Are they being seen by another professional i.e. district nurse, practice nurse or podiatry? 
4. What is their current treatment plan? e.g. are they using compression stockings at home? 
5. What was the reason for admission?
Factors Affecting Healing
Consider factors which may slow or prevent wound healing. 
Some wounds may never heal, and therefore the aim is to make them managable to live with (i.e. controlling exudate, odour or pain)
Factors to consider: 

    1. Smoking history  
  


    2. Diabetes 
  


    3. Age 
  


    4. Other comorbidities such as vascular disease (if leg ulcer or diabetic foot ulcer, can pulses be felt/ heard on doppler) 
  


    5. Baseline mobility prior to admission 
  


    6. Nutritional status i.e MUST score in risk assessment booklet (Do the dieticians need to be involved?)

name: Things to Watch Out For (red flags)
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial paediatric treatment urinary tract infection see ukpas guidance - section archived 1st aug 2025 [probable pyelonephritis] things to watch out for (red flags) things to watch out for (red flags) the following risk factors for uti and serious underlying pathology should be recorded, and renal imaging requested if present: poor urine flow
history suggesting previous uti or confirmed previous uti
recurrent fever of uncertain origin
antenatally diagnosed renal abnormality
family history of vesicoureteric reflux (vur) or renal disease
dysfunctional voiding
enlarged bladder
abdominal mass
evidence of spinal lesion
poor growth
high blood pressure.
non-e.coli uti constipation is an important factor to consider - manage bowels with aperients where present to encourage healthy toileting. abdominal imaging may be required to rule out intra-abdominal pathology if diagnosis unclear. see also appendicitis guideline.
children less than 3 months old with uti are at particularly high risk of complications - ensure prompt senior clinician review

potassium containing laxatives e.g. movicol, laxido
losalt
excess diet (usually only if concurrent renal impairment) other causes amphotericin
nicorandil
propofol
zoledronic acid there are rarer associations between medications and hyperkalaemia and it is important every prescribed medication is reviewed in the context of hyperkalaemia. appendix 2 non-pharmaceutical causes of hyperkalaemia renal acute kidney injury
chronic kidney disease (especially end stage renal disease (esrd) on dialysis) interstitial nephritis or tubular disease lack of aldosterone addison’s disease
renal tubular acidosis t4
rarely congenital adrenal hyperplasia
advanced ccf (congestive cardiac failure) redistribution acidosis e.g. diabetic ketoacidosis
cell tissue breakdown, e.g. trauma, crush injury, rhabdomyolysis, haemolysis, tumour lysis, transfusion pseudo-hyperkalaemia long transit time or poor storage
difficult venepuncture
wrong order of drawing blood tubes
haematological conditions e.g. high platelets

name: Read This Tab First
searchField:
hospital guidelines antimicrobial adult treatment ophthalmology endophthalmitis (endogenous) read this tab first read this tab first 
Endogenous Endophthalmitis is severe inflammation of the anterior and/or posterior chambers of the eye, arising from bacteraemic or fungaemic seeding of the eye. 
It is considered an ocular emergency and should be referred to a Consultant Ophthalmologist for investigation urgently.
 See separate guideline for exogenous endophthalmitis, including prophylaxis after trauma.

name: Administration of Insulin
searchField:
hospital guidelines clinical guidance and policies prescribing guidance northern services insulin prescribing sop administration of insulin administration of insulin administration of insulin northern services getting insulin right' standard operating procedure' (review date may 2024) on hub search "getting insulin right"
please note - it is now out of date use with caution and check with the bnf administration of insulin - initial considerations blood sugars must be checked before administering any diabetes medication and prescriptions must be clear and unambiguous – any concerns must be immediately escalated to the medical team
where appropriate, it is always preferable for the patient to self-administer insulin, please refer to the self-administration of medication standard operating procedure (under review) on hub
where patients cannot self-administer insulin type and dose should be checked with them if possible.
practitioners should follow the injectable medicines policy and associated sops referenced here.
if the practitioner is unfamiliar with the device it should not be used.
always seek support from the diabetes team if you are unsure about any aspect of diabetes care here. administration of insulin - procedure all insulin doses should be measured and administered with an insulin syringe or pen device only.
never extract insulin from an insulin cartridge or a prefilled pen device.
when staff are using a pen device a trust safety needle must be used, not the patients’ own needle.
if using a vial, draw up the required dose using an insulin needle.
if using a device, prime as necessary and dial up dose as per the device instructions.
the main injection sites are the stomach, sides of the thighs and buttocks.
ensure area is clean and dry before injection.
when delivering insulin using an insulin syringe the injection must be inserted into a lifted skin fold.
insert the needle at a 90 degree angle and depress the plunger/activate device.
count to ten slowly before removing needle.
dispose of needle in sharps bin as normal.
injection sites should be rotated following a recommended rotation scheme and recorded to reduce risk of cutaneous amyloidosis and other skin reactions (mhra safety update 2020). administration of insulin administration of insulin

name: Burns
searchField:
burns external referrals burns,app,paeds burns,peadiatric burns,adult burns burns please discuss all external referrals with a senior prior to referring. bristol burns unit at southmead hospital for adults (16 and over)
swuk paediatric burns centre at the royal hospital (15 and under)
the welsh burns centre, morriston hospital swansea for adult major trauma burns before referral: • date and time of injury • cause, effected areas, size and depth • review events and environment related to injury • other co-existing injuries • fasting status • tetanus status • potential safe-guarding issues if paediatric patient • first aid measures already taken referral process: • download the secure image data (sid) app from the app store or android store to take photos • open the webpage https://nww.mdsas.nhs.uk/burns/ and follow the on-screen instructions after clicking on new referral • use the app to scan the qr code which will come up after you have put in the referral details, then follow the instructions to upload and send photos • then phone the relevant burns service. • for adults (16 and over) this is the bristol burns unit at southmead hospital • for children (15 and under) this is the swuk paediatric burns centre at the royal hospital for children. • for adult major trauma burns – discuss with the welsh burns centre, morriston hospital swansea • ask to be put through to who is on call for burns. guidance review committee/group guidance review date eolas page date created & editor number 25/5/25, jb, 009 eolas page date last checked & editor number 25/5/25, jb, 009
